system_architecture.system_name: Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System
system_architecture.life_event: I am an American considering dental tourism at Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic in San José, Costa Rica (aestheticdentistrycr.com) for major restorative dental work — likely implants, crowns, or full-mouth restoration (All-on-X). I have no framework for evaluating an international dental clinic, planning medical travel logistics, coordinating pre-operative records transfer, managing the financial comparison between US and Costa Rica pricing, navigating post-operative recovery in a foreign country, or maintaining continuity of care with a stateside dentist after returning home. I need a complete operational control system that takes me from initial research and clinic evaluation through travel planning, treatment execution, onsite recovery, and long-term follow-up — with decision gates, checklists, cost worksheets, and timeline templates at every stage. The system should be specific to the Cavallini clinic's infrastructure (onsite hotels, onsite lab, onsite pharmacy, IV sedation availability, Nobel Biocare/Zimmer implant systems, lifetime warranty program) and Costa Rica as the destination country.
system_architecture.operating_premise: A US patient committing to implants, crowns, or All-on-X treatment at Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic must evaluate a foreign provider, transfer diagnostic records across borders, prepay or reserve high-cost care, travel internationally for an irreversible dental procedure, recover in Costa Rica, and secure US follow-up support; if any handoff fails before surgery or before return to the US, the patient can face unusable dental work, warranty gaps, unmanaged complications, or total treatment cost exceeding US care.
system_architecture.system_objective: Select, schedule, complete, and stabilize a Costa Rica restorative dental treatment plan at Cavallini clinic within 3 to 18 months from initial inquiry, with a documented treatment decision, verified price comparison versus US alternatives, complete pre-op records accepted before travel, no unmanaged post-op complication in the first 30 days after each treatment trip, and a confirmed US follow-up provider before final return home.

control_domains.name: Cavallini Clinic Due Diligence and Treatment Suitability Verification
control_domains.purpose: Determines whether Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic is an acceptable provider and whether the patient's case is clinically suitable for Costa Rica-based restorative treatment before any deposit, travel booking, or irreversible tooth preparation.
scope_in: Clinic credential verification for dentist, specialists, sedation capability, implant brands, onsite lab, onsite pharmacy, and onsite hotel infrastructure
Validation of whether the patient's case type fits cross-border care: crowns, implant placement, implant-supported bridges, All-on-X, or full-mouth restoration
Review of clinic communication responsiveness, treatment planning process, and willingness to review US records before travel
Assessment of lifetime warranty terms and exclusions for prosthetics and implants
Decision on whether Costa Rica treatment remains under consideration versus eliminated from options
scope_out: US dental insurance reimbursement submission after treatment
Post-return maintenance scheduling with a US dentist
primary_outputs: Case suitability determination for dental tourism
Warranty terms summary specific to proposed treatment
Provider comparison sheet versus at least one US alternative

control_domains.name: US Diagnostic Record Collection and Cross-Border Treatment Planning Transfer
control_domains.purpose: Ensures the clinic receives complete, usable pre-operative information early enough to issue a reliable treatment plan and avoid case changes after arrival in Costa Rica.
scope_in: Collection of panoramic X-rays, CBCT if available, intraoral photos, periodontal charting, treatment history, medication list, and medical clearance requirements
Secure transfer of records from US providers to Cavallini clinic
Translation or formatting of records if required by clinic intake process
Clarification of missing records before treatment date is confirmed
Confirmation that Cavallini has accepted records as sufficient for provisional treatment planning
scope_out: Independent radiology interpretation not requested by treating dentists

control_domains.name: US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control
control_domains.purpose: Creates a true all-in financial comparison so the patient knows whether Cavallini treatment remains financially justified after travel, recovery, contingency, and revision exposure are included.
scope_in: Collection of Cavallini treatment quote by procedure type and implant/prosthetic brand
Collection of at least one comparable US quote for the same restorative scope
Inclusion of airfare, local ground transport, hotel nights, companion travel if required, food, medications, missed work, revision reserve, and follow-up trip risk
Deposit schedule, payment timing, foreign transaction considerations, refund terms, and cancellation exposure
Decision on affordability threshold and financing requirements
scope_out: General household budgeting unrelated to the dental case
Tax treatment of medical expenses unless directly needed for payment planning
primary_outputs: Net savings or overrun decision sheet

control_domains.name: Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness
control_domains.purpose: Coordinates the patient's ability to arrive in San José medically, legally, and operationally ready for treatment at the clinic's required date and condition.
scope_in: Passport validity check and travel document readiness
Flight selection aligned to pre-op appointment, surgery date, sedation restrictions, and recovery duration
Use of Cavallini-related onsite hotel lodging or equivalent near-clinic lodging
Ground transport planning between airport, hotel, clinic, and pharmacy needs
Companion attendance planning if IV sedation or immediate post-op assistance is required
Packing plan for medications, records, payment instruments, and post-op recovery supplies
scope_out: Clinical decision on implant number or prosthetic design
Long-term maintenance scheduling after return home
primary_outputs: Confirmed itinerary linked to treatment calendar
Trip contact sheet for clinic, hotel, airline, and emergency support

control_domains.name: Cavallini Procedure Execution and In-Country Recovery Stabilization
control_domains.purpose: Controls what happens from arrival through immediate recovery so that treatment scope, consent, medications, and complication response are documented before the patient leaves Costa Rica.
scope_in: Arrival-day and pre-op consultation confirmation
Final review of treatment plan, informed consent, and any scope changes after in-person diagnostics
Use of onsite lab coordination for restorations where applicable
Use of onsite pharmacy for prescribed post-op medications
Monitoring of sedation-day discharge instructions and early recovery checks
Verification that the patient is clinically cleared for hotel recovery, local movement, and eventual air travel
scope_out: Long-term prosthetic maintenance beyond the Costa Rica stay
Independent malpractice claims or legal disputes
primary_outputs: Travel-home clinical clearance note or equivalent discharge confirmation

control_domains.name: US Reentry Continuity of Care and Cavallini Warranty Preservation
control_domains.purpose: Prevents the common dental tourism failure in which the patient returns home without a maintenance dentist, complication pathway, or documentation needed to preserve warranty rights.
scope_in: Identification and confirmation of a US dentist willing to provide hygiene, monitoring, minor adjustment, or emergency evaluation after Costa Rica treatment
Transfer of final records from Cavallini to the US follow-up provider
Scheduling of post-return exams and imaging at required intervals
Documentation of any complication, breakage, implant issue, bite issue, or prosthetic defect within warranty reporting windows
Assessment of whether a return trip to Costa Rica is required under warranty terms
scope_out: Selection of a different foreign clinic for revision treatment
Routine non-related dentistry unrelated to the restorative case
General travel planning for future vacations

key_roles.responsibility: Owns all go/no-go decisions, signs consents, approves budget, authorizes record transfers, confirms travel bookings, and accepts or rejects final treatment scope changes.

key_roles.role: Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini Clinic Treatment Coordinator
key_roles.responsibility: Provides clinic intake requirements, quote structure, scheduling windows, lodging coordination information, and record receipt confirmation; coordinates patient communication before and during the Costa Rica stay.

key_roles.role: Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini Treating Dentist or Assigned Clinical Lead
key_roles.responsibility: Determines clinical suitability, confirms final treatment plan, recommends implant or prosthetic approach, and authorizes whether treatment proceeds after in-person evaluation.
Supplies historical records, diagnostic imaging, periodontal status, and existing restorative information needed for cross-border treatment planning.
Accepts continuity-of-care responsibility after the patient returns home, performs monitoring or emergency assessment, and communicates findings relevant to Cavallini warranty or revision needs.
Provides same-day supervision for IV sedation recovery, assists with transport, medication pickup, food, and escalation if the patient cannot self-manage during immediate recovery.

success_criteria: Cavallini clinic due diligence is documented before any deposit is paid, including confirmation of treatment capability for the patient's procedure type and written capture of warranty terms and exclusions.

100% of required pre-op records requested by Cavallini are transmitted and acknowledged as received no later than 21 days before the first treatment-related departure date, unless the clinic specifies a different deadline in writing.

A written US-versus-Costa Rica cost worksheet is completed before booking travel and includes procedure quote, airfare, lodging, local transport, medication, missed work, and a complication reserve rather than procedure price alone.

A formal go/no-go decision is recorded before any irreversible treatment step, with the expected Costa Rica total cost not exceeding the patient's approved budget ceiling by more than 10% without a new approval decision.
If IV sedation is used, a same-day escort plan is confirmed before the procedure date and discharge is completed without the patient self-transporting after sedation.
The patient departs Costa Rica only after receiving final records for that treatment stage, medication instructions, and a documented escalation path for post-op concerns.
A US follow-up dentist is identified and contacted before final return home, or the case is flagged as high-risk and not closed operationally.
No unmanaged post-operative complication requiring emergency hospitalization occurs within 30 days of each Costa Rica treatment trip.

At least one post-return follow-up interaction with a US dentist or Cavallini clinic occurs within 14 days of return if the procedure involved surgery, implant placement, or major full-mouth restoration.
All warranty-relevant issues are documented and reported within the timeline required by Cavallini's written warranty terms, preserving eligibility for review or corrective action.
operating_constraints: Treatment planning accuracy is limited by the completeness and recency of US diagnostic records before in-person evaluation in Costa Rica.
Major restorative work such as implants or All-on-X may require staged treatment over multiple Costa Rica trips rather than a single visit.
Irreversible dental decisions may occur under travel pressure because the patient is already in-country and financially committed to flights, lodging, and time off work.
Costa Rica treatment requires valid US traveler identification documents and sufficient travel flexibility to remain in-country if recovery or treatment timing changes.
If IV sedation is used, same-day self-management capacity is reduced and a discharge support plan may be operationally necessary.
The patient's final all-in cost is affected by exchange rates, card processing, wire timing, refund policies, and unplanned changes in treatment scope.
US dental insurance may provide limited or no reimbursement for out-of-country treatment, so the system must operate assuming high out-of-pocket exposure unless verified otherwise.
Continuity of care after return is constrained by whether a US dentist is willing to monitor or service work initiated by a foreign clinic.
Warranty preservation depends on compliance with Cavallini's written terms, documentation retention, maintenance behavior, and timely reporting of defects or complications.
Clinical compatibility for future maintenance is constrained by the specific restorative components and implant systems used, including Nobel Biocare or Zimmer where applicable.

Use of the clinic's onsite hotel, lab, pharmacy, and integrated infrastructure may improve convenience but does not eliminate the need for independent verification of records, costs, consent terms, and discharge readiness.

The system must account for Costa Rica as the treatment destination, including international flight timing, local transport in San José, and recovery in a foreign country away from the patient's normal medical support network.
system_architecture.time_horizon: 3–18 months from initial clinic evaluation to 12-month post-final prosthetic follow-up, depending on whether treatment is single-trip crowns/veneers or staged implant/All-on-X restoration

critical_milestones.description: Patient has verified clinic identity, treatment capabilities relevant to the case, implant systems under consideration, sedation availability, onsite hotel/lab/pharmacy infrastructure, and has opened a documented due diligence file.

critical_milestones.milestone: Diagnostic Record Packet Accepted by Clinic
critical_milestones.description: Cavallini clinic confirms receipt and usability of required US records sufficient to issue a preliminary treatment recommendation or identifies exact missing items preventing planning.

critical_milestones.milestone: All-In Costa Rica Financial Case Approved
critical_milestones.description: Patient completes a written Costa Rica-versus-US cost comparison including travel and contingency reserve and records a go/no-go decision based on total expected spend rather than procedure quote alone.

critical_milestones.milestone: Clinical Go/No-Go and Treatment Scope Decision
critical_milestones.description: Patient receives a documented preliminary treatment plan specifying expected procedure class such as crowns, implants, or All-on-X and formally decides whether to proceed with Cavallini.
Passport validity, flights, lodging, airport transfer plan, payment method, medication packing, and companion support for IV sedation if required are all confirmed before departure.

critical_milestones.milestone: In-Person Costa Rica Treatment Plan Reconfirmed
critical_milestones.description: After onsite examination and any updated imaging, the final scope, consent, timing, and price impact of any case change are documented before irreversible treatment begins.

Patient has filled medications, received written post-op instructions, completed early recovery monitoring, and is clinically stable in hotel recovery without unaddressed bleeding, pain escalation, or sedation-related concern.

critical_milestones.milestone: US Return with Continuity-of-Care Handoff Active
critical_milestones.description: Before or immediately upon return, the patient has a named US dentist, transmitted treatment records, and a scheduled follow-up visit if required by the restorative plan.

At 12 months after final prosthetic delivery or equivalent completion point, the patient has documented implant/prosthetic status, any complications reported, and warranty compliance records preserved.

failure_modes: The patient compares only Cavallini procedure pricing to a US quote and omits flights, hotel recovery days, medication, missed work, and revision reserve; the apparent savings disappears after commitment, creating mid-treatment funding shortfall.

US records are incomplete or too old, but travel is booked anyway; after arrival, the clinic changes scope or delays treatment because anatomy, infection, bone condition, or periodontal status differs from assumptions.

The patient authorizes implant placement, tooth preparation, or All-on-X extractions before final in-person confirmation of scope and cost; once teeth are altered or extracted, reversal is no longer possible and negotiating leverage is gone.

The patient undergoes IV sedation without a reliable escort or recovery support plan; discharge, medication compliance, and immediate complication monitoring fail during the highest-risk post-op period.

A post-operative issue such as bite instability, prosthetic fracture, swelling escalation, suspected infection, or implant concern emerges after return to the US, but no stateside dentist is willing to evaluate foreign work, delaying intervention beyond the easiest correction window.

The patient leaves Costa Rica without operative notes, implant system details, prosthetic specifications, or imaging; later US providers cannot service the case efficiently and warranty claims lack required documentation.

The patient assumes a 'lifetime warranty' guarantees no-cost correction under all conditions, but exclusions for maintenance failures, non-compliance, outside modifications, or missed reporting windows void practical coverage after the point of failure.

Flights are scheduled too tightly after surgery, causing the patient to travel while unstable, miss a required Cavallini review, or lose the chance to correct an immediate fit/function problem before international departure.

The patient relies on a single preliminary quote without documenting what triggers price changes for grafting, extractions, temporaries, sedation, or prosthetic upgrades; final invoice exceeds approved funds after arrival.

A US maintenance plan is never activated, leading to missed hygiene, occlusal adjustments, or early implant/prosthetic monitoring, with failures surfacing months later when corrective action is more invasive and expensive.

document_outline.document_title: The Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Operations Manual for U.S. Patients
document_outline.document_subtitle: A working reference system for American patients evaluating, purchasing, traveling for, completing, and stabilizing major restorative dental treatment at Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic in San José, Costa Rica
document_outline.system_name: Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System
master_operating_rules: Do not pay a treatment deposit or book international travel until Cavallini has reviewed your diagnostic file and issued a written preliminary treatment pathway tied to your actual records — Rationale: implant, crown, and All-on-X cases can change materially when records are incomplete, making early financial commitments unreliable. Trigger: before any deposit request, surgery date hold, or airfare purchase. Risk: nonrefundable travel loss, deposit exposure, and a same-week treatment scope change that can add thousands of dollars and extend the stay.

Treat the accepted pre-op records packet as a controlled deliverable, not an informal email exchange — Rationale: cross-border treatment planning fails when panoramic images, CBCT files, medication lists, or periodontal data are missing or unusable on arrival. Trigger: once Cavallini requests records or you begin collecting U.S. diagnostics. Risk: delayed surgery, repeated imaging, extra hotel nights, and a treatment-plan revision after arrival in Costa Rica.

Approve Costa Rica treatment only after comparing Cavallini's quote against at least one U.S. like-for-like restorative quote that includes travel, recovery, revision reserve, and missed-work costs — Rationale: headline dental tourism pricing often excludes the very expenses that erase savings in implant and full-mouth cases. Trigger: when a preliminary Cavallini quote is received and before any financing or payment commitment. Risk: total case cost exceeding U.S. treatment despite traveling internationally, with no budget reserved for complications or follow-up trips.

Do not schedule IV sedation, implant surgery, or full-arch preparation travel without a documented escort and first-48-hours recovery plan in San José — Rationale: sedation, swelling, bleeding, and medication effects can impair safe self-management immediately after major restorative procedures. Trigger: when the treatment plan includes IV sedation, extractions with implants, or extensive same-day prosthetic work. Risk: unmanaged post-op symptoms, missed medication intervals, emergency transport costs, or inability to comply with discharge instructions.

Before leaving Costa Rica after any major restorative phase, obtain and duplicate the complete discharge packet including implant system details, prosthetic information, medications, images if provided, and written complication instructions — Rationale: U.S. follow-up providers cannot safely intervene without brand, component, and procedure documentation from the treating clinic. Trigger: at final checkout for each treatment trip and before airport departure. Risk: warranty ambiguity, refusal of stateside follow-up care, delayed complication treatment, and avoidable remake costs.

Do not treat the Cavallini lifetime warranty as a substitute for maintenance and follow-up logistics — Rationale: warranty programs usually depend on compliance conditions, and remedial care may still require international travel, maintenance records, or proof of proper home-country follow-up. Trigger: when reviewing warranty terms and before accepting treatment as financially protected. Risk: denied warranty coverage, an uncovered return trip to Costa Rica, and full out-of-pocket replacement of prosthetic work.

Secure a U.S. dentist willing to perform post-return surveillance or emergency triage before the final return flight from Costa Rica — Rationale: complications such as bite problems, loose provisionals, tissue irritation, or early implant concerns often surface after the patient is back home. Trigger: before completing the final treatment trip or immediately after provisional/final prosthetic delivery is scheduled. Risk: unmanaged complications in the first 30 days, emergency out-of-network costs, and loss of treatment stability before Cavallini can reassess.

document_outline.introduction_text: This manual is for U.S. patients considering or undertaking major restorative dental treatment at Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic in San José, Costa Rica. It converts clinic evaluation, diagnostic transfer, cost comparison, travel logistics, treatment execution, recovery, and U.S. follow-up into a sequenced control system with decision gates and documented handoffs. Used correctly, it gives the patient an operational framework to move from initial inquiry to stable post-return care without relying on memory or informal coordination.

chapters.domain_name: Cavallini Clinic Due Diligence and Treatment Suitability Verification
chapters.chapter_title: Cavallini Credential Verification, Cross-Border Case Fit Screening, and Lifetime Warranty Boundary Review
chapters.chapter_purpose: This chapter determines whether Cavallini is an acceptable provider for the patient's specific restorative case before any deposit, travel booking, or irreversible dental work is authorized.
key_topics: Verification of Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini clinic credentials, specialist access, IV sedation capability, onsite lab, onsite pharmacy, and onsite hotel infrastructure
Confirmation that the proposed case type is appropriate for cross-border treatment, including crowns, implants, implant-supported bridges, and All-on-X
Assessment of clinic communication responsiveness and willingness to review U.S. records before travel
Documentation of Nobel Biocare and Zimmer implant system use where relevant to the proposed treatment
Extraction of lifetime warranty terms, exclusions, travel obligations, and maintenance conditions tied to prosthetics and implants
chapters.common_gap: Patients rely on website claims and price appeal without converting Cavallini's infrastructure, implant systems, sedation availability, and warranty exclusions into a documented go/no-go file, so they cannot detect missing capability or hidden follow-up obligations before committing.
cascade_triggers: When the Cavallini go/no-go decision record is marked GO, it triggers pre-op record collection and transfer in US Diagnostic Record Collection and Cross-Border Treatment Planning Transfer
When the provider comparison sheet and warranty terms summary are complete, it triggers all-in quote analysis in US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini Clinic Infrastructure and Restorative Capability Inventory
worksheet_plan.purpose: Captures a structured inventory of Cavallini's verified capabilities relevant to implants, crowns, All-on-X, sedation, onsite lab support, pharmacy access, and lodging integration.

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini Website Claim Verification and Missing-Evidence Checklist
worksheet_plan.purpose: Verifies which clinic claims have documentary support, which require direct confirmation, and which remain unresolved before the clinic stays under consideration.

worksheet_plan.title: Dental Tourism Case-Fit Screening Form for Crowns, Implants, and All-on-X at Cavallini
worksheet_plan.purpose: Determines whether the patient's specific restorative case is operationally suitable for treatment in Costa Rica rather than locally in the United States.

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini Lifetime Warranty Exclusions and Return-Trip Exposure Summary
worksheet_plan.purpose: Summarizes warranty coverage boundaries, maintenance requirements, exclusions, and the practical cost of needing to return to Costa Rica for remedial work.

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini-versus-U.S. Restorative Provider Go/No-Go Decision Record
worksheet_plan.purpose: Produces a documented decision on whether Cavallini remains an approved option compared with at least one U.S. provider.

chapters.domain_name: US Diagnostic Record Collection and Cross-Border Treatment Planning Transfer
chapters.chapter_title: Pre-Operative Dental Record Assembly, Secure Transfer to Cavallini, and Diagnostic Sufficiency Confirmation
chapters.chapter_purpose: This chapter ensures Cavallini receives a complete and usable diagnostic package early enough to issue a reliable treatment plan before travel is locked in.
key_topics: Collection of panoramic X-rays, CBCT data if available, intraoral photographs, periodontal charting, treatment history, and medication list from U.S. sources
Medical clearance requirement identification for sedation or major restorative procedures
Formatting and secure transmission of diagnostic records to the clinic
Tracking clinic acknowledgement of receipt and follow-up on missing or unreadable files
Written confirmation that the records are sufficient for preliminary treatment planning
chapters.common_gap: Patients send partial diagnostics from multiple U.S. offices without a master transmission log, so Cavallini receives incomplete or unusable records and the treatment plan remains provisional until arrival.
cascade_triggers: When Cavallini acknowledges receipt of the complete pre-op records packet, it triggers preliminary quote reconciliation in US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control
When diagnostic sufficiency is confirmed and a planning pathway is issued, it triggers date-based flight and lodging coordination in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness

worksheet_plan.title: U.S. Diagnostic Record Packet Inventory for Cavallini Treatment Planning
worksheet_plan.purpose: Lists every required pre-op item, its source, date, format, and collection status so the records packet can be assembled as a single controlled deliverable.

worksheet_plan.title: Panoramic, CBCT, Periodontal, and Photo File Quality Verification Checklist for Cross-Border Use
worksheet_plan.purpose: Checks whether each diagnostic file is current, readable, properly labeled, and usable for Cavallini's treatment-planning process.

worksheet_plan.title: U.S. Provider Records Request and Release Routing Form for Cavallini Submission
worksheet_plan.purpose: Coordinates requests to dentists, specialists, and imaging centers so records are released to the patient or clinic without missing authorizations.

worksheet_plan.title: Cross-Border Diagnostic Transmission Log and Clinic Receipt Register
worksheet_plan.purpose: Tracks each upload or email transfer, confirmation received, missing-file notice, and final acknowledgement from Cavallini.

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini Diagnostic Sufficiency Confirmation and Missing-Item Resolution Form
worksheet_plan.purpose: Documents whether the clinic considers the file adequate for treatment planning and what remaining items must be supplied before scheduling.

chapters.domain_name: US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control
chapters.chapter_title: All-In Cavallini Price Modeling, U.S. Comparison, Deposit Timing, and Complication Reserve Control
chapters.chapter_purpose: This chapter produces a true financial decision on whether Cavallini treatment remains justified after all travel, recovery, payment, and revision costs are included.
key_topics: Collection of Cavallini quote components by procedure type, implant brand, and prosthetic scope
Collection of at least one comparable U.S. quote for the same restorative scope
Inclusion of airfare, lodging, local transport, food, medications, companion travel, missed work, and follow-up trip risk
Review of deposit schedule, payment timing, refund terms, cancellation exposure, and foreign transaction handling
Calculation of contingency reserve for complications, revisions, or return travel
chapters.common_gap: Patients compare only chairside procedure prices and ignore second-trip exposure, missed work, companion travel, and revision reserve, causing the apparent Costa Rica savings to disappear after commitment.
cascade_triggers: When the net savings or justified-overrun decision sheet is approved, it triggers booking execution in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness
When the deposit and payment calendar is finalized, it triggers payment-instrument preparation and travel cash-flow planning in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini Restorative Quote Line-Item Capture for Implants, Crowns, and All-on-X
worksheet_plan.purpose: Records the quoted Costa Rica treatment scope in a consistent format for later comparison against U.S. pricing and ancillary costs.

worksheet_plan.title: Costa Rica Treatment Budget Inclusion Checklist for Travel, Recovery, and Return-Trip Risk
worksheet_plan.purpose: Verifies that non-clinical expenses and contingency items are included before any savings claim is accepted as real.

worksheet_plan.purpose: Produces a side-by-side comparison of U.S. and Costa Rica costs for the same treatment scope using standardized categories.

worksheet_plan.title: Deposit, Final Payment, and Foreign Transaction Timing Planner for Cavallini Care
worksheet_plan.purpose: Schedules payment deadlines, card or wire method selection, refund dependencies, and currency-fee exposure before funds are committed.

worksheet_plan.title: Complication Reserve and Savings Threshold Approval Form for Dental Tourism Commitment
worksheet_plan.purpose: Documents the minimum acceptable savings or strategic justification and the reserved amount set aside for complications or revision travel.

chapters.domain_name: Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness
chapters.chapter_title: Passport-to-Chair Travel Execution, Sedation-Compatible Scheduling, and San José Recovery Logistics Setup
chapters.chapter_purpose: This chapter aligns travel documents, flights, lodging, local transport, escort support, and packing so the patient arrives in San José ready for treatment on the clinic's schedule.
key_topics: Passport validity and travel document readiness for entry to Costa Rica and timely return to the United States
Flight selection aligned with pre-op appointments, surgery timing, IV sedation restrictions, and planned recovery days
Use of Cavallini-related onsite hotel lodging or equivalent near-clinic recovery lodging
Ground transport planning among airport, hotel, clinic, and pharmacy access points
Companion requirement planning and packing of medications, records, payment instruments, and post-op recovery supplies
chapters.common_gap: Patients book low-cost flights before locking the clinical sequence, then discover misaligned arrival times, sedation-day escort needs, or insufficient post-op lodging days that force costly rebooking or unsafe compression of recovery.
cascade_triggers: When the confirmed itinerary and lodging plan are complete, it triggers appointment-sequenced treatment execution in Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management

When companion coverage and recovery supplies are confirmed for a sedation or surgery case, it triggers discharge-day monitoring preparation in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance

worksheet_plan.title: Costa Rica Surgical Trip Logistics Inventory for Flights, Lodging, Transfers, and Escort Coverage
worksheet_plan.purpose: Captures every trip component required to move from U.S. departure to clinic attendance and immediate recovery without missing a dependency.

worksheet_plan.title: Passport Validity, Entry Document, and Sedation-Day Escort Compliance Checklist for Costa Rica Dental Travel
worksheet_plan.purpose: Verifies that legal travel readiness and escort requirements are complete before tickets are treated as final.

worksheet_plan.title: Cavallini Appointment-to-Flight Timing Planner
worksheet_plan.purpose: Maps pre-op, surgery, lab, pharmacy, and checkout timing against arrival and departure windows to prevent schedule compression.

worksheet_plan.title: San José Post-Procedure Lodging and Ground Transport Routing Form
worksheet_plan.purpose: Builds a local operations plan covering airport pickup, clinic transfers, hotel return, pharmacy access, and emergency transport fallback.

worksheet_plan.title: Major Restorative Dental Travel Packing and Payment Instrument Preparation Sheet
worksheet_plan.purpose: Prepares the exact items needed for treatment, recovery, documentation, and payment execution during the Costa Rica trip.

chapters.domain_name: Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management
chapters.chapter_title: Procedure-Day Identity Verification, Sedation Control, Implant/Prosthetic Specification Capture, and Discharge Handoff
chapters.chapter_purpose: This chapter controls what happens at the clinic so the planned procedure, materials, medications, and discharge information are accurately executed and documented before the patient leaves the chair and the facility.
key_topics: Pre-op confirmation of planned procedure, teeth involved, implant number, restorative scope, and sedation pathway
Day-of-treatment identity, consent, medication, and escort coordination for IV sedation or extensive restorative work
Capture of implant system, prosthetic components, lab involvement, and any same-day plan changes
Collection of prescriptions, medication instructions, and clinic contact routes for urgent post-op issues
Discharge packet assembly including written instructions and records needed for U.S. follow-up
chapters.common_gap: Patients leave the clinic with only verbal explanations and no structured record of what was actually placed, adjusted, prescribed, or changed from the original plan, making later complication management and warranty enforcement far harder.
cascade_triggers: When the discharge handoff is complete and medication instructions are documented, it triggers symptom and medication monitoring in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance

When implant system and prosthetic specification records are captured, it triggers U.S. provider handoff preparation in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control

chapters.chapter_purpose: This chapter manages the patient's in-country recovery window so complications are detected, escalated, and stabilized before return travel is attempted.
key_topics: Daily monitoring of pain, swelling, bleeding, temperature, oral function, and medication adherence after Cavallini treatment
Use of written clinic instructions and urgent contact routes for emerging complications
Decision points for seeking clinic reassessment versus emergency intervention in Costa Rica
Evaluation of hydration, eating tolerance, mobility, and symptom stability before commercial air travel
Assembly of final return packet before departure from San José
chapters.common_gap: Patients assume discomfort is routine after implant or full-mouth treatment and fail to track symptom progression against discharge instructions, so early infection, uncontrolled bleeding, or medication problems are not escalated until travel day.
cascade_triggers: When the patient meets return-to-travel criteria and the return packet is complete, it triggers stateside handoff in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control
When a complication exceeds the clinic's post-op threshold during the Costa Rica stay, it triggers immediate reassessment or emergency escalation before any flight segment proceeds

worksheet_plan.purpose: Identifies which symptoms require self-monitoring, clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency care while still in Costa Rica.

worksheet_plan.title: Clinic Contact, Local Emergency Option, and Escalation Routing Form for Post-Op Issues
worksheet_plan.purpose: Builds an actionable escalation path covering Cavallini contacts, local support, and emergency fallback during the recovery stay.

worksheet_plan.title: Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment
worksheet_plan.purpose: Determines whether the patient's symptoms, medication status, and recovery stage support safe commercial travel back to the United States.

Tracking of Cavallini warranty conditions, exclusions, and documentation needed to support a claim
Creation of a long-term maintenance schedule for cleanings, surveillance imaging, and restorative review
chapters.common_gap: Patients return to the United States without a committed follow-up dentist or complete implant and prosthetic records, so minor stability or bite issues become expensive emergency problems and warranty support is harder to invoke.
cascade_triggers: When the U.S. follow-up provider receives the full treatment packet, it triggers the first post-return review and complication surveillance cycle

chapter_opener.what_this_is_for: This chapter determines whether Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic is acceptable for your specific restorative case and cross-border treatment model before any money, travel, or irreversible dentistry is authorized.
chapter_opener.when_it_matters: Use this chapter at initial clinic evaluation and complete it before paying a deposit, reserving a surgery date, booking airfare, or allowing any tooth preparation tied to Costa Rica treatment.
chapter_opener.failure_looks_like: You rely on website claims or broad verbal assurances, commit funds or travel, and later discover missing records review, unclear sedation logistics, limited warranty access, or a case complexity that does not fit safe cross-border execution.
produces: Cavallini go/HOLD/no-go decision record
Clinic due diligence file with verified infrastructure and capability evidence
Cross-border case-fit determination for your likely restorative scope
Written lifetime warranty boundary summary with return-trip exposure notes
Provider comparison sheet versus at least one U.S. restorative option
do_first: Open ws-01-a and inventory every visible Cavallini claim about implants, crowns, All-on-X, IV sedation, onsite lab, onsite pharmacy, onsite hotel integration, implant brands, and warranty.
Open ws-01-b and mark each claim as documentary verified, verbally stated but not documented, or unresolved before asking any new questions.

minimum_viable_actions: Build the Cavallini Clinic Infrastructure and Restorative Capability Inventory in ws-01-a using the website and any direct clinic materials.
Verify whether Cavallini will review U.S. diagnostic records before travel and specify the minimum records required for preliminary treatment planning.
Classify your likely case in ws-01-c as localized crowns, limited implant restoration, multi-unit implant/prosthetic case, or full-arch/All-on-X reconstruction.
Extract the lifetime warranty terms in ws-01-d and identify exclusions, maintenance conditions, and whether corrective work requires return travel to Costa Rica.
Track communication reliability by logging response time, answer completeness, and missed callbacks or promised documents.
Complete ws-01-e only after critical capabilities are verified and unresolved items are either closed or explicitly marked as blocking.
quick_reference_rules: Do not pay a deposit or hold a surgery date until Cavallini confirms a record-review pathway tied to your actual diagnostics — Rationale: record-free estimates can change materially on arrival. Trigger: any request for deposit, scheduling fee, or treatment reservation before records review. Risk: nonrefundable travel loss, treatment-scope expansion, and extra hotel nights in San José.

Treat website claims as unverified until they are linked to documentary evidence or direct written confirmation — Rationale: marketing language is not an operational control. Trigger: when you see claims about Nobel Biocare, Zimmer, IV sedation, onsite lab, onsite pharmacy, onsite hotel, or warranty. Risk: selecting a clinic on capabilities that are missing, limited, or inapplicable to your case type.

If IV sedation is relevant, confirm who provides it, what medical clearance is required, and whether an escort is mandatory before marking the sedation pathway acceptable — Rationale: sedation changes travel, discharge, and first-night recovery control requirements. Trigger: planned extractions, immediate implants, full-arch work, or any procedure expected to use IV sedation. Risk: unsafe discharge, missed medication intervals, unmanaged swelling or bleeding, and inability to comply with post-op instructions.

Verify implant system use at the case level, not at the marketing level — Rationale: U.S. follow-up dentists may need exact brand and component information for triage. Trigger: any implant, implant-supported bridge, or All-on-X discussion. Risk: delayed stateside intervention, inability to identify components, and avoidable prosthetic instability after return home.

Downgrade communication reliability if three consecutive core questions require re-asking or two promised callbacks/documents are missed — Rationale: early response behavior predicts cross-border coordination quality under time pressure. Trigger: repeated incomplete answers about records, sedation, specialist access, implant systems, or warranty exclusions. Risk: later failures in treatment planning, discharge handoff, and post-op support.

Keep Cavallini in HOLD status if any core capability needed for your likely scope cannot be verified in writing or by reliable documentary evidence — Rationale: unresolved capability gaps must not be bypassed by enthusiasm or price appeal. Trigger: missing proof on sedation, specialist availability, records review, implant systems, or warranty boundaries. Risk: committing to a provider that cannot safely or predictably support your case.

Do not treat lifetime warranty language as financial protection until return-trip obligations and voiding conditions are listed in ws-01-d — Rationale: coverage can exist on paper while remaining costly to access. Trigger: any reference to a lifetime warranty for implants or prosthetics. Risk: projected Costa Rica savings disappearing after one corrective trip or warranty denial.

For likely full-arch or full-mouth cases, require explicit clarification of trip count, healing intervals, provisional-to-final timing, and U.S.-side adjustment boundaries before GO status — Rationale: multi-phase reconstruction creates repeat travel and follow-up dependencies. Trigger: All-on-X, full-mouth reconstruction, or multi-unit implant/prosthetic planning. Risk: underestimating total timeline, repeat-trip exposure, and post-return adjustment friction.

decision_guide.if_condition: All critical capabilities for the likely procedure scope are verified in writing or documentary evidence, including records review pathway, relevant infrastructure, and warranty boundary summary.
decision_guide.then_action: Mark Cavallini GO in ws-01-e and release structured outputs to Domain 02 and Domain 03.
decision_guide.else_action: Keep Cavallini in HOLD and continue evidence collection only; do not authorize deposit, travel, or date hold.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Any core capability remains unanswered after 5 business days from first request.

decision_guide.if_condition: Cavallini states it will review U.S. records before travel and specifies what diagnostics are required for preliminary planning.
decision_guide.then_action: Mark Domain 02 as unblocked and capture the required records list and contact pathway.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark Domain 02 blocked and treat the case-fit score as downgraded due to same-week plan-change risk.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The clinic requires deposit or arrival in Costa Rica before any records review.

decision_guide.if_condition: IV sedation availability is confirmed for the planned procedure type, including provider, pre-clearance requirements, discharge rules, and escort expectations.
decision_guide.then_action: Record sedation as verified in ws-01-a and note downstream escort and lodging constraints for Domain 04.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark the sedation pathway unresolved and withhold GO status for any sedation-dependent procedure.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Sedation is advertised but the clinic cannot specify first-night recovery or discharge requirements.

decision_guide.if_condition: Your likely case band, travel tolerance, escort availability, work flexibility, and U.S. follow-up prospects make multi-phase Costa Rica treatment operationally realistic.
decision_guide.then_action: Continue with Cavallini evaluation and note case-fit conditions in ws-01-c.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark the case conditional HOLD or NO-GO for cross-border execution even if the clinic itself appears clinically acceptable.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The likely treatment requires multiple trips over 3 to 18 months and you cannot sustain the trip structure.

decision_guide.if_condition: Coverage, exclusions, maintenance requirements, outside-provider modification limits, and return-to-Costa-Rica obligations are supplied in writing.
decision_guide.then_action: Complete ws-01-d and pass warranty constraints to Domain 03 for financial modeling.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark warranty boundary unresolved and downgrade the apparent protection value.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The clinic references a lifetime warranty but will not provide exclusions or logistics in writing.

decision_guide.if_condition: Core questions are answered specifically, in one follow-up cycle when reasonable, and promised callbacks/documents are delivered.
decision_guide.then_action: Record communication reliability as acceptable in the due diligence file.
decision_guide.else_action: Downgrade reliability and require management-level clarification before GO.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Three consecutive core questions require re-asking or two promised callbacks/documents are missed.

decision_guide.if_condition: The clinic confirms expected implant system use for the proposed case type and states that brand/component details will be supplied at discharge.
decision_guide.then_action: Record implant-system verification source and discharge-document expectation in ws-01-a and ws-01-b.
decision_guide.else_action: Leave implant documentation unresolved and treat future U.S. follow-up continuity as at risk.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Implant treatment is under consideration but brand-specific documentation cannot be confirmed.

decision_guide.if_condition: All five worksheets are complete, critical claims are verified or blocked with reason, and ws-01-e records GO, HOLD, or NO-GO with date and rationale.
decision_guide.then_action: Close Chapter 1 and release only the structured outputs allowed by status.
decision_guide.else_action: Leave the chapter open and prohibit downstream execution other than evidence collection.
decision_guide.escalate_when: A pressured timeline or attractive quote is being used to bypass incomplete worksheets.

trigger_blocks: Trigger: Cavallini requests deposit before records review. Immediate response: classify as control exception, suspend commitment, and request written records-review policy and minimum diagnostics list.

Trigger: Website references Nobel Biocare or Zimmer. Immediate response: capture the source, ask whether that system applies to your likely case type, and record whether discharge documentation will include brand details.

Trigger: Website or staff mention IV sedation. Immediate response: confirm provider, procedure eligibility, pre-clearance requirements, escort requirement, and first-night recovery expectations before keeping sedation-dependent treatment in scope.
Trigger: Likely case is full-arch or All-on-X. Immediate response: require explicit trip count, healing interval, provisional-to-final sequence, and return-trip exposure analysis in ws-01-d.
Trigger: A core due diligence question goes unanswered for 5 business days. Immediate response: escalate once in writing and set a 3-business-day response deadline.
Trigger: The clinic answers with marketing language but no operational specifics. Immediate response: keep Cavallini in HOLD and move to provider comparison review before treating it as primary.
Trigger: You cannot identify a plausible U.S. dentist for surveillance or emergency triage. Immediate response: place any implant-supported bridge or All-on-X pathway into conditional HOLD.
Trigger: Warranty terms are mentioned without exclusions. Immediate response: do not count the warranty as protection until ws-01-d contains written limits and return-travel obligations.
risk_blocks: Risk: arrival in Costa Rica with a materially changed treatment plan due to missing record review. Mitigation: require pre-travel record-review confirmation and block deposits or airfare until that pathway is defined.

Risk: selecting Cavallini based on claimed infrastructure that is convenient but not actually available for your procedure. Mitigation: verify each infrastructure element in ws-01-a and ws-01-b with evidence state markers.

Risk: assuming sedation support exists without discharge logistics. Mitigation: verify escort requirement, next-day review expectation, and recovery lodging implications before approving sedation-dependent treatment.

Risk: inability of a U.S. dentist to assist after return because implant brand or component data are missing. Mitigation: verify implant-system documentation and discharge packet expectations during due diligence.
Risk: projected savings erased by warranty access costs. Mitigation: model at least one possible return trip to Costa Rica in ws-01-d and pass that exposure to Domain 03.

Risk: false case fit where the clinic may be acceptable but the patient cannot sustain travel, PTO, escort, or follow-up demands. Mitigation: use ws-01-c to test operational fit before any GO decision.

Risk: communication failures during urgent phases later in treatment. Mitigation: log response times, missed callbacks, and specificity of answers now and downgrade provider status if thresholds are breached.
Risk: ordinary U.S.-side adjustments voiding warranty protection. Mitigation: require written clarification on whether outside-provider modifications affect coverage before approving Cavallini.
output_summaries: Clinic due diligence file: central evidence record proving what Cavallini can and cannot support for your likely case; downstream domains use it as the source of truth.
Cavallini go/HOLD/no-go decision record: formal admission-control result that determines whether the project may progress beyond research.
Cross-border case-fit determination: patient-specific suitability screen that prevents clinically possible but operationally unworkable Costa Rica treatment.
Warranty terms and exclusions summary: converts marketing warranty language into usable operational constraints for later cost and follow-up planning.
Provider comparison sheet versus at least one U.S. option: captures operational tradeoffs, not just price, and feeds later financial justification.
Verified records-review pathway: tells Domain 02 exactly what diagnostics Cavallini requires and how they must be transferred.
Sedation and lodging constraint notes: informs Domain 04 whether escort support and clinic-aligned lodging are optional, recommended, or control-critical.

chapters.scenario_scene: Melissa, a 58-year-old U.S. patient considering All-on-X, receives a broad estimate from Cavallini and is tempted to book a flight within 48 hours to secure a low fare. Instead, she uses ws-01-a and ws-01-b to verify IV sedation, implant-system documentation, and record-review requirements, then discovers the clinic has not yet confirmed what diagnostics are needed for preliminary planning. By day 5, one warranty question and one sedation-discharge question remain unanswered, so she escalates in writing and keeps Cavallini in HOLD. That pause prevents her from placing a deposit that could have exposed her to a 2-trip, 6-to-12-month treatment sequence she had not yet modeled.
success_metrics: 100% of critical claims for the likely case scope are marked as verified, verbally stated but not documented, or unresolved with reason in ws-01-a and ws-01-b.
0 deposits paid, 0 flights booked, and 0 surgery dates held before record-review viability and case-fit screening are completed.
All core due diligence questions receive a specific answer or documented escalation within 5 business days, with one follow-up escalation deadline of 3 additional business days if needed.
All 5 Chapter 1 worksheets are completed before final provider status is recorded in ws-01-e.
At least 1 U.S. provider comparison is documented and 1 written warranty boundary summary is completed before marking Cavallini GO.
chapters.detailed_explanation:
This chapter functions as admission control for the entire system. It is not a general research step and it is not a place for impressions. Its job is to test whether Cavallini is acceptable for your actual likely treatment scope under cross-border conditions. That means the chapter must convert visible claims into verified operating facts, convert patient enthusiasm into case-fit screening, and convert warranty language into explicit obligations. Until that conversion is complete, downstream work stays blocked because every later domain depends on whether this provider can reliably support the treatment pathway you may actually need.

The first operating principle is capability sufficiency. Infrastructure claims matter only when they change control design for your case. An onsite lab is highly relevant when same-stay prosthetic corrections, provisional adjustments, or remakes could affect the length and stability of your trip. Onsite pharmacy access matters because medication fulfillment after surgery is part of discharge execution, not a convenience detail. Onsite hotel integration may be optional for a simple crown workflow, but it becomes control-critical when IV sedation, extractions, immediate implants, or All-on-X increase swelling, fatigue, bleeding risk, and follow-up appointment density. Because these items do not carry equal weight across all procedures, this chapter requires you to verify the infrastructure that is material to your probable scope rather than compiling a generic amenity list.

The second principle is records-before-commitment. A clinic may appear attractive and still remain operationally unacceptable if it will not review U.S. diagnostics before travel or cannot specify what records it needs for preliminary planning. That failure matters because crowns, implants, multi-unit prosthetics, and full-arch cases can change substantially when imaging, periodontal information, medical history, or treatment history are incomplete. The chapter therefore tests whether Cavallini can shift from general sales communication to case-based planning communication. If it cannot, the risk of arriving in Costa Rica to discover a new implant count, graft requirement, extraction sequence, or extended stay becomes too high to justify commitment.

The third principle is patient-side case fit. A clinic can be clinically legitimate while still being the wrong operating model for a specific U.S. patient. The case-fit screen therefore asks whether you can actually support the travel, escort, work leave, repeat-trip structure, and U.S. follow-up demands associated with your likely case band. This distinction becomes especially important in full-mouth and All-on-X pathways, where trip count, healing intervals, provisional-to-final conversion, and bite or esthetic adjustments may extend over months. If the patient cannot sustain that structure, Costa Rica may be a poor fit even if Cavallini itself remains acceptable.

The fourth principle is communication reliability as a control indicator. In domestic care, weak communication is frustrating. In cross-border restorative care, weak communication can become expensive and destabilizing because records, timelines, discharge data, and follow-up questions must travel across distance and time pressure. This chapter therefore treats answer completeness, response speed, and missed callbacks as operational data. A clinic that answers direct questions with exact process descriptions is easier to coordinate than one that responds with broad reassurance. That is why the chapter includes explicit downgrade thresholds rather than leaving communication quality to intuition.

The fifth principle is warranty intelligibility. Lifetime warranty language has little control value until you know four things: what is covered, what is excluded, what maintenance is required to preserve coverage, and who pays the logistics of obtaining corrective care. For a U.S. patient, the access path to the remedy matters almost as much as the remedy itself. If the patient must fund another international trip, hotel stay, time off work, and possibly companion support to obtain a correction, then the warranty remains real but operationally limited. This chapter surfaces that limit early so later cost modeling does not overstate savings or protection.

The chapter closes only after three tests pass. First, capability sufficiency: Cavallini can support the probable clinical scope with verified infrastructure and defined processes. Second, case suitability: the patient can realistically execute cross-border treatment, recovery, and follow-up for that scope. Third, warranty intelligibility: coverage limits and return-trip obligations are explicit enough to model. If any one of those tests remains unresolved, GO is not authorized. The correct output is HOLD or NO-GO, because preserving decision quality here prevents far more expensive failures in records transfer, financial commitment, travel execution, and post-return continuity later in the system.
chapters.narrative:
## Orientation Snapshot
This chapter is the admission control gate for the entire Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System. Its purpose is not to decide whether dental treatment is needed in general, but to determine whether Dr. Marco Muñoz Cavallini International Dental Clinic remains an acceptable provider for this specific U.S. patient, this specific restorative scope, and this specific cross-border operating model. Until this chapter is complete, no deposit, no flight booking, no surgery date hold, and no irreversible tooth preparation is authorized.

The stakes are high because crowns, implants, implant-supported bridges, and All-on-X treatment are not interchangeable consumer purchases. Once the patient commits to an international provider, the decision creates downstream dependencies in records transfer, payment timing, travel sequencing, sedation support, recovery logistics, and U.S. follow-up continuity. A weak due diligence file at this stage can produce three expensive outcomes later: arrival in Costa Rica with an unusable or incomplete treatment plan, acceptance of a warranty that actually requires costly return travel, or selection of a clinic infrastructure that does not match the patient’s procedural complexity.

The required output of this chapter is a documented Cavallini go/no-go decision record, not a general impression. That record must capture verified evidence of clinic infrastructure, specialist access, IV sedation capability, onsite lab support, onsite pharmacy access, onsite hotel integration, implant system usage where relevant, communication performance, and lifetime warranty boundaries. It must also produce a cross-border case-fit determination: whether this patient’s likely scope is suitable for treatment in Costa Rica rather than requiring U.S.-based continuity from the outset.

Decision threshold: if any core capability needed for the probable treatment scope cannot be verified in writing or by reliable documentary evidence, Cavallini remains in HOLD status, not GO. HOLD means no money leaves the patient’s control and no downstream domain is activated except evidence collection. Escalation trigger: if the clinic does not answer core due diligence questions within 5 business days, or answers them only with marketing language and no specifics, the case moves to provider comparison review before Cavallini can remain primary.

## Immediate Execution Path
Start by building the Cavallini Clinic Infrastructure and Restorative Capability Inventory using worksheet ws-01-a. The patient should extract all visible claims from aestheticdentistrycr.com and any direct clinic materials into a controlled list: dentist identity, specialist availability, IV sedation availability, onsite lab, onsite pharmacy, onsite hotel or hotel integration, implant brands, restorative services, warranty references, and contact channels. Each claim is then marked as one of three states: verified by documentary evidence, verbally stated but not documented, or unresolved.

Next, run ws-01-b, the Website Claim Verification and Missing-Evidence Checklist. This is where marketing language gets converted into operational proof. If the site states Nobel Biocare or Zimmer implant system use, the patient must capture where that claim appears and ask whether those systems are used for the proposed case type. If the site references IV sedation, the patient must confirm whether sedation is available for the planned procedure type, who provides it, what pre-clearance is required, and whether a companion is mandatory after discharge.

Communication responsiveness is not a soft factor; it is an operating reliability indicator. Measure first-response time, completeness of answer, willingness to review U.S. records before travel, and whether answers are specific to crowns, implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X rather than generic. If the clinic requires a deposit or surgery date reservation before reviewing records, then mark that as a control exception because it conflicts with the system rule that diagnostic review precedes financial commitment.

Decision rule: if Cavallini confirms willingness to review U.S. records before travel and can state what records are needed for preliminary treatment planning, the clinic remains operationally viable for Domain 02. If the clinic will not review records until arrival in Costa Rica, or cannot specify minimum diagnostics, then the case-fit score drops materially because the risk of same-week treatment-plan change increases. Downstream effect: Domain 02 remains blocked until a record acceptance pathway is clearly defined.

Then complete ws-01-c, the Dental Tourism Case-Fit Screening Form. This step evaluates the patient, not just the clinic. A single crown or a limited implant case may tolerate cross-border execution differently than a medically complex full-mouth reconstruction with multiple extractions, bone grafting, sedation, and provisionalization. The patient must classify the likely case into one of four bands: localized crowns, limited implant restoration, multi-unit implant/prosthetic case, or full-arch/All-on-X reconstruction.

If the case is likely full-arch or full-mouth restoration, add a higher scrutiny layer immediately. Confirm expected trip count, healing intervals, whether provisional and final phases occur on separate trips, and what happens if occlusion, esthetics, or fit require adjustment after the patient returns to the United States. Trigger: any case expected to require more than one Costa Rica trip or provisional-to-final conversion should be flagged for return-trip exposure analysis in ws-01-d before the provider can move to GO.

The warranty review comes next, using ws-01-d. The patient must extract exactly what the lifetime warranty covers, what components are excluded, whether maintenance compliance is required, whether smoking, bruxism, missed hygiene visits, trauma, or outside-provider modifications can void coverage, and whether the patient must return to Costa Rica for remedial work. A lifetime warranty that requires international return travel at the patient’s expense is not false protection, but it is limited protection and must be modeled as an operational obligation.

Finally, use ws-01-e to compare Cavallini with at least one U.S. restorative provider. This is not yet the full cost chapter; here the comparison is about provider acceptability, continuity, and controllability. If Cavallini has stronger infrastructure but weaker warranty accessibility due to travel distance, that tradeoff must be made explicit. Closure decision: Cavallini is marked GO only when evidence, case fit, and warranty boundaries are all documented and no unresolved critical capability remains.

## Deep Operational Guidance
The central logic of this chapter is that international dental treatment is only safe to operationalize when provider capability and case fit intersect. A clinic may be legitimate yet still be a poor match for a given patient’s procedural complexity, time tolerance, healing risk, or follow-up environment. Conversely, a patient may be highly motivated by Costa Rica pricing but still be unsuitable for cross-border restorative work if continuity demands exceed what can realistically be managed after return to the U.S.

Infrastructure verification matters because each claimed capability changes downstream control design. An onsite lab can reduce turnaround time for prosthetic adjustments, remakes, shade modifications, and provisional corrections during the stay in San José. An onsite pharmacy or integrated medication fulfillment pathway can tighten discharge execution and reduce friction after surgery. Onsite hotel integration matters because sedation recovery, next-day review appointments, and transportation burden all improve when lodging is physically aligned with the clinic rather than dispersed across the city.

Do not treat these infrastructure items as equal. For a simple crown sequence, onsite hotel integration may be convenient but not decisive. For IV sedation, extractions with immediate implants, or All-on-X, lodging proximity and post-op logistics become control-critical because swelling, fatigue, bleeding, and medication timing can impair independent movement. If the likely procedure includes IV sedation and the clinic cannot specify escort requirements and first-night recovery expectations, then mark the sedation pathway unresolved and withhold GO status.

Implant system verification must be specific. The patient is not simply confirming that Nobel Biocare or Zimmer appears somewhere in clinic materials; the patient is confirming whether the proposed treatment plan, if accepted, is expected to use one of those systems and whether documentation will be provided for future U.S. follow-up. This matters because a U.S. dentist handling a loose component, broken screw, provisional issue, or implant identification problem after return may need exact brand and component data. Output requirement: the due diligence file must note the implant system(s) discussed, source of confirmation, and any statement about how those details are supplied at discharge.

Communication behavior predicts treatment control quality. A clinic that answers direct questions with exact process descriptions is easier to coordinate across borders than one that replies in broad assurances. The patient should track whether questions are answered in one cycle or require repeated follow-up, whether quotes and timelines are tied to actual records versus estimates, and whether the clinic distinguishes preliminary treatment planning from final diagnosis on arrival. Threshold rule: if three consecutive core questions require re-asking, or if two promised callbacks/documents are missed, communication reliability is downgraded and the clinic cannot be marked GO without management-level clarification.

Case-fit screening must also account for patient-side constraints that are easy to overlook at the research stage. Cross-border restorative care is less forgiving when the patient has limited travel flexibility, no available escort for sedation-related care, uncertain paid time off, unstable access to a cooperative U.S. follow-up dentist, or a history of complex dental revisions. None of these automatically eliminate Cavallini, but they change whether Costa Rica remains operationally suitable. A clinic can be clinically competent while the overall travel-treatment-follow-up system remains unworkable for this specific patient.

Decision rule: if the patient cannot identify a plausible U.S. dentist willing to perform surveillance or emergency triage after return, then any implant-supported bridge or All-on-X case should remain in conditional HOLD even if Cavallini itself appears acceptable. Downstream effect: the project can continue only for information gathering, not commitment. This threshold exists because the first 30 days after a major restorative phase may involve bite issues, tissue irritation, loose temporaries, medication questions, or hygiene concerns that do not justify international travel but still require professional assessment.

Warranty review requires discipline because the phrase lifetime warranty commonly conceals operating conditions. The patient must separate four elements: what is covered, what is excluded, what maintenance is required to preserve coverage, and who pays the logistics of obtaining corrective care. Coverage on paper is weaker than it appears if the patient must fund another flight to San José, another hotel stay, time off work, and potential companion travel just to access the remedy.

If the warranty excludes failures linked to bruxism, poor hygiene, missed maintenance, smoking, trauma, or adjustments performed by outside dentists, then those exclusions must be listed plainly in ws-01-d. If the clinic cannot supply exclusions in writing, mark the warranty boundary as unresolved. If/then decision: if the patient is likely to need U.S.-side occlusal adjustments after return and the warranty may be affected by outside-provider modifications, then Cavallini must clarify that boundary before GO; otherwise the patient risks voiding protection through ordinary follow-up care.

Provider comparison at this stage should focus on operational asymmetry. A U.S. provider may cost more but offer easier remakes, easier emergency access, and less warranty friction. Cavallini may offer stronger integrated infrastructure, such as onsite lab and hotel access, but impose longer-distance correction pathways if a prosthetic issue emerges months later. The patient should write the trade directly: lower procedure cost and integrated travel-treatment environment versus reduced convenience for remedial access once back in the U.S.

Three closure tests should be applied before finishing this chapter. First, capability sufficiency: can Cavallini support the probable clinical scope with verified infrastructure and process clarity? Second, case suitability: can this patient realistically execute international treatment, recovery, and return follow-up for this scope? Third, warranty intelligibility: are coverage limits and return-trip obligations explicit enough to price later? If any of the three tests fail, the chapter remains open.

## Failure Dynamics and Recovery
Failure scenario 1: the patient relies on website claims, accepts a broad verbal estimate, pays a deposit, and books airfare 6 weeks before travel without obtaining record-based preliminary review. On arrival, updated imaging changes the case from straightforward implant restoration to extraction, grafting, or altered implant count, adding several thousand dollars and extending the stay by 3 to 7 nights. Recovery action: freeze further payment beyond unavoidable clinical diagnostics, request a written revised treatment plan, and re-run provider comparison before authorizing irreversible treatment. Preventive control was simple: no deposit or travel until the due diligence file confirmed willingness to review U.S. records and tied planning to actual diagnostics.

Failure scenario 2: the patient sees “lifetime warranty” and assumes long-term protection, but the exclusion list later shows that remedial work requires return to Costa Rica and that outside adjustments may jeopardize coverage. Eight months after provisional or final restoration, a prosthetic issue appears in the U.S., and the patient faces a return flight, hotel stay, time off work, and out-of-pocket local triage because no nearby dentist wants to intervene without detailed component records. Financial consequence can erase projected savings quickly when one extra international trip is added. Recovery action: obtain the written warranty and exclusions immediately, estimate at least one possible return-trip cost, and downgrade claimed savings until warranty accessibility is modeled as real travel exposure.

Failure scenario 3: the clinic states IV sedation is available, but the patient does not confirm discharge requirements. The procedure includes sedation and extensive restorative work; after discharge the patient has no escort for the first night and no reliable plan for medications, transport, or next-day review. This creates risk of missed medication intervals, unmanaged bleeding or swelling, emergency transport costs, and inability to comply with discharge instructions during the first 24 to 48 hours. Recovery action: if escort requirements cannot be met, reschedule before travel or redesign the treatment sequence; do not improvise on site.

Failure scenario 4: the patient assumes any implant brand detail can be reconstructed later and does not insist on brand-specific confirmation. After returning to the U.S., a loose component or restorative complication occurs within 30 days, and the local dentist asks for exact implant system information, restorative details, and procedure notes before touching the case. Delay of even 72 hours can matter if tissue is inflamed, temporaries are unstable, or the bite is functionally compromised. Recovery action: during due diligence, require confirmation that implant system details and discharge documentation will be supplied before departure from Costa Rica.

Escalation conditions should be enforced early rather than rationalized away. If core questions about sedation, implant systems, specialist access, or warranty exclusions remain unanswered after 5 business days, escalate once in writing and set a 3-business-day response deadline. If the clinic still does not provide clear answers, move Cavallini from active primary option to conditional backup or eliminate it entirely. A delayed answer at this stage is a warning about later cross-border coordination under time pressure.

Another failure pattern is false case fit. A patient may be clinically eligible for implants but operationally ineligible for dental tourism because work leave is inflexible, repeat trips are unrealistic, or a U.S. follow-up dentist is unavailable. If/then decision: if the likely treatment requires multiple phases across 3 to 18 months and the patient cannot sustain that trip structure, then Costa Rica should be marked NO-GO for that scope even if the clinic itself is acceptable. The downstream effect is not abandonment of treatment, but redirection toward a U.S.-based provider comparison.

Recovery from an incomplete due diligence chapter is straightforward: do not force a binary yes. Use three statuses only—GO, HOLD, or NO-GO. GO activates Domain 02 and Domain 03. HOLD means information is still collectible and Cavallini remains under review. NO-GO means the provider or cross-border format is unsuitable for this case and should not consume more operational time except archival documentation.

Closure criteria for this chapter are strict. The chapter is operationally complete only when all five worksheets are completed, all critical claims are marked verified or unresolved with reason, the probable case type has been screened for cross-border fit, the lifetime warranty has a written boundary summary, and ws-01-e records a final GO, HOLD, or NO-GO decision with date and rationale. Anything less leaves the system exposed to avoidable uncertainty.

## Cross-Domain Handoffs
This chapter hands off only structured outputs, not impressions. If Cavallini is marked GO, Domain 02 receives the clinic due diligence file, the verified list of required pre-op records if known, the identified contact pathway for records transfer, and any notes about what diagnostics the clinic needs before issuing a preliminary treatment pathway. If that handoff is vague, Domain 02 will fail because records collection will become an unstructured email exercise rather than a controlled packet.

Domain 03 depends on the provider comparison sheet and the warranty terms summary. The financial model must know whether onsite hotel use is likely, whether sedation implies companion travel, whether a second trip is probable, and whether warranty remedies require return travel to Costa Rica. If these conditions are not captured here, the cost chapter will understate total exposure and may falsely show savings versus U.S. care.
