There is also a future handoff to Domain 04, even though travel execution has not started. This chapter should surface whether IV sedation is expected, whether escort rules exist, whether onsite hotel integration is recommended or required, and whether procedure complexity suggests staying near the clinic rather than selecting unrelated lodging. These details become travel readiness constraints later.

Final decision gate: release the case into downstream execution only when the Cavallini go/no-go decision record is signed complete by the patient, all unresolved items are noncritical, and no unanswered question could materially change provider acceptability, case fit, or warranty value. Operational completeness is achieved when Cavallini is either approved with defined conditions or removed from consideration with documented reasons. That disciplined stop/go boundary is what prevents an attractive website and a lower quote from overruling the actual control system.

chapters.chapter_title: Pre-Operative Dental Record Assembly, Secure Transfer to Cavallini, and Diagnostic Sufficiency Confirmation

chapter_opener.what_this_is_for: This chapter converts scattered U.S. dental records into one controlled diagnostic packet that Cavallini can actually use for preliminary treatment planning before you book travel or pay toward major restorative care.
chapter_opener.when_it_matters: It matters as soon as Cavallini is still under consideration and before any deposit, surgery-date hold, airfare purchase, or IV sedation trip planning for crowns, implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X treatment.
chapter_opener.failure_looks_like: Failure looks like sending partial or stale files from multiple U.S. offices without naming control, quality checks, receipt confirmation, or a written sufficiency decision, leaving your quote, travel dates, and treatment scope unstable.
produces: Completed pre-op records packet inventory with source, date, format, and status for each required item
Transmission log with file-by-file delivery trail and Cavallini acknowledgement status
Written Cavallini diagnostic sufficiency confirmation or named missing-item list
Controlled packet version that downstream cost and travel decisions can rely on
do_first: Open ws-02-a and create one line item for each required record: panoramic X-ray, CBCT if available, intraoral photo set, periodontal charting, treatment history, medication list, allergy list if relevant, and medical-condition notes affecting surgery or sedation.
Assign a source office, source contact, record date, expected format, request date, and current status to every item before requesting anything.

minimum_viable_actions: Build the master inventory in ws-02-a before contacting any U.S. office.
Use ws-02-c to request original digital records and releases from each source office or imaging center.
Rename every file using one convention: Last_First_RecordType_Source_YYYY-MM-DD.
Run ws-02-b quality control on pano, CBCT, photos, perio charting, and medication list before any transfer.
Send the packet in one controlled submission event or one clearly linked sequence and log it in ws-02-d.
Do not advance to deposit, firm quote reliance, or flight booking until ws-02-e records a written Cavallini sufficiency decision.
quick_reference_rules: Treat the packet as a controlled deliverable, not an email chain — Rationale: Cavallini can only plan reliably from a complete evidence set tied to your identity and dates. Trigger: when you start collecting records from more than one U.S. source. Risk: treatment plan instability, duplicate imaging, and quote changes after arrival in San José.

Do not count a file as delivered until Cavallini confirms receipt and readability — Rationale: sending attachments does not prove they opened, matched, or remained intact. Trigger: after every upload or email transmission. Risk: false assumption that planning is underway when critical diagnostics were never usable.

If a pano is older than 12 months in an implant, extraction, full-mouth, or All-on-X case, flag it as potentially stale — Rationale: anatomy, pathology, and treatment sequence may have changed. Trigger: while reviewing imaging dates in ws-02-a and ws-02-b. Risk: unreliable preliminary quote, re-imaging in Costa Rica, and extra hotel nights.

If a recent CBCT exists for an implant or full-arch case, obtain and transmit it before any deposit or flight booking — Rationale: 3D data materially improves preliminary planning reliability. Trigger: when the contemplated treatment includes implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X. Risk: placeholder estimates, altered implant strategy, or same-week redesign after arrival.

Mark any low-resolution, printed, faxed, screenshot, or phone-photo record as quality risk — Rationale: proof of existence is not the same as diagnostic usability. Trigger: when a U.S. office cannot provide original or clinic-usable digital files. Risk: Cavallini receives nonfunctional evidence and planning remains provisional.

If medical clearance may be required, move it onto the critical path immediately — Rationale: clearance delays can block sedation or surgery even when travel is already booked. Trigger: when medication history, medical conditions, or clinic feedback indicate physician review. Risk: flight change penalties, procedure delay, or surgery cancellation.

If there have been more than 2 corrective transmissions, stop and rebuild the packet as a version-controlled complete set — Rationale: piecemeal re-sends create duplicate and superseded file confusion. Trigger: after repeated missing-file corrections. Risk: clinic review against outdated images or charts.

Receipt is not sufficiency — Rationale: 'records received' only confirms arrival, not that the file can support preliminary treatment planning. Trigger: when the clinic acknowledges delivery without stating whether the packet is sufficient. Risk: downstream cost and travel decisions made without a validated planning basis.

decision_guide.decision: Is the record inventory complete enough to begin packet assembly?
decision_guide.if_condition: ws-02-a lists every required item with source office, contact, date, expected format, request date, and status.
decision_guide.then_action: Proceed to records requests using ws-02-c and start collecting the files against the inventory.
decision_guide.else_action: Stop and complete the inventory first; assign an owner and status to each missing line item.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Any required item still has no source owner or status after the initial inventory build.

decision_guide.decision: Can an existing panoramic X-ray be used for preliminary planning?
decision_guide.if_condition: The pano is readable, dated, linked to the patient, and not older than 12 months for an implant, extraction, full-mouth, or All-on-X case unless Cavallini explicitly accepts it.
decision_guide.then_action: Include it in the packet and log its date and source in ws-02-a and ws-02-b.
decision_guide.else_action: Request updated imaging or ask Cavallini whether the existing pano is acceptable only for limited preliminary review.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The case depends on implants or full-arch planning and no current orientation image is available.

decision_guide.decision: Is CBCT required on the critical path?
decision_guide.if_condition: A recent CBCT exists and the case includes implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X treatment.
decision_guide.then_action: Acquire the CBCT in DICOM or clinic-usable digital format and transmit it before any deposit or travel booking.
decision_guide.else_action: Ask Cavallini whether pano, photos, perio charting, and history are sufficient for preliminary planning and record the answer in ws-02-e.
decision_guide.escalate_when: A CBCT exists but the imaging center or office cannot provide a usable export, or Cavallini cannot confirm the format is usable.

decision_guide.decision: Are the files diagnostically usable for cross-border review?
decision_guide.if_condition: ws-02-b confirms each image or document is readable, correctly oriented, dated, identified, and complete enough for planning context.
decision_guide.then_action: Assemble the version-controlled packet and prepare a single transmission event.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark the item as incomplete or quality risk, re-request the source file, and do not present the packet as final.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Any required item remains unreadable, partial, undated, or identity-mismatched after one correction cycle.

decision_guide.decision: Has Cavallini actually received and opened the packet?
decision_guide.if_condition: ws-02-d shows file names, send date/time, submission method, recipient, and a clinic acknowledgement confirming receipt and readability.
decision_guide.then_action: Request formal diagnostic sufficiency review and log the response path.
decision_guide.else_action: Follow up with a structured list of every transmitted file and request confirmation of what was opened versus what is missing.
decision_guide.escalate_when: No acknowledgement is received within 2 business days or no clear receipt/readability response is received within 5 business days.

decision_guide.decision: Is the packet sufficient to support downstream cost and travel actions?
decision_guide.if_condition: ws-02-e records Cavallini's written statement that the packet is sufficient for preliminary treatment planning, with any caveats clearly named.
decision_guide.then_action: Release the handoff package to Domain 03 for quote reconciliation and to Domain 04 for date-based readiness planning if a pathway is issued.
decision_guide.else_action: Keep this domain open, resolve named missing items, and label any quote or schedule discussion as provisional only.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The clinic states that final planning remains blocked by missing diagnostics, medical clearance, or unreadable files.

trigger_blocks: Trigger: Cavallini requests records or you begin gathering diagnostics from U.S. providers -> Response: start ws-02-a as the master control sheet and create a tracked line item for every required record.

Trigger: A U.S. office offers only printed copies, screenshots, faxed charting, or low-resolution images -> Response: mark the item as quality risk in ws-02-b and request original digital output before treating it as final.

Trigger: A pano is older than 12 months in an implant or full-mouth case -> Response: request updated imaging or obtain written clinic acceptance that the older image is sufficient only for limited preliminary review.
Trigger: A recent CBCT exists for an implant or All-on-X case -> Response: obtain the export in DICOM or clinic-usable digital format and verify that it can be opened before travel planning advances.

Trigger: Medication history or medical conditions suggest physician review may be needed -> Response: request the exact medical-clearance requirement set and move clearance tracking onto the critical path.

Trigger: No clinic acknowledgement appears within 2 business days after transmission -> Response: send a file-by-file follow-up referencing ws-02-d and request confirmation of receipt and readability.

Trigger: No substantive sufficiency response appears within 5 business days -> Response: escalate through the clinic coordinator and ask which files were opened, which were missing, and whether preliminary planning is blocked.
Trigger: More than 2 separate corrective transmissions have occurred -> Response: stop piecemeal updates and rebuild a single version-controlled packet with only current files.
risk_blocks: Risk: fragmented multi-office record collection produces mismatched or missing diagnostics -> Mitigation: use ws-02-a and ws-02-c to assign each record to one source, one request date, and one status before transmission.

Risk: stale imaging leads to unreliable planning for implants or full-mouth restoration -> Mitigation: review record age in ws-02-b and treat older diagnostics as conditional unless Cavallini explicitly accepts them.

Risk: low-quality files force re-imaging after arrival in Costa Rica -> Mitigation: reject screenshots, cropped images, cellphone photos of monitors, unreadable faxes, and unopened CBCT exports before submission.

Risk: expired transfer links or unlogged emails leave the clinic without a usable complete set -> Mitigation: transmit in one controlled event or linked sequence and log every file, date, recipient, and acknowledgement in ws-02-d.

Risk: medication omissions or unresolved medical clearance block sedation or surgery close to departure -> Mitigation: update the medication list to the transmission date and resolve clearance at least 14 days before tentative travel for major surgery or IV sedation.

Risk: receipt is mistaken for sufficiency and downstream teams act too early -> Mitigation: require ws-02-e with a written yes/no sufficiency determination before approving firm quote reliance, deposits, or trip locking.

Risk: duplicate and superseded files confuse the clinic's review set -> Mitigation: apply one naming convention and rebuild the full packet after repeated corrections instead of continuing ad hoc re-sends.
output_summaries: Pre-op records packet inventory: defines every required diagnostic item, source, date, format, and status so packet assembly can be managed instead of guessed.
File quality verification record: shows whether pano, CBCT, photos, charting, and medication data are clinically usable or still carry quality risk.
Provider request and release routing trail: proves that each U.S. office was asked for the correct records through a traceable release path.
Transmission log and clinic receipt register: creates the auditable trail needed to confirm what Cavallini actually received and opened.
Diagnostic sufficiency confirmation or missing-item list: determines whether Domain 03 can model costs on a credible clinical scope and whether Domain 04 can plan dates against a real pathway.
Archived version-controlled packet: establishes a reusable documentation standard for later discharge, warranty, and U.S. follow-up handoffs.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: U.S. Diagnostic Record Packet Inventory for Cavallini Treatment Planning
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use at the very start of this chapter before contacting any U.S. dentist, specialist, or imaging center.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks organized record collection and prevents fragmented packet assembly.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Panoramic, CBCT, Periodontal, and Photo File Quality Verification Checklist for Cross-Border Use
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use after files begin arriving and before anything is sent to Cavallini as a final or near-final packet.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks reliable transmission by separating diagnostically usable files from proof-of-existence files.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: U.S. Provider Records Request and Release Routing Form for Cavallini Submission
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use when requesting diagnostics from general dentists, periodontists, oral surgeons, endodontists, and imaging centers.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks timely release of records and exposes authorization, fee, or routing barriers before they delay the packet.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Cross-Border Diagnostic Transmission Log and Clinic Receipt Register
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use at the moment each file, upload, or packet version is transmitted to Cavallini.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks follow-up, receipt confirmation, and escalation when files are missing, unreadable, or unacknowledged.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Cavallini Diagnostic Sufficiency Confirmation and Missing-Item Resolution Form
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use immediately after the clinic acknowledges receipt and begins records review.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks controlled handoff into Domain 03 cost reconciliation and Domain 04 travel readiness only when planning sufficiency is stated in writing.

cascade_triggers: When Cavallini acknowledges receipt of the complete pre-op records packet, trigger preliminary quote reconciliation in US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control.
When ws-02-e records that diagnostics are sufficient for preliminary treatment planning, trigger scope-based budget modeling in US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control.

When diagnostic sufficiency is confirmed and Cavallini issues a planning pathway tied to your actual records, trigger appointment-timed flight and lodging coordination in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness.

When the records packet reveals likely medical-clearance needs, multiple-stage treatment, or conditional same-trip delivery, trigger constraint-based planning adjustments in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness.

When Cavallini identifies named missing items instead of sufficiency, trigger a blocked-status hold on downstream deposits, final quote reliance, and travel locking until the missing-item cycle is closed.
chapters.scenario_scene: Jane Smith in Texas wants an All-on-X consultation and hopes to book flights within 3 weeks. Her records are spread across a general dentist, a periodontist, and an imaging center, and one CBCT link expires after 72 hours. She uses ws-02-a through ws-02-d to rebuild the file into one version-controlled packet, gets a written Cavallini response on day 5 that the records are sufficient with one medication clarification, and avoids purchasing a nonrefundable ticket against an unstable treatment scope. The measurable outcome is that Domain 03 can now compare Costa Rica versus U.S. costs using a packet Cavallini has actually reviewed, rather than a rough estimate built on missing diagnostics.
success_metrics: 100% of required record categories listed in ws-02-a with source, date, expected format, and status before packet transmission.
0 required files transmitted without standardized naming and source/date labeling.
Clinic acknowledgement of receipt and readability obtained within 2 business days of transmission, or follow-up triggered on day 2.
Substantive sufficiency response or missing-item clarification obtained within 5 business days of submission, or escalation triggered on day 5.
Medical clearance, if required for major surgery or IV sedation, identified and pursued early enough that unresolved clearance remains 0 items at 14 days before tentative travel.
No downstream deposit approval or flight booking based on a packet lacking ws-02-e sufficiency status.
chapters.detailed_explanation:
This chapter controls the evidence base for the entire case. In a domestic dental workflow, incomplete records often cause inconvenience. In this cross-border system, incomplete records create financial and logistical instability because quote comparison, deposit timing, and travel readiness all depend on whether Cavallini can plan from real diagnostics instead of assumptions. That is why the operating target is not 'send records' but 'produce a controlled packet with traceable receipt and a written sufficiency decision.'

The minimum evidence set comes from the chapter narrative and plan: panoramic X-ray, CBCT if available, intraoral photographs, periodontal charting, treatment history, medication list, allergy information if relevant, and any medical conditions likely to affect surgery or IV sedation. The packet must be assembled as one coherent object. If diagnostics arrive from three offices in three different formats over a week with inconsistent file names, the clinic may technically receive data while still lacking a usable planning package. Operationally, that is a failed handoff.

Imaging quality and age matter because restorative travel compresses decision time. A pano usually provides orientation, but complex implant and full-arch planning often depends on whether a recent CBCT exists. If one already exists, obtaining it early materially improves preliminary planning reliability. If no CBCT exists, that does not automatically stop the case, but it does require a documented answer from Cavallini about whether the remaining file supports only broad discussion or a more credible preliminary pathway. That distinction changes whether Domain 03 is modeling a real scope or just a placeholder estimate.

Periodontal charting, treatment history, and medication data carry equal operational weight even though patients often undervalue them. Tissue status, prior failures, extractions, endodontic history, mobility, anticoagulants, diabetes medications, bisphosphonates, and other healing or bleeding factors can alter sequencing, sedation readiness, and final candidacy for same-trip restorative work. If the clinic signals that medical clearance may be required, that requirement becomes a critical-path dependency. Travel should remain blocked until the clearance timeline is known and managed.

Transmission discipline is the final control layer. Use the clinic's requested submission method, log every file in ws-02-d, and require a clear difference between receipt and sufficiency. Receipt means the files arrived. Sufficiency means Cavallini can use them for preliminary treatment planning. Only the second state supports controlled advancement into cost reconciliation and date-based travel readiness. If the clinic instead names missing items, this domain remains open and downstream commitments stay blocked until the missing control object is obtained and logged.
chapters.narrative:
## Orientation Snapshot

This domain controls whether Cavallini can plan your case from evidence instead of assumptions. For crowns, implants, implant-supported bridges, and All-on-X treatment, the difference between a usable pre-op file and a fragmented one is the difference between a written preliminary pathway and a same-week redesign after you arrive in San José. The operational stake is high because this chapter sits upstream of both the US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control and Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness. If the record packet is incomplete, old, unlabeled, or spread across multiple U.S. offices without a transmission log, every downstream quote, deposit, and flight decision becomes unstable.

Your objective in this domain is not merely to “send records.” It is to produce a controlled deliverable: one pre-op records packet, one transmission history, one acknowledgement from Cavallini, and one written diagnostic sufficiency decision. The required evidence set is defined by the chapter plan: panoramic X-rays, CBCT data if available, intraoral photographs, periodontal charting, treatment history, medication list, and any medical clearance requirements that may affect sedation or major restorative work. The packet must be assembled early enough that Cavallini can identify gaps before travel is booked, not after.

Decision threshold: if you do not have a documented Cavallini acknowledgement that the packet was received and is readable, treat the file as not delivered. Emailing files without confirmation does not satisfy this domain. Output required before moving forward: completed ws-02-a inventory, ws-02-d transmission log, and ws-02-e sufficiency confirmation or missing-item list.

The common gap in this domain is operational fragmentation. Patients often request a pano from one dentist, periodontal notes from another office, a CBCT from an imaging center, and medication information from memory, then send them in separate messages over several days. Cavallini then receives mismatched patient names, incomplete arches, screenshots instead of original image files, or radiographs without dates. That pattern produces provisional treatment discussions that cannot support deposit, schedule, or travel commitments.

## Immediate Execution Path

Start by opening ws-02-a, the U.S. Diagnostic Record Packet Inventory for Cavallini Treatment Planning, and make it your master control sheet. Create one line item for each required record: panoramic X-ray, CBCT if available, full intraoral photo set, periodontal charting, restorative and extraction history, current medication list, allergy list if relevant to treatment, and any known medical conditions likely to affect surgery or IV sedation. Assign a source office, source contact, record date, expected format, request date, and status to each item. Until each item has an owner and status, you do not have a packet; you have a wish list.

Next, use ws-02-c, the U.S. Provider Records Request and Release Routing Form, to request records from each source. Ask for original diagnostic files where applicable, not compressed screenshots or printed phone photos of monitors. For radiology, request the panoramic image in its original digital format and ask whether the CBCT can be exported in DICOM with a viewer. For periodontal charting, request the most recent chart with probing depths, recession if available, bleeding notation if available, and date of capture.

If a source office says they can only provide a printed copy or low-resolution image, then mark that item as “quality risk” in ws-02-b and do not transmit it to Cavallini as the final version without noting the limitation. If the panoramic X-ray is older than 12 months and your case involves implants, full-mouth restoration, extractions, or All-on-X consideration, then treat it as potentially stale and request updated imaging before relying on it for travel-linked planning. Downstream effect: stale or low-grade diagnostics weaken quote reliability and can force re-imaging, extra hotel nights, or a changed procedure sequence after arrival.

Build the packet in one naming convention before any transfer. Minimum naming rule: patient last name, first name, record type, source office, and date in YYYY-MM-DD format. Example structure: Smith_Jane_Pano_USDentist_2026-03-04 or Smith_Jane_PerioChart_PerioOffice_2026-02-18. This prevents file collision when Cavallini receives multiple attachments and allows ws-02-d to match each transmitted item to its source.

Then perform quality control using ws-02-b, the Panoramic, CBCT, Periodontal, and Photo File Quality Verification Checklist for Cross-Border Use. Verify that every image is readable, oriented correctly, dated, and linked to your identity. Confirm that the photo set is not partial; if only smile photos exist but there are no retracted intraoral views, mark the packet incomplete for aesthetic or restorative planning. Confirm that the medication list is current as of the transmission date, not copied from a prior annual physical without review.

Decision threshold: if any required item is missing, unreadable, or older than the acceptable planning window defined by the treating context, do not ask Cavallini for a definitive scheduling commitment. Instead, request a preliminary records review and ask for a missing-item list. Output: a controlled packet suitable for provisional or preliminary planning, with identified deficiencies explicitly logged rather than hidden.

When the packet is ready, transmit it using one planned submission event or one clearly linked sequence, and record every action in ws-02-d, the Cross-Border Diagnostic Transmission Log and Clinic Receipt Register. Log date, time, method, file names, recipient, acknowledgement received, and any missing-file response. If no acknowledgement is received within 2 business days, trigger follow-up. If no clear receipt confirmation is received within 5 business days, escalate through the clinic contact channel and do not assume the case is advancing.

Your execution target is written diagnostic sufficiency confirmation from Cavallini. That confirmation may say the records are sufficient for preliminary treatment planning, sufficient with caveats, or insufficient pending named items. The acceptable output is not silence and not a vague statement such as “looks good.” You need ws-02-e completed with a yes/no determination on sufficiency, any missing items, and whether a preliminary treatment pathway can now be issued.

## Deep Operational Guidance

This chapter exists because cross-border dentistry compresses uncertainty into expensive moments. In a domestic case, incomplete records may only mean another local visit. In this system, incomplete records can distort financial comparison, mis-sequence travel, and break the handoff from due diligence into scheduling. Cavallini’s planning quality depends not only on clinical skill but on the integrity of what you send. The packet therefore functions as a control object across jurisdictions, offices, and payment decisions.

The panoramic X-ray is usually the minimum orientation image, but it is rarely enough on its own for complex implant or full-arch planning. A pano can show broad anatomy, missing teeth, prior restorations, and obvious pathology patterns, but it does not replace 3D information where implant position, sinus anatomy, bone volume, or nerve proximity matter. That is why the operating rule is “CBCT if available,” not “CBCT optional in effect.” If your U.S. providers already have a recent CBCT and can lawfully release it, the file materially improves preliminary planning reliability and reduces the chance that Cavallini’s in-person findings differ sharply from the remote case concept.

Decision rule: if the case under consideration includes implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X and a recent CBCT exists, then acquire and transmit it before any deposit or flight booking. If no CBCT exists, then ask Cavallini whether preliminary planning can proceed from pano plus photos plus periodontal and treatment history, and log their answer in ws-02-e. Downstream effect: this answer determines whether Domain 03 is comparing a credible preliminary treatment scope or a placeholder estimate.

Periodontal charting and treatment history are often undervalued by patients because they are less visually obvious than X-rays. Operationally, they matter because implant and restorative sequencing depends on tissue status, prior failures, extractions, endodontic history, mobility, and maintenance patterns. A patient who sends images without charting may appear straightforward until active periodontal problems or unstable teeth change the restorative foundation. That can alter whether treatment is staged, whether provisional work is advisable, and whether the final cost and time horizon remain acceptable.

Your medication list must be current to the date of transmission. “Current” means reviewed against what you are actually taking now, including prescriptions, anticoagulants, bisphosphonates if relevant, diabetes medications, immunosuppressive therapy, and supplements with bleeding implications if known. This list supports not only Cavallini’s treatment planning but also identification of whether medical clearance may be required for sedation or surgery. Incomplete medication disclosure can create last-minute surgical delays or same-day contraindication review.

If the clinic indicates that medical clearance may be required, treat that as a critical-path dependency, not a side task. Obtain the exact clearance question set or criteria, identify the responsible U.S. physician, and time-box the request. If clearance is not in hand at least 14 days before a tentative travel date for major restorative surgery or IV sedation, then Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness should remain blocked. Downstream effect: flights and hotel dates should not be finalized against unresolved medical clearance risk.

File quality control is not clerical; it is a planning safeguard. A common failure mode is transmitting “proof of existence” rather than diagnostically usable files. Examples include cellphone photos of printed radiographs, screenshots cropped without patient identifiers, zipped folders that cannot be opened, CBCT discs without exportable files, and intraoral photos that only show the front smile. Cavallini may still respond courteously, but the internal reality is that treatment planning remains incomplete. Your control standard is whether an outside treating team can actually review the file, not whether you have technically sent something.

Set acceptance thresholds in ws-02-b. For panoramic images: readable full arch, date visible, patient identity linked, no severe compression artifact. For CBCT: exportable DICOM or clinic-usable digital set, date visible, source known. For photos: frontal smile, retracted frontal, right and left buccal, upper occlusal, lower occlusal if possible, each adequately lit and in focus. For charting: dated periodontal chart from a named office. For treatment history: concise chronology of extractions, implants, crowns, root canals, failed dental work, and symptoms relevant to the target arch.

Another hidden dependency is records age. In slower-moving cosmetic decisions, older records may still orient the conversation. In restorative travel planning, elapsed time matters because decay, fractures, infections, and bone conditions can change. A panoramic image from 18 months ago may no longer represent current reality for a patient with active breakdown, and a medication list from six months ago may miss drugs that affect bleeding or healing. Use a conservative operating assumption: if records are old enough that they could materially change treatment sequence, ask Cavallini whether they remain acceptable for preliminary planning and log the answer.

If Cavallini replies that the packet is sufficient only for preliminary discussion and that final decisions depend on in-person imaging or exam, then treat every quote and proposed schedule as conditional. Do not convert a conditional pathway into a firm financial or travel commitment. Domain 03 can still model costs, but it must label them preliminary. Domain 04 must not lock travel around a treatment assumption that the clinic itself has not validated.

Security and routing discipline matter because this domain spans personal health information and international transmission. Use the clinic’s requested submission method and avoid scattering files across unlinked emails, text threads, and consumer file-sharing links unless the clinic explicitly accepts that workflow. The operational aim is traceability: every transmitted file should be reproducible from ws-02-d, and every acknowledgment should point to a known packet version. If you revise the packet after initial submission, assign a version date and list only the delta files so the clinic is not forced to guess which pano or chart is current.

Decision threshold: if there have been more than 2 separate transmissions correcting prior omissions, stop and rebuild the packet as a version-controlled complete set rather than continuing piecemeal. Downstream effect: this reduces planning errors caused by duplicate or superseded files and increases the chance of a clean sufficiency confirmation.

## Failure Dynamics and Recovery

Failure scenario 1: the patient sends a panoramic X-ray from 16 months ago, three smile photos, and a self-written history, but no periodontal charting, no CBCT despite a prior scan existing, and no current medication list. Cavallini gives a broad estimate for implant-supported restoration, but on arrival new imaging reveals additional extractions, different bone conditions, and a wider prosthetic scope. Consequence: the case cost can rise by thousands of dollars, the patient may need extra hotel nights, and the original US-vs-Costa Rica comparison becomes invalid because it was built on weak diagnostics. Recovery action: stop downstream commitments as soon as the gap is identified, request the full missing set, and re-open Domain 03 only after ws-02-e shows sufficiency or explicitly bounded caveats.

Failure scenario 2: files are sent from three U.S. offices over 9 days with inconsistent names and no transmission log. One office emails a pano, another uploads a CBCT link that expires after 72 hours, and the periodontal office faxes charting that is unreadable when scanned. The patient assumes the clinic has “everything” because each office said it was sent. Consequence: Cavallini never receives a usable complete set, scheduling discussions remain provisional, and if airfare is purchased during this window the patient risks nonrefundable travel loss if the case cannot be confirmed. Recovery action: use ws-02-d to reconstruct what was actually delivered, re-request expired or unreadable items, and issue one consolidated packet with version labeling.

Failure scenario 3: the patient is planning IV sedation and extensive implant surgery, but the medication list omits an anticoagulant and no physician clearance is requested. Seven days before departure, the clinic identifies the omission during final review and states that clearance is required before proceeding. Consequence: the patient may face surgery delay, flight change penalties, extra lodging, or cancellation exposure if deposits were already paid. In more serious terms, proceeding without full medication review can create bleeding or anesthesia management risk. Recovery action: escalate the clearance request same day, obtain physician documentation, and if it cannot be secured at least 72 hours before departure, trigger travel rescheduling rather than betting on day-before resolution.

Failure scenario 4: the patient sends a CBCT on a physical disc only and assumes the clinic can access it after arrival. At pre-op review in Costa Rica, the data cannot be opened on available systems, forcing repeat imaging locally. Consequence: this may not be catastrophic, but it can add same-day delay, duplicate imaging cost, and a changed treatment conversation with little time to decide. Recovery action: before travel, verify that the CBCT has been opened or accepted in clinic-usable format; if no such confirmation exists, treat the CBCT as nonfunctional and plan accordingly.

Failure scenario 5: the patient treats the clinic’s statement “records received” as equivalent to “records sufficient.” These are not the same operational status. Receipt only proves file arrival; sufficiency confirms that the file supports preliminary planning. Consequence: the patient may enter payment and booking phases without knowing whether critical diagnostics are still missing. Recovery action: obtain a binary or clearly qualified sufficiency statement in ws-02-e before allowing any schedule-dependent downstream control to advance.

Explicit escalation rules are required here. If no clinic acknowledgement is received within 2 business days of transmission, send a structured follow-up listing every attached file by name. If no substantive response on readability or sufficiency is received within 5 business days, escalate through the designated clinic coordinator and request confirmation of what was opened versus what remains missing. If a required U.S. provider has not released requested records within 7 business days, follow up directly and confirm whether a signed authorization, fee, or alternate release path is blocking completion.

Operational recovery always follows the same pattern: identify the missing control object, not the vague problem. The missing control object may be a dated pano, a readable perio chart, a current medication list, a functioning CBCT export, or a written sufficiency statement. Once identified, assign owner, deadline, and downstream block status. This prevents the common failure of “working around” missing diagnostics by accelerating travel planning prematurely.

Closure criteria for this chapter are strict. The chapter is operationally complete only when five conditions are met: ws-02-a shows every required record item with source and status; ws-02-b confirms file quality or explicitly logs limitations; ws-02-c shows all requests and releases completed; ws-02-d shows the actual transmission and clinic acknowledgement trail; and ws-02-e records Cavallini’s written determination that the packet is sufficient for preliminary treatment planning or lists named missing items still blocking scheduling. If any one of these is absent, this domain remains open.

## Cross-Domain Handoffs

This domain hands off directly into two downstream controls, and the handoff quality determines whether those domains can operate on facts. First, when Cavallini acknowledges receipt of the complete pre-op records packet, it triggers preliminary quote reconciliation in US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control. The financial team for your own decision-making cannot compare Costa Rica versus U.S. care accurately if the clinical scope is still unstable. Therefore the handoff package must include the accepted inventory, the clinic’s receipt acknowledgement, and any caveats about missing diagnostics or possible in-person changes.

Decision rule: if Cavallini’s sufficiency confirmation says the current file supports only broad preliminary discussion and not a credible procedural pathway, then Domain 03 must mark all savings estimates as provisional and must not approve deposit timing as final. If Cavallini confirms that the diagnostics are sufficient for preliminary treatment planning and issues a planning pathway tied to your actual records, then Domain 03 may produce the all-in Costa Rica treatment budget and comparable U.S. worksheet on a controlled basis.

Second, when diagnostic sufficiency is confirmed and a planning pathway is issued, it triggers date-based flight and lodging coordination in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness. That trigger exists because scheduling without a stable diagnostic file causes cascading loss: flights may be booked before clearance needs are known, lodging may be too short for the actual case, and a companion may not be arranged for sedation-related recovery support. Domain 04 therefore depends on this chapter not just for files but for confidence level.

If the records packet reveals likely medical clearance needs, multiple-stage treatment, or uncertainty about same-trip prosthetic delivery, then Domain 04 must plan around those constraints before selecting flights or onsite hotel nights. If the packet is complete and Cavallini has accepted it for preliminary planning, then Domain 04 can align appointment timing, arrival buffer, and recovery duration to a defined pathway rather than guesswork. Downstream effect: fewer change fees, fewer extra nights, and lower risk of being in Costa Rica without sufficient treatment certainty.

This domain also supports later continuity of care, even though the formal handoff occurs after treatment. The discipline you establish here—file naming, packet control, and transmission logging—should become the model for discharge packet management and return-to-U.S. follow-up. A patient who cannot reconstruct what was sent before surgery usually also struggles to reconstruct what was done after surgery. For that reason, archive the final pre-op packet and all acknowledgements in a case folder that will remain available through treatment completion and warranty tracking.

Final closure decision: this chapter is complete only when Cavallini has confirmed, in writing, one of two states—either the records are diagnostically sufficient for preliminary treatment planning, or the clinic has identified the exact missing items preventing sufficiency. In the first state, the case advances to cost reconciliation and travel readiness. In the second state, this domain remains active and blocks downstream commitments until the missing-item resolution cycle is closed.

chapters.chapter_title: All-In Cavallini Price Modeling, U.S. Comparison, Deposit Timing, and Complication Reserve Control

chapter_opener.what_this_is_for: This chapter determines whether Cavallini treatment is still financially justified after procedure price, travel, recovery, payment friction, cancellation exposure, and complication reserve are all fully modeled.
chapter_opener.when_it_matters: Use this chapter after Cavallini remains under consideration and after diagnostically sufficient records have produced a preliminary treatment path, but before any deposit, airfare purchase, or nonrefundable lodging commitment.
chapter_opener.failure_looks_like: You approve Costa Rica based on the quoted dental fee alone, then later discover that flights, hotel extensions, companion travel, missed work, card or wire fees, or an unplanned second trip erase the savings or make the case unaffordable.
produces: All-in Costa Rica treatment budget
Comparable U.S. treatment cost worksheet
Net savings or justified-overrun decision sheet
Deposit and payment calendar
Complication contingency reserve amount
do_first: Open ws-03-a and capture the Cavallini quote exactly as offered, including implant brand, prosthetic scope, sedation assumptions, temporaries, finals, trip count, and any bundled items.
Open ws-03-c and obtain at least one like-for-like U.S. comparator before making any savings claim or releasing any deposit.

minimum_viable_actions: Enter the Cavallini quote into ws-03-a without combining categories or rounding bundled fees.
Build ws-03-b with every non-clinical Costa Rica cost: airfare, lodging, local transport, food, pharmacy, companion costs, missed work, and emergency return-trip exposure.
Normalize at least one U.S. comparator in ws-03-c so implant brand, prosthetic type, sedation, arch count, and trip assumptions match as closely as possible.
Complete ws-03-d before payment by recording deposit deadlines, refund triggers, payment method, foreign transaction fees, wire costs, and backup payment rails.
Set the complication reserve in ws-03-e and verify that the reserve is accessible in cash before authorizing deposit release.
If any worksheet is incomplete, mark the financial status as "incomplete for commitment" and stop booking activity.
quick_reference_rules: Do not claim Costa Rica savings until ws-03-a through ws-03-e are complete — Rationale: a dental fee alone is not a financial model. Trigger: any time a quote arrives or booking pressure starts. Risk: false savings, premature deposit release, and avoidable nonrefundable loss.

Compare like-for-like restorative scope only — Rationale: implant surgery alone cannot be compared against surgery plus provisional plus final prosthetics. Trigger: when building the U.S. comparator in ws-03-c. Risk: distorted comparison and approval based on mismatched treatment architecture.

If non-clinical costs exceed 20% of the quoted clinical fee and were omitted from the first-pass comparison, invalidate the original result and recalculate — Rationale: travel dentistry cost erosion usually happens outside the operatory. Trigger: immediately after ws-03-b is completed. Risk: understated total cost and approval of a financially weak case.

If expected Costa Rica trip count is unknown, set status to "REQUIRES CLARIFICATION" and suspend deposit authorization — Rationale: one unplanned second trip can erase several thousand dollars of savings. Trigger: when the quote does not clearly state same-trip final delivery versus later prosthetic return. Risk: hidden airfare, lodging, missed-work, and companion cost exposure.

Separate payment friction when foreign transaction fee, wire fee, and exchange spread exceed 3% of the treatment payment amount — Rationale: on a $20,000 to $40,000 case, payment friction can materially change the net result. Trigger: during ws-03-d payment planning. Risk: buried fees that falsely preserve an apparent savings margin.

Flag the case as high exposure if more than 25% of the projected Costa Rica total becomes nonrefundable before records acceptance and written preliminary treatment pathway confirmation — Rationale: early financial lock-in before scope confirmation creates sunk-cost pressure. Trigger: before any deposit release. Risk: forced continuation after scope expansion or travel-plan change.

Do not approve final commitment unless the complication reserve can cover at least one emergency return flight plus three hotel nights and local transport — Rationale: warranty language does not pay for logistics. Trigger: when completing ws-03-e. Risk: delayed complication response, unstable return, or inability to access follow-up care.

If modeled Costa Rica total is higher than the U.S. comparator and no written strategic justification is accepted, reject on cost control grounds — Rationale: higher-cost international care needs an explicit operational reason. Trigger: after ws-03-c and ws-03-e are complete. Risk: accepting more travel burden and continuity risk for no validated benefit.

decision_guide.decision: Approve or hold initial financial modeling
decision_guide.if_condition: The Cavallini quote has been captured in ws-03-a, all major categories are identifiable, and the quote is tied to actual records rather than a marketing estimate or verbal approximation.
decision_guide.then_action: Proceed to ws-03-b and ws-03-c for all-in modeling and U.S. comparison.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark financial status as "incomplete for commitment" and stop deposit, flight, and lodging decisions.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The quote does not specify implant brand, prosthetic scope, sedation assumptions, temporaries versus finals, or trip architecture.

decision_guide.if_condition: The U.S. comparator matches treatment intent on implant or crown scope, arch count, sedation assumptions, provisional and final prosthetic inclusions, and major surgical categories.
decision_guide.then_action: Use ws-03-c to compute net cost difference and continue to reserve testing.
decision_guide.else_action: Invalidate the comparator and obtain a corrected U.S. quote before approval.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Either side is missing major categories such as final prosthetics, sedation, extractions, bone grafting, or follow-up assumptions.

decision_guide.decision: Test whether non-clinical cost burden changes the conclusion
decision_guide.if_condition: All Costa Rica non-clinical costs have been entered in ws-03-b and total less than or equal to 20% above the quoted clinical fee assumptions already reviewed.
decision_guide.then_action: Continue to ws-03-d and ws-03-e using the revised total as the working model.
decision_guide.else_action: Recalculate the entire comparison and reopen approval status.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Non-clinical costs exceed 20% of quoted clinical fee or materially change the net savings result.

decision_guide.if_condition: ws-03-d shows deposit amount, due date, refund trigger, accepted payment method, fee exposure, and backup payment rail, and no more than 25% of projected Costa Rica total is nonrefundable before records-based treatment confirmation.
decision_guide.then_action: Deposit release may be considered after reserve approval.
decision_guide.else_action: Hold payment and request clarification or revised timing from the clinic.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The deposit becomes nonrefundable before records acceptance, before written preliminary treatment pathway confirmation, or before the patient can fund the complication reserve.

decision_guide.if_condition: The dedicated reserve is funded in accessible cash and is at least equal to one emergency return flight plus three hotel nights and local transport.
decision_guide.then_action: Record reserve status as adequate in ws-03-e and finalize financial approval if other conditions are met.
decision_guide.else_action: Classify the case as "financially underprepared" and defer commitment.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The treatment plan includes full-arch work, IV sedation, multiple implants, or staged prosthetics and the reserve still does not cover baseline disruption costs.

decision_guide.if_condition: Costa Rica remains lower-cost after all travel, recovery, payment, and reserve costs, or a higher total is accepted with written strategic justification such as onsite lab coordination, onsite pharmacy access, integrated lodging proximity, or scheduling speed.
decision_guide.then_action: Approve ws-03-e as either "approved net savings" or "approved justified overrun" and release the handoff package to travel readiness.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark the case "rejected on cost control grounds" and stop the Costa Rica pathway.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Modeled savings are positive but lower than the reserve amount, indicating a financially fragile case requiring explicit patient sign-off.

trigger_blocks: Trigger: Cavallini sends a bundled quote with limited line-item detail. Immediate response: use ws-03-a to break the quote into surgery, prosthetics, sedation, imaging, medications, follow-up visits, and any hotel or transfer inclusions before comparing costs.
Trigger: The U.S. quote arrives with missing categories. Immediate response: mark missing items in ws-03-c and do not use the quote as a benchmark until clarified.

Trigger: ws-03-b shows companion travel is required because IV sedation or immediate post-op support is planned. Immediate response: re-run the all-in Costa Rica total with companion airfare, lodging, meals, and missed-work exposure included.
Trigger: The quote does not state whether final prosthetics are same-trip or second-trip. Immediate response: assign financial status "REQUIRES CLARIFICATION" and suspend deposit authorization.
Trigger: Payment method fees exceed 3% of treatment payment amount. Immediate response: create a separate payment-friction line in ws-03-d and update the net savings calculation.
Trigger: Booking deadlines arrive before reserve and payment planning are finalized. Immediate response: do not accelerate booking; finish ws-03-d and ws-03-e first.
Trigger: Treatment scope changes after in-person exam in Costa Rica. Immediate response: freeze further payments and rerun ws-03-a through ws-03-e before approving continuation.
risk_blocks: Risk: Apparent savings disappear after flights, lodging, food, transport, and missed work are added. Mitigation: require ws-03-b completion before any savings language or booking approval.

Risk: Like-for-like comparison is false because U.S. and Costa Rica quotes include different prosthetic or surgical categories. Mitigation: normalize diagnostics, extractions, implant surgery, grafting, abutments, crowns or bridges, provisional prosthesis, final prosthesis, sedation, medications, and trip count in ws-03-c.
Risk: Unknown trip architecture hides a second-trip requirement. Mitigation: do not approve financially until the quote file shows 1-trip, 2-trip, or staged multi-trip treatment architecture.
Risk: Recovery-driven hotel extension increases cost after surgery. Mitigation: model both baseline stay and contingency stay rather than a single lodging number in ws-03-b.

Risk: Deposit becomes sunk cost before records-based treatment confirmation. Mitigation: flag as high exposure if more than 25% of projected total becomes nonrefundable before records acceptance and written preliminary treatment pathway confirmation.

Risk: Payment failure during procedure week delays prosthetic delivery or discharge. Mitigation: use ws-03-d to establish primary and backup payment rails at least 7 business days before travel and verify bank limits and fraud settings.

Risk: Warranty is mistaken for a full financial backstop. Mitigation: treat reserve funding as mandatory because warranty terms do not automatically pay for return airfare, U.S. triage, hotel extensions, or missed work.

Risk: Positive savings are too small to survive one moderate disruption. Mitigation: if modeled savings are less than the complication reserve, classify the case as financially fragile and require explicit sign-off before travel readiness begins.
output_summaries: All-in Costa Rica treatment budget: the full modeled cost of choosing Cavallini, including clinical and non-clinical spending, used as the primary financial control number for commitment decisions.
Comparable U.S. treatment cost worksheet: the normalized benchmark that prevents false savings claims based on mismatched scope or incomplete domestic estimates.

Net savings or justified-overrun decision sheet: the formal yes/no decision record showing whether Costa Rica is cheaper after all modeled costs or is being accepted for a documented strategic reason despite higher cost.
Deposit and payment calendar: the timing map for cash outflow, refund exposure, foreign transaction handling, and backup payment readiness before travel execution.

Complication contingency reserve amount: the dedicated accessible buffer required to absorb hotel extensions, emergency transport, U.S. triage, or return-trip disruption without destabilizing the case.
