cascade_triggers: When ws-04-c shows appointment-to-flight compatibility and ws-04-b shows travel and escort compliance complete, trigger appointment-sequenced treatment execution in Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management.

When confirmed arrival date, lodging location, escort status if applicable, payment readiness, and local routing are documented, trigger patient intake and procedure-day continuity setup in Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management.

When companion coverage and recovery supplies are confirmed for a sedation or surgery case, trigger discharge-day monitoring preparation in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance.

When a disruption alters arrival date, lodging location, escort coverage, or transport within 48 hours of pre-op, surgery, or discharge, trigger same-day logistics update and exception handling in Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management.

When the patient has usable lodging, post-discharge transport, medication access, communication tools, and a flexible extension path, trigger recovery-environment preparation in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance.
chapters.scenario_scene: Angela, a 58-year-old U.S. patient scheduled for implant surgery with IV sedation, finds a flight arriving in San José at 2:55 p.m. for a 5:00 p.m. pre-op and wants to save $380 by booking it. In ws-04-c, she tests the itinerary against a four-hour disruption rule and sees that one immigration delay would erase the appointment, so she shifts to an arrival the prior evening and adds one hotel night near the clinic. Her companion then confirms escort coverage in ws-04-b with phone number and hotel details, and ws-04-d assigns airport pickup, clinic transport, and post-sedation return routing. The result is a SEDATION-COMPATIBLE ITINERARY CONFIRMED status and no same-week rebooking costs.
success_metrics: 100% completion of ws-04-a through ws-04-e before departure approval is issued.
0 nonrefundable flight or lodging bookings made before a date-linked clinical sequence is documented.
Inbound itinerary preserves at least one full disruption buffer before the first clinic event, with no single delay of 4 hours or less causing loss of that event.
100% of sedation cases have a named escort confirmed no later than 24 hours before the scheduled procedure.
Patient can execute the first 24 hours in San José using carry-on contents alone if checked baggage is delayed.
0 departures to Costa Rica with PAYMENT FRAGILE status at the time of final pre-travel review.
chapters.detailed_explanation:
This chapter exists to preserve sequence integrity between an approved treatment plan and actual arrival in San José. Its core function is not travel convenience; it is operational compatibility with Cavallini's appointment structure, onsite lab dependencies, sedation model, and local recovery requirements. That is why the first gate is not airfare price. The first gate is whether the clinic sequence is stable enough to support booking decisions.

The chapter should be read as a dependency-control layer. Flights, lodging, and transport are not independent consumer choices here. They are support systems for pre-op review, surgery timing, pharmacy access, discharge movement, and post-op observation. A cheap ticket that compresses those events is not a savings event; it is a treatment-risk event. The same is true of a short hotel stay that assumes the fastest possible recovery instead of the longest likely one for implants, extractions, or All-on-X work.

Passport and entry readiness come first because unresolved document issues invalidate every later booking decision. The required standard is simple: the patient must be able to leave the United States, enter Costa Rica, and board the return itinerary without last-minute remediation. If that cannot be established cleanly, trip execution stops. The manual treats early clinic rescheduling as a smaller loss than gambling on document turnaround inside 45 days.

The flight planner is the most important timing tool in the chapter because it forces the patient to test real transportation conditions against real clinical events. The worksheet should not only list departure and arrival times. It should distinguish chair-time events from clinically consequential events such as pre-op review, sedation preparation, surgery, provisional delivery, bite checks, medication pickup, and discharge clearance. If the itinerary can be broken by a modest delay, it is operationally weak even if the airline considers it legal.

Lodging is part of the recovery environment, not a generic reservation. Near-clinic lodging is preferred because it reduces transfer friction, shortens post-discharge movement, and supports follow-up attendance if swelling, provisional adjustment, or medication issues arise. The right property is the one that supports elevator access if needed, communication reliability, soft-food access, predictable routing, and extension options. For this system, the hotel is part of treatment continuity.

Escort planning is separated because sedation creates a different discharge model. If IV sedation or major same-day restorative work is involved, the patient cannot rely on an informal or aspirational plan. A named person, with contact details and physical availability, must exist before the itinerary is treated as compliant. This is one of the chapter's non-waivable dependencies. If escort coverage collapses, the travel plan is no longer execution-ready until the clinic confirms a revised approach.

Payment readiness is included here because payment failure behaves like a travel failure on treatment day. A patient who arrives with only one card and no tested backup may lose the procedure slot even if every other travel detail is perfect. For that reason, payment instruments belong in the same readiness review as flights, lodging, and packing. The operational standard is two usable paths, not one hopeful path.

Packing closes the chapter because it converts planning into survivability. The patient must be able to land, check in, attend intake, pay charges, manage medications, and get through the first recovery night using carry-on items alone. That standard protects against one of the most common travel disruptions: delayed checked baggage. If a delayed bag can derail clinic attendance, the packing plan is incomplete.

The handoff standard at chapter close is strict by design. This domain is complete only when all five worksheets are finished, the itinerary is robust, the recovery stay is sufficient, local routing is assigned, escort compliance is documented when required, payment methods are viable, and the first 24 hours are executable without checked baggage. Only then can the status move to TRIP READY FOR APPOINTMENT-SEQUENCED TREATMENT EXECUTION. Anything less is apparent readiness, not controlled readiness.
chapters.narrative:
## Orientation Snapshot
This chapter converts an approved Costa Rica treatment decision into a controlled travel operation. The objective is not to buy the cheapest ticket or fill a suitcase; it is to arrive in San José on the clinic’s usable timeline, with legal entry readiness, payment readiness, escort coverage if required, and enough local recovery infrastructure to complete treatment without compressing post-op stability. In this domain, a scheduling error is not a minor inconvenience. It can force treatment delay, increase hotel nights, break sedation eligibility, or push a patient onto a return flight before the clinic has confirmed safe travel.

The controlling principle is sequence integrity. Flights, lodging, and ground transport are downstream of the confirmed clinical sequence, not the other way around. If the appointment structure is still unstable, travel booking is premature. The common failure pattern is simple: a patient sees a low airfare, books around it, and then discovers that pre-op review, surgery timing, onsite lab steps, or IV sedation discharge requirements do not fit the flight windows.

The minimum required outputs of this chapter are five completed operational artifacts: ws-04-a Costa Rica Surgical Trip Logistics Inventory for Flights, Lodging, Transfers, and Escort Coverage; ws-04-b Passport Validity, Entry Document, and Sedation-Day Escort Compliance Checklist for Costa Rica Dental Travel; ws-04-c Cavallini Appointment-to-Flight Timing Planner; ws-04-d San José Post-Procedure Lodging and Ground Transport Routing Form; and ws-04-e Major Restorative Dental Travel Packing and Payment Instrument Preparation Sheet. If any one of these remains incomplete, the trip is not operationally ready.

Decision threshold: if Cavallini has not provided a date-linked appointment sequence with at least a pre-op attendance window, procedure date, and expected local recovery duration, then do not lock nonrefundable airfare or lodging. Output if blocked: mark travel status as HOLD FOR CLINICAL SEQUENCE and escalate back to Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management scheduling coordination. Downstream effect: no trip readiness approval can be issued.

## Immediate Execution Path
Start with legal travel readiness because every later booking depends on it. Confirm passport possession, expiration date, name match against airline booking name, and the ability to re-enter the United States on the planned return date. The chapter does not extend into immigration theory; it requires a simple operational determination: the patient must hold the documents needed to depart the U.S., enter Costa Rica, and board the return itinerary without same-week remediation.

If the passport is expired, missing, materially damaged, or cannot support the planned travel window, then trip execution stops immediately and no airfare should be purchased. If passport validity or entry readiness is uncertain within 45 days of intended departure, escalate to expedited document resolution and move the treatment date rather than gambling on document turnaround. The downstream effect is cost protection: changing a clinic date early is usually less damaging than losing airfare, hotel deposits, and procedure scheduling continuity.

Next, build ws-04-c by mapping the clinical sequence against transportation reality. Anchor the planner with the pre-op appointment date and time, procedure date and estimated duration, any expected onsite lab dependency, medication pickup timing, and the clinic’s guidance on how long the patient must remain local after surgery or prosthetic delivery. Then only select flights that create margin before the first appointment and margin after the last clinically significant event.

A practical control rule applies here: for major restorative cases such as implant placement, multiple extractions with provisionals, or All-on-X, same-day international arrival followed by immediate chair time should be treated as a high-risk compression pattern unless Cavallini has explicitly structured it that way. Arrival delay, lost baggage, dehydration, and fatigue are common destabilizers. If the arrival flight lands too late to absorb delay and still reach the pre-op appointment comfortably, reject the itinerary even if the fare is favorable.

Decision threshold: if scheduled arrival in San José provides less than one full disruption buffer between touchdown and the first required clinic event, then rebook to an earlier arrival or add a pre-treatment hotel night. If the treatment plan includes IV sedation, then escort availability becomes a gating condition, not a preference. Output: either SEDATION-COMPATIBLE ITINERARY CONFIRMED or SEDATION ITINERARY NONCOMPLIANT.

After flight logic, lock lodging based on recovery function. The default preference within this system is Cavallini-related onsite hotel lodging or equivalent near-clinic lodging because proximity reduces transfer complexity, allows easier attendance for follow-up checks, and shortens the distance between surgery discharge and bed rest. Lodging selection must be evaluated for elevator access if mobility may be limited, food availability compatible with soft-diet recovery, reliable communication access, and predictable transfer time to the clinic.

Do not treat hotel nights as a generic travel reservation. They are part of the post-op control environment. If surgery is scheduled on Day 2 and the clinic expects observation, medication use, swelling management, and follow-up review through Day 5, then booking departure on Day 3 to save two hotel nights is not thrift; it is schedule failure. The stay must cover the longest likely local recovery period contemplated by the current treatment phase, not the shortest hoped-for recovery.

Then complete ws-04-d and assign every local movement in advance: airport to hotel, hotel to clinic, clinic to pharmacy if needed, clinic to hotel after sedation, and backup transport if the primary method fails. The system should identify whether the clinic, hotel, private driver, rideshare, or taxi fills each segment. For major surgery days, discharge transportation cannot remain undefined because the patient may be groggy, swollen, unable to navigate payment, or ineligible to travel alone.

Final immediate step: build ws-04-e and pack for treatment execution, not leisure travel. Required categories include passport and travel documents, records copies if needed, medication list, current medications in original containers where appropriate, payment instruments, chargers, post-op comfort items, and clothing that does not interfere with swelling or discharge transport. Packing is complete only when the patient could land, attend clinic, pay required charges, manage first-night recovery, and respond to a lost-luggage event using carry-on contents alone.

## Deep Operational Guidance
This domain sits between financial approval and treatment execution, so its real function is preserving the assumptions made upstream. Domain-03 may show that Costa Rica treatment produces net savings, but those savings evaporate quickly if travel execution creates rebooking fees, extra nights, missed-work extensions, or a second international trip caused by avoidable scheduling compression. The travel plan therefore has to be built as a clinical support system, not a tourism itinerary.

The first dependency is on the degree of treatment intensity. A single crown sequence, a multi-unit implant case, and a full-arch All-on-X procedure do not tolerate the same travel posture. Full-mouth and implant surgery cases require wider timing buffers because swelling, bleeding, anesthesia recovery, prosthetic adjustments, and dietary limitations can alter the patient’s ability to move through airports or tolerate same-week departures. This is why ws-04-c should distinguish between chair-time events and clinically consequential events. The latter category includes pre-op review, sedation preparation, surgery, provisional delivery, bite check, medication reconciliation, and discharge clearance.

Use exact timing logic rather than optimism. International air travel is vulnerable to delay at four points: origin departure, connection misalignment, immigration and customs processing, and local ground transfer into San José. A 90-minute airline connection may be legal while still being operationally fragile. Likewise, a flight landing three hours before a pre-op appointment may appear sufficient until baggage delay, traffic, and fatigue erase the margin. If any single delay would cause a missed clinic event, the itinerary does not contain enough slack.

Decision threshold: if any flight change, missed connection, or baggage delay of four hours or less would cause loss of the first clinic appointment, then the itinerary fails robustness review and must be rebuilt. Downstream effect: appointment confirmation should be withheld until a robust itinerary is documented in ws-04-c. This is especially important if the clinic’s onsite lab or specialist schedule is synchronized to the patient’s attendance window.

Escort planning is a separate control path because IV sedation changes the discharge model. When IV sedation, major implant surgery, or extensive same-day prosthetic work is in scope, the patient needs a named escort with contact information, physical availability on procedure day, and lodging alignment for the first recovery phase. A vague plan such as “I can probably get someone” is not compliant. The escort must be reachable, understand the discharge role, and be available during the first transport from clinic to lodging.

If no escort is available for a sedation case, then either the treatment plan must be restructured to remove the escort-dependent element or the trip must not proceed as scheduled. The effect is immediate: ws-04-b remains open, travel readiness remains unapproved, and Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management cannot assume sedation-day continuity. This chapter is not authorized to waive that dependency.

Payment readiness also belongs in travel readiness because the procedure may depend on deposit completion, card operability, or split payment timing. The patient should travel with at least two usable payment methods, awareness of foreign transaction behavior, and a pre-checked method for large medical charges. A common failure mode is arriving with a single card that triggers fraud controls abroad, leaving the patient operationally stranded before treatment. That is not a financial domain abstraction; it is a same-day treatment blocker.

Decision threshold: if the patient cannot demonstrate at least one primary and one backup payment path that can cover expected trip-phase charges, then treatment-day status is PAYMENT FRAGILE and departure should be delayed until resolved. Output required in ws-04-e: named primary payment instrument, backup instrument, billing contact path, and card issuer notification status if applicable. Downstream effect: preserves appointment viability and avoids clinic-side payment disputes on procedure day.

Packing must be organized around continuity, not convenience. Carry-on contents should include passport, records summary, medication list, essential medications, payment instruments, phone power, and first 24-hour recovery essentials. Checked baggage can contain redundant clothing and low-consequence supplies, but any item required to attend treatment or survive the first recovery night belongs in carry-on. Lost luggage is inconvenient for a beach trip; it is destabilizing when you have surgery scheduled within 24 hours.

Local routing in San José should minimize unknowns. If using Cavallini-related onsite hotels or affiliated arrangements, document exactly how transfer coordination occurs, where pickup happens, and who confirms timing. If using independent lodging, document the route to the clinic, average travel time by time of day, emergency fallback transport, and whether the escort can navigate the route without the patient’s input. After surgery, cognitive load may be too impaired for improvisation.

Finally, set a departure decision rule before the trip begins. Return travel after major restorative treatment should occur only after the patient has medications in hand, understands the discharge instructions, can tolerate oral intake to the degree expected, has a documented mechanism to contact the clinic, and has not been told to remain local longer. This chapter cannot create medical clearance, but it can require that commercial travel not be treated as automatic because the airline date arrived.

## Failure Dynamics and Recovery
Failure scenario 1: the patient books a discount ticket 21 days before travel because it saves $380, but the arrival lands at 3:40 p.m. and the clinic’s pre-op review is at 5:00 p.m. A 70-minute immigration queue plus traffic into San José causes a missed pre-op, forcing the surgery to move by one day. Consequence: one extra hotel night, one extra missed workday, one rescheduled driver transfer, and potentially a specialist or lab sequence disruption. Recovery action: contact the clinic before boarding the originating flight if delay is known, convert the appointment sequence immediately, and document all revised dates in ws-04-c and ws-04-d before departure from the airport.

Failure scenario 2: the patient plans IV sedation for implant surgery but arrives without confirmed escort coverage because a companion cancels 48 hours before departure. The clinic may refuse to discharge the patient unaccompanied after sedation or may require treatment-plan modification. Consequence: same-day sedation cancellation, procedure delay, additional lodging expense, and possible loss of synchronized surgery time. Recovery action: attempt named substitute escort confirmation before boarding; if unavailable, escalate to the clinic to determine whether the procedure can be restructured without IV sedation or must be postponed. Threshold: if no qualified escort is confirmed by 24 hours before scheduled sedation, treat the itinerary as noncompliant.

Failure scenario 3: the patient books only two recovery nights after a full-arch surgery because the original quote emphasized savings. On post-op Day 2, swelling, medication timing, and provisional adjustment needs make airport transit unrealistic, and the clinic requests an additional local review. Consequence: same-week airfare change fees, additional hotel nights, local transport changes, and elevated travel stress during active recovery. Recovery action: always build a lodging reserve window into ws-04-a and do not fully optimize to the minimum anticipated stay. Decision rule: if the treatment phase involves multiple extractions, immediate load provisionals, or extensive implant placement, carry at least a flexible extension plan for lodging and return flights.

Failure scenario 4: the patient arrives with one primary credit card for a five-figure treatment installment, and the issuer declines the transaction as suspicious foreign activity. Surgery is scheduled for the next morning, and resolution through the bank’s fraud team takes 12 hours. Consequence: payment friction can jeopardize the procedure slot and increase stress on the eve of surgery. Recovery action: pre-notify issuers, carry a backup instrument, and test access to issuer support from Costa Rica before travel. Threshold: if the backup method cannot independently cover at least the near-term charges due on arrival or procedure day, payment readiness is incomplete.

Failure scenario 5: the patient packs medications, records notes, and payment cards in checked luggage, which is delayed overnight. The patient can physically arrive at the hotel but cannot verify medication interactions, settle the room smoothly, or present backup documentation at clinic intake the next morning. Consequence: intake friction, possible medication reconciliation delay, and heightened risk if the patient has chronic prescriptions. Recovery action: reassign all mission-critical items to carry-on and maintain a digital duplicate of key documents accessible without checked baggage.

The recovery pattern for this domain is always the same: detect the failed dependency early, revise the schedule before it breaks the clinic sequence, and update the worksheets rather than carrying stale assumptions forward. A disrupted itinerary is manageable; an undisclosed disruption that reaches surgery day is what causes compounding loss. If a failure changes arrival date, escort coverage, lodging location, or payment ability, then the clinic must be informed as an operational update, not merely as courtesy travel chatter.

Decision threshold: if a disruption alters any event within 48 hours of pre-op, surgery, or discharge transport, then same-day communication to the clinic is mandatory and ws-04-a through ws-04-d must be revised before the next control point. Downstream effect: protects Onsite Treatment Execution from relying on invalid logistics assumptions. This is the chapter’s escalation trigger for unstable travel conditions.

Closure criteria for failure recovery are strict. The issue is considered operationally recovered only when the revised itinerary, revised lodging, revised escort status if applicable, and revised payment or transport plan are all documented and acknowledged by the patient as executable. If the revision creates a new mismatch with the clinic sequence, then recovery is incomplete and travel readiness reverts to HOLD.

## Cross-Domain Handoffs
This chapter receives its authority from earlier domains and hands clean inputs to later ones. From US Diagnostic Record Collection and Cross-Border Treatment Planning Transfer, it assumes that Cavallini has enough accepted records to support preliminary treatment planning. From US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control, it assumes the travel budget, payment calendar, and contingency reserve have already been approved. If either upstream condition is false, then this chapter can organize logistics but cannot certify trip readiness.

The primary outbound handoff goes to Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management. That domain needs a confirmed arrival date, lodging location, escort status if sedation is involved, payment readiness, local routing plan, and a patient who can physically present with documents and medications on the expected timeline. The handoff is successful only when ws-04-c shows appointment-to-flight compatibility and ws-04-b shows legal travel and escort compliance complete.

A second outbound handoff goes to Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance. That team of actions depends on the patient already having a functioning recovery environment: usable lodging, post-discharge transport, medication access, communication tools, and a plan for extending the stay if recovery runs longer than expected. If this chapter underbuilds recovery logistics, the recovery domain inherits preventable instability and may be forced into reactive problem-solving after surgery.

Decision threshold: if companion coverage and recovery supplies are not confirmed for a sedation or surgery case, then the handoff to Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance is blocked. Output: RECOVERY ENVIRONMENT INCOMPLETE. Downstream effect: discharge-day monitoring preparation cannot be treated as ready.

Operational completion for this chapter is reached only when all of the following are true: ws-04-a through ws-04-e are complete; passport and travel document readiness are confirmed; flights are aligned to the clinic sequence with disruption margin; lodging covers the expected treatment and recovery window; local transfers including sedation-day discharge transport are assigned; escort coverage is documented when required; payment instruments and backups are validated; and the patient can execute the first 24 hours in San José even if checked baggage is lost. At that point, the domain status can be set to TRIP READY FOR APPOINTMENT-SEQUENCED TREATMENT EXECUTION.

If any one of those conditions is missing, this chapter is not complete, even if flights are booked and the suitcase is packed. The system recognizes only controlled readiness, not apparent readiness. The purpose of this domain is to ensure that when the patient leaves the United States, they are traveling toward a viable treatment event rather than toward an expensive scheduling gamble.

chapters.chapter_title: Procedure-Day Identity Verification, Sedation Control, Implant/Prosthetic Specification Capture, and Discharge Handoff

chapter_opener.what_this_is_for: This chapter converts a scheduled Cavallini treatment day into a controlled, documented clinical event so identity, scope, sedation, materials, medications, and discharge records are locked before facility departure.
chapter_opener.when_it_matters: It applies from clinic arrival through final discharge on any day involving extractions, implants, crowns, bridges, veneers, full-arch work, temporaries, finals, grafting, or IV sedation after travel readiness has already been confirmed.
chapter_opener.failure_looks_like: The patient leaves the clinic or onsite hotel route with only verbal instructions, no verified record of what was actually done, missing implant or prosthetic details, unclear medication timing, or undocumented same-day changes from the pre-trip plan.
produces: Complete procedure-day record including actual treatment performed, variances, sedation pathway, medications, and discharge contents
Transfer-ready implant/prosthetic and post-op documentation for Costa Rica recovery monitoring and later U.S. follow-up
do_first: Open ws-05-a before any pre-medication, anxiolytic discussion, or consent finalization and match the day-of-treatment scope to the prior written plan
Confirm patient identity, procedure type, operative sites, sedation pathway, and escort status before any irreversible step starts

minimum_viable_actions: Verify full name, date of birth, and scheduled procedure against passport or matching government ID at clinic check-in.
Compare the day-of-treatment plan to the prior written treatment pathway and document any change in ws-05-a before consent or sedation.
If IV sedation or another impairment-producing pathway is planned, complete ws-05-b with escort name, mobile number, discharge route, and first-night supervision plan before sedation starts.
Capture implant brand, sites, quantities, and prosthetic status in ws-05-c while staff and labels are still available onsite.
Log every medication, first dose timing, interval, food restriction, and urgent symptom threshold in ws-05-d before leaving the clinic area.
Use ws-05-e to physically verify receipt of the discharge packet, urgent contact route, and next in-country follow-up step before transport to lodging.
quick_reference_rules: Do not allow consent to convert into procedure start until the clinic's day-of-treatment scope matches the accepted written plan or the variance is documented in ws-05-a — Rationale: cross-border restorative care often changes at the point of examination, and undocumented scope drift becomes irreversible fast. Trigger: any difference in tooth count, arch, extraction count, implant count, prosthetic type, sedation method, cost, or stay length. Risk: unauthorized treatment expansion, budget overrun, and recovery planning failure.

Open ws-05-a before any anxiolytic or IV sedation discussion — Rationale: recall quality degrades once stress medication or sedation enters the workflow. Trigger: arrival for a major restorative or sedation day. Risk: loss of accurate memory about what was approved versus what was performed.

If same-day changes increase total case cost by more than 10% of the accepted quote or extend stay by more than 2 nights, stop for re-approval before proceeding — Rationale: financial and travel assumptions from prior domains may no longer hold. Trigger: revised treatment plan after imaging or chairside exam. Risk: loss of savings, inadequate funds, missed work expansion, and invalidated recovery logistics.

No IV sedation pathway is discharge-ready without a documented escort who is physically present and reachable at release — Rationale: a sedated patient cannot reliably self-manage transport, instructions, or first-night medication timing. Trigger: any IV sedation or impairment-producing procedure. Risk: unsafe discharge, medication errors, and failed overnight monitoring.

Treat implant and prosthetic identifiers as mandatory discharge data, not optional technical extras — Rationale: U.S. providers often cannot adjust or repair components without exact system information such as Nobel Biocare or Zimmer details. Trigger: any implant placement, immediate load case, abutment placement, full-arch temporary, crown, bridge, or prosthetic delivery. Risk: refusal of stateside care, delayed intervention, and warranty ambiguity.

Convert all medications into first-24-hour clock times before transport to the onsite hotel or other lodging — Rationale: post-op failures often start with missed first doses after sedation or travel fatigue. Trigger: any antibiotic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, rinse, or adjunct medication issued or prescribed. Risk: missed doses, uncontrolled pain, infection-control failure, and avoidable after-hours calls.

Do not treat discharge as complete until ws-05-e confirms written post-op instructions, medication list or prescriptions, procedure summary, urgent contact route, and follow-up timing are in hand or have a written delivery deadline within 24 hours — Rationale: verbal discharge does not support downstream control. Trigger: checkout after surgery or restorative delivery. Risk: broken handoff to recovery monitoring and U.S. follow-up.

If warranty coverage is referenced, request the treatment-specific warranty conditions tied to what was actually delivered that day, especially provisional versus final status — Rationale: warranty protection depends on actual completed work and conditions, not sales language. Trigger: discharge discussion mentioning lifetime warranty or coverage. Risk: denied future coverage and inability to prove delivered status.

decision_guide.if_condition: The clinic's proposed same-day treatment differs materially from the previously accepted written plan in arch, tooth count, extraction count, implant count, implant brand, prosthetic type, sedation method, treatment sequence, cost, or expected recovery duration.
decision_guide.then_action: Pause progression, document the exact revised scope and reason in ws-05-a, and obtain clarified financial and recovery implications before consent or procedure start.
decision_guide.else_action: Proceed with planned consent and treatment while recording the confirmed scope in ws-05-a.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The revision increases total case cost by more than 10% of the accepted quote, extends stay by more than 2 nights, or changes the irreversible treatment sequence.

decision_guide.if_condition: IV sedation or another impairment-producing pathway is planned and a physically present, reachable escort is confirmed with transport and first-night supervision details in ws-05-b.
decision_guide.then_action: Proceed with sedation-day workflow and require discharge instructions to be delivered directly to the escort as well as the patient.
decision_guide.else_action: Hold sedation progression and resolve by companion arrival, clinic-approved change in sedation plan, or case rescheduling.
decision_guide.escalate_when: No escort is present at discharge, the escort cannot be reached, or the patient arrived alone for a sedation-dependent procedure.

decision_guide.if_condition: The clinic states final treatment choices will depend on findings after sedation, extraction, or intraoperative assessment.
decision_guide.then_action: Pre-designate who can authorize changes and limit any expansion to the documented pre-consented boundaries recorded in ws-05-a.
decision_guide.else_action: Keep treatment confined to the fixed documented scope already reviewed before sedation.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Authority for expanded irreversible treatment is unclear, or the patient would be impaired before the decision point occurs.

decision_guide.if_condition: Implant system, sites, component details, restorative type, and provisional versus final status are available from the clinic before discharge.
decision_guide.then_action: Complete ws-05-c onsite and duplicate the information for later U.S. provider transfer.
decision_guide.else_action: Request named staff follow-up and a written commitment for delivery by email or print within 24 hours, then log the gap in ws-05-e.
decision_guide.escalate_when: No written record of implant or prosthetic specifications can be produced before departure or no accountable contact is assigned.

decision_guide.if_condition: Each medication has a name, strength, interval, first-dose status, first-dose time, food instruction, and urgent symptom threshold documented in ws-05-d.
decision_guide.then_action: Convert instructions into a first-24-hour clock schedule and confirm who is responsible for each dose.
decision_guide.else_action: Remain at the clinic or onsite pharmacy interface until instructions are clarified and labels match the written plan.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The first dose timing is unclear, verbal instructions conflict with labels, or the schedule cannot realistically be executed because of sedation, nausea, or transit.

decision_guide.if_condition: Written post-op instructions, medication list or prescriptions, procedure summary, urgent contact route, and in-country follow-up timing are physically received and checked in ws-05-e.
decision_guide.then_action: Approve discharge and transfer copies immediately to the recovery and U.S. follow-up file.
decision_guide.else_action: Classify discharge as incomplete and remain onsite until documents are produced or a documented delivery deadline within 24 hours is assigned.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The clinic cannot provide a written record of what was placed, prescribed, changed, or when urgent contact is needed.

trigger_blocks: Trigger: clinic arrival for a major restorative procedure day -> Response: verify ID, planned procedure, operative sites, and open ws-05-a before any medication or consent finalization.
Trigger: new imaging or oral findings alter same-day feasibility -> Response: record the exact reason, revised scope, and stay/cost impact in ws-05-a and ws-05-e before proceeding.
Trigger: IV sedation is planned -> Response: complete ws-05-b with escort contact, discharge transport, and first-night supervision plan before sedation administration.
Trigger: same-day implant placement, grafting, or full-arch conversion occurs -> Response: capture implant system and prosthetic details in ws-05-c while components, labels, and staff are available.
Trigger: onsite pharmacy issues medications or the clinic provides prescriptions -> Response: complete ws-05-d with first-dose status, clock times, restrictions, and call thresholds before leaving.

Trigger: clinic mentions lifetime warranty during discharge -> Response: request treatment-specific warranty conditions tied to the actual delivered work, especially whether the prosthesis is provisional or final.
Trigger: discharge documents are handed over -> Response: check them against ws-05-e before entering transport to lodging or airport route.

Trigger: the clinic recommends delayed return travel because of swelling, oral intake problems, or procedure severity -> Response: record the instruction in writing and update the recovery and travel plan immediately.
risk_blocks: Risk: wrong patient file or mismatched records enter the day's workflow -> Mitigation: verify full name, date of birth, ID, planned procedure, and operative sites before consent finalization.

Risk: same-day treatment expands beyond the approved plan because the patient feels committed after travel -> Mitigation: use ws-05-a as a scope-lock and force re-approval when cost rises above 10% or stay extends beyond 2 nights.
Risk: sedated patient cannot accurately retain discharge instructions -> Mitigation: complete ws-05-b before sedation and require direct handoff of instructions to the escort.

Risk: U.S. dentist cannot intervene because implant or component details are missing -> Mitigation: complete ws-05-c onsite and do not leave without written brand and component capture or a named 24-hour delivery commitment.

Risk: missed antibiotic or pain-medication doses in the first 12 to 24 hours -> Mitigation: convert written instructions into exact clock times in ws-05-d and confirm who administers each first-night dose.

Risk: patient departs Costa Rica with an incomplete discharge packet -> Mitigation: use ws-05-e as a physical receipt check and classify discharge as incomplete until required documents are received or deadline-tracked.

Risk: recovery monitoring starts with the wrong severity assumptions because extractions, grafting, or additional implants were added -> Mitigation: export the actual performed procedure and revised travel restrictions to the next domain the same day.

Risk: warranty expectations are based on marketing language instead of actual delivered status -> Mitigation: request treatment-specific warranty terms tied to the real procedure, materials, and provisional/final designation.
output_summaries: ws-05-a completed procedure-day treatment scope and material verification record — establishes what was actually performed and whether the treatment remained within the approved plan.
ws-05-b sedation consent, escort, and discharge readiness record — proves whether release conditions were met for IV sedation or other impairment-producing care.
ws-05-c implant system, abutment, and prosthetic specification capture — enables later U.S. intervention, component compatibility checks, and warranty review.
ws-05-d medication and post-op instruction log with first-24-hour schedule — feeds immediate recovery monitoring and reduces first-night dosing errors.
ws-05-e discharge packet receipt and missing-document resolution form — confirms whether the handoff package is usable or still operationally open.
Documented urgent contact route and next in-country follow-up timing — allows the recovery domain to respond quickly if bleeding, swelling, pain, or medication problems escalate.
Duplicated procedure-day records and discharge documents — creates a backup set for later U.S. provider transfer and warranty preservation.

worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use at check-in and again before consent finalization whenever the clinic confirms or revises the day's planned work.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks procedure start only after the actual scope, same-day changes, and cost/recovery implications are documented.

worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks safe sedation-day execution and discharge planning by confirming escort, transport, and first-night supervision.

worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks U.S. follow-up continuity, compatibility checks, emergency adjustment planning, and warranty documentation.

worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It unblocks first-24-hour recovery execution by converting verbal discharge advice into an actionable medication and symptom plan.

cascade_triggers: When ws-05-d and ws-05-e confirm medication instructions, urgent contact route, and follow-up timing, trigger symptom and medication monitoring in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance.

When ws-05-c captures implant system and prosthetic specification details, trigger U.S. provider handoff preparation in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When same-day treatment expands beyond the accepted plan and changes stay length or procedure severity, trigger immediate plan revision in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance.
When the cost delta from same-day changes exceeds 10% or a stay extension exceeds 2 nights, trigger re-entry review in US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control.
When escort status, lodging needs, or discharge transport assumptions change on sedation day, trigger immediate updates in Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness.

When the discharge packet is incomplete but has a written 24-hour delivery commitment, trigger priority follow-up tasking in both Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance and U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.
chapters.scenario_scene: At 7:30 a.m., Lisa arrives at Cavallini with her brother for a planned 4-implant upper-arch procedure under IV sedation and opens ws-05-a before pre-medication. New imaging leads the team to propose 6 implants plus grafting, which adds 3 hotel nights and raises the quote by 14%, so the case is paused and the revision is documented before consent. By 3:15 p.m., ws-05-b confirms the brother's escort role, ws-05-c records the implant brand and sites, and ws-05-d converts the first antibiotic and pain doses into exact clock times through the night. Lisa leaves for the onsite hotel only after ws-05-e confirms written instructions, urgent contact information, and the next-day follow-up appointment, reducing the next-domain handoff from guesswork to a same-day documented transfer.
success_metrics: 100% of treatment days have ws-05-a completed before sedation or irreversible treatment start.
0 discharges after IV sedation without a documented escort name, mobile number, and first-night supervision plan in ws-05-b.
100% of implant or full-arch cases leave with implant/prosthetic brand and status captured in ws-05-c or a named written 24-hour delivery commitment.
100% of medications issued on procedure day are converted into first-24-hour clock times in ws-05-d before transport to lodging.
0 clinic departures classified as operationally complete unless ws-05-e confirms receipt of written post-op instructions, urgent contact route, and follow-up timing.
Any same-day increase above 10% of accepted quote or more than 2 additional hotel nights is escalated before procedure continuation.
chapters.detailed_explanation:
This chapter exists because treatment execution is the point where planning stops being provisional and becomes irreversible. Upstream domains have already handled clinic evaluation, records transfer, cost modeling, and travel readiness. On procedure day, the control problem narrows: make sure the work done at Cavallini is exactly identified, properly consented, technically traceable, and transferred into a usable record before the patient becomes impaired, fatigued, or transported away.

The core operating principle is that memory is not an acceptable procedure record. A patient can be anxious, medicated, sedated, swollen, in pain, or focused only on leaving the chair. That is why ws-05-a must open before pre-medication and why identity, scope, and branch conditions have to be locked early. If the clinic says treatment depends on bone quality, extraction findings, primary stability, or same-day lab feasibility, those conditional paths must be written down before the patient loses decision clarity.

Material same-day change control is the chapter's main decision gate. A revision in implant count, arch, prosthetic type, grafting, sedation method, or expected recovery length is not a minor update. It changes financial exposure, hotel use, flight pressure, companion burden, and post-op risk. The 10% cost and 2-night stay thresholds exist to force a deliberate pause before momentum and sunk-cost pressure turn a revised treatment concept into an unreviewed irreversible event.

Sedation control is not only a medical safety issue; it is a documentation preservation issue. Once IV sedation or a similar pathway begins, the patient should be assumed unreliable as the sole recorder of instructions. That makes the escort an operational role with defined duties: remain reachable, receive instructions directly, confirm transport, oversee first dosing, and monitor the first night. If instructions are verbal only or given only to the sedated patient, the handoff is incomplete and must be corrected before discharge.

Implant and prosthetic specification capture deserves special emphasis because it determines whether later intervention is possible. For an implant case, brand alone is not enough if other details are available; site, quantity, and component information matter. For a full-arch case, the record must distinguish between provisional conversion work and a final prosthesis. For crown and bridge treatment, the useful record is the actual restorative scope, material type if provided, and retention status if applicable. Without that level of capture, a U.S. dentist may be unable or unwilling to touch the case.

Medication logging must also move from general advice to executable instructions. The patient and escort need names, strengths, intervals, first-dose timing, food rules, oral hygiene restrictions, and symptom thresholds that trigger contact with the clinic. Converting the first 24 hours into clock times is a control step, not a convenience feature. It addresses the exact failure mode where a patient assumes the clinic already gave a first dose or sleeps through a critical interval after sedation.

Discharge completion is the final control gate. The patient should not judge the day complete just because the dental work is finished. Operational completion occurs only when the documents, medication plan, urgent contact route, follow-up timing, and specification records are physically in hand or tracked with a named 24-hour resolution path. That packet is what allows the next chapter to monitor recovery correctly and allows the later U.S. follow-up domain to preserve treatment continuity and warranty support.
chapters.narrative:
## Orientation Snapshot
This chapter begins when Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness has already produced a confirmed arrival, lodging, payment instrument, escort arrangement if needed, and a scheduled Cavallini clinic appointment. Its job is narrower and more critical: convert a planned restorative case into a documented clinical event without losing control of identity, consent, sedation readiness, material selection, same-day changes, or discharge data. The operating risk is not only clinical performance; it is information loss at the exact point where the treatment becomes irreversible.

At Cavallini, the operational advantage of onsite hotel access, onsite lab capability, onsite pharmacy support, IV sedation availability, and named implant systems such as Nobel Biocare or Zimmer can reduce friction only if those assets are tied to a documented handoff. A patient who receives extractions, implants, temporaries, crowns, or full-arch work and then leaves with only verbal instructions is not in a controlled system. That failure propagates immediately into Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance and later into U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

The primary output of this chapter is a complete procedure-day record, not a memory of what happened. That record must capture the actual teeth treated, the final scope performed, sedation pathway used, medications dispensed or prescribed, implant and prosthetic identifiers, and the discharge packet contents. If any of those elements are missing before facility departure, the case remains operationally open and the patient should not treat the visit as complete.

Decision point: if the day-of-treatment plan presented at the clinic differs materially from the written preliminary plan accepted before travel, pause progression until the variance is explained and documented. Material means any change in arch, tooth count, extraction count, implant count, implant brand, prosthetic type, sedation method, treatment sequence, cost, or expected recovery duration. Downstream effect: no final consent, no sedation, and no procedure start until ws-05-a records the revised scope and associated financial and recovery implications.

## Immediate Execution Path
Use the clinic day as a controlled sequence rather than a passive patient experience. On arrival, confirm identity with passport or matching government identification, verify the scheduled procedure, and match the day’s scope against the prior written treatment pathway. The patient or escort should open ws-05-a before any pre-medication or sedation discussion begins, because recall quality degrades once anxiolytics, IV sedation, or procedure stress enters the workflow.

The first execution block is scope verification. Confirm the teeth or arches involved, whether extractions are planned, whether implants will be placed or only evaluated, whether temporary prosthetics will be delivered same day, and whether the onsite lab is expected to fabricate or modify components during the visit. If the clinic identifies a same-day limitation based on new imaging or oral findings, record the exact reason, the revised plan, and whether that revision adds hotel nights, follow-up appointments, or a second Costa Rica trip.

Decision point: if a same-day change increases total case cost by more than 10% of the accepted quote or extends the stay by more than 2 nights, escalate to a financial and logistics re-approval before proceeding. The output is a revised treatment/cost note attached to ws-05-a and ws-05-e. Downstream effect: Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance must be updated for a longer recovery window, and US-vs-Costa Rica Restorative Cost and Payment Decision Control may need re-entry if savings assumptions are broken.

The second execution block is sedation-day control. If the procedure includes IV sedation or another impairment-producing pathway, complete ws-05-b before sedation starts: consent status, last food/liquid timing if instructed by the clinic, current medication disclosure, escort name and mobile number, discharge transport route, and first-night supervision plan. A patient who arrives alone for a sedation-dependent procedure without a documented escort is not discharge-ready.

Decision point: if IV sedation is scheduled and no escort is physically present and reachable at discharge, the release pathway must escalate before the procedure or before sedation administration, depending on clinic policy. Required output: documented resolution, such as companion arrival, change in sedation plan approved by the treating team, or case rescheduling. Downstream effect: Costa Rica Medical Travel and Surgical Trip Readiness and recovery lodging assumptions are updated immediately.

The third execution block is intra-day specification capture. For implants, record the system actually used, including Nobel Biocare or Zimmer if applicable, implant site, quantity, diameter/length if provided, and whether grafting, immediate loading, or multi-unit components were used. For crown, bridge, veneer, or full-arch cases, capture the restorative type, whether the onsite lab fabricated or adjusted the work, and whether provisionals or finals were delivered.

The fourth execution block is medication and discharge acquisition. Before leaving the chair area or discharge desk, complete ws-05-d and ws-05-e with every medication name, dosage, interval, first dose timing, food restrictions, oral hygiene restrictions, contact route for urgent symptoms, and all documents received. The patient should not enter transport to the onsite hotel or airport route until the discharge packet has been physically checked against the required list.

Decision point: if the clinic cannot provide a written record of what was placed, prescribed, or changed that day, classify discharge as incomplete and remain onsite until either the documents are produced or a documented follow-up delivery deadline is assigned within 24 hours. Downstream effect: without that packet, the case cannot cleanly hand off to recovery monitoring or U.S. follow-up preparation.

## Deep Operational Guidance
Procedure-day control exists because pre-trip planning is only provisional until the treating clinicians examine the patient in person. In restorative dental tourism, the highest-friction failure is not always a surgical error; it is mismatch between what the patient thinks was authorized and what was actually performed. Cross-border care makes this more severe because the patient may be sedated, in pain, using a temporary hotel room as a recovery base, and flying home within days. That compresses the time available to detect missing information.

Identity verification sounds basic, but it anchors every later record. The correct patient file, radiographs, financial ledger, implant labels, prescriptions, and discharge documents all depend on exact matching at the start of the day. If multiple appointments, multiple providers, or same-day lab work are involved, the patient and escort should explicitly confirm full name, date of birth, planned procedure, and operative sites before consent finalization. The output is not just reassurance; it is a confirmed record path tied to all paper and digital artifacts generated that day.

Consent control must be treated as a scope-locking mechanism. For implant, extraction, and full-mouth work, verbal summaries are insufficient because same-day changes can alter warranty implications, recovery demands, or return-trip timing. The patient should confirm whether the procedure is diagnostic only, surgical only, prosthetic delivery only, or a combined surgery-and-temporary-restoration day. If the answer is mixed or conditional, ws-05-a must record the branch conditions, such as “implant placement only if bone quality adequate” or “same-day temporary only if primary stability achieved.”

Decision point: if the clinic states that final treatment decisions depend on findings after sedation or after extraction, then pre-designate the decision authority and limit. If the patient will be impaired, the escort may need only observation authority, not consent authority, unless explicit arrangements exist; if no such authority path is clear, treatment should be limited to the documented pre-consented scope. Downstream effect: prevents unreviewed expansion from a partial plan into a larger irreversible intervention.

Sedation control is both a safety and documentation issue. Once sedatives are administered, the patient becomes a poor recorder of details and may agree to information without retaining it. That means all core data capture—planned scope, expected medications, escort routing, post-op contact number, and immediate restrictions—must be complete before impairment. For cases involving IV sedation, extractions with immediate implants, or full-arch preparation, the escort becomes an operating role, not a courtesy companion.

The escort’s minimum duties are concrete: remain reachable for the entire procedure window, receive discharge instructions directly from clinic staff, confirm possession of medications and documents, accompany the patient to the onsite hotel or equivalent lodging, and monitor the first dosing interval. If the escort is not bilingual and instructions are complex, require written English instructions before departure. Trigger: any instruction given only verbally or only to the sedated patient requires re-issuance to the escort and notation in ws-05-b.

Implant and prosthetic specification capture is the most important deep-read control in this chapter because it determines whether later intervention is possible. U.S. providers often need exact implant system, connection type, restorative platform, temporary/final status, and component information before they can tighten, repair, adjust, or even order compatible parts. “Implants were placed” is operationally useless. “Nobel Biocare implants placed at sites #REQUIRES CLARIFICATION with provisional prosthesis delivered” is still incomplete unless site count, component information, and treating-provider documentation are retained.

For full-arch or All-on-X style work, same-day language can hide multiple distinct events: extractions, bone reduction, implant placement, multi-unit abutment selection, conversion denture fabrication, bite registration, temporary arch insertion, and post-insertion adjustment. Each has follow-up implications. If the onsite lab modified a prosthesis on procedure day, that should be captured because future fit disputes, fracture review, or warranty questions may depend on whether the delivered piece was provisional conversion work or a later-stage final prosthesis.

Decision point: if any implant or prosthetic brand, component, or restorative designation is missing at discharge, request completion before leaving the facility. If not available immediately, obtain a named staff contact and written commitment for delivery by email or print within 24 hours. Downstream effect: U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control cannot be considered ready until ws-05-c is complete.

Medication logging must be exact because post-op failures often begin as timing failures. Record the name of each antibiotic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, rinse, or adjunct medication; the strength; dose interval; first dose time; whether to take with food; and the symptom threshold for calling the clinic. “Take as needed” is insufficient without a pain or symptom boundary. If medications are supplied through the onsite pharmacy, the patient or escort should verify that labels match the written instructions before leaving.

A practical control is to convert every medication into clock times for the first 24 hours. For example, if the first antibiotic dose is due at 6:00 p.m. and the next at 2:00 a.m., that burden must be consciously accepted by the escort or patient, especially after sedation. Trigger: if the interval schedule cannot be executed because of travel, sleep, nausea, or patient impairment, the clinic must clarify how to restart or adjust dosing. Output: corrected medication schedule noted in ws-05-d.

Discharge handoff is complete only when the patient possesses a usable packet. Minimum expected contents are written post-op instructions, medication list or prescriptions, procedure summary, implant/prosthetic details if applicable, urgent contact route, and follow-up appointment timing while in Costa Rica. If imaging, photos, or operative labels are offered, duplicate them before departure if feasible. The rule from the document operating framework applies here with full force: before leaving Costa Rica after any major restorative phase, obtain and duplicate the complete discharge packet.

## Failure Dynamics and Recovery
Failure scenario 1: the patient undergoes implant placement with IV sedation, returns to the onsite hotel, and realizes that neither they nor the escort knows the implant brand, number placed, or whether grafting was performed. Five days later, after returning to the United States, a provisional feels loose and a local dentist asks for system details before touching it. Consequence: delay of urgent evaluation, possible refusal of treatment by the U.S. provider, and risk that a simple component issue becomes a larger prosthetic or implant stability problem. Recovery action: use ws-05-c and ws-05-e to request same-day or next-business-day records from Cavallini before return flight whenever possible; after U.S. return, unresolved missing specs become an escalation item within 24 hours, not an administrative afterthought.

Failure scenario 2: the day-of-treatment plan changes from 4 implants with temporary restoration to 6 implants plus grafting, adding 3 hotel nights and a materially higher payment amount. The patient is already in Costa Rica and feels pressured to proceed because flights are booked. Consequence: total all-in case cost can exceed the approved budget by thousands of dollars, missed-work exposure increases, and the original recovery timeline becomes invalid. Recovery action: if the cost delta exceeds 10% or stay extension exceeds 2 nights, stop and re-run approval against available funds, revised lodging, revised flight exposure, and companion availability before authorizing expansion.

Failure scenario 3: the patient is discharged after sedation with antibiotics and pain medication but receives only verbal instructions, then misses two antibiotic doses in the first 12 hours because the escort assumed the first dose was already given onsite. Consequence: unmanaged pain, infection-control failure risk, after-hours phone calls, and possible emergency assessment in a foreign country. If fever, increasing swelling, uncontrolled bleeding, or severe pain escalates overnight without a written threshold, the patient may either under-react or overuse emergency services. Recovery action: the first 24-hour medication grid must be completed before transport, and the escort must confirm the exact first-dose status for each medication line item.

Failure scenario 4: a patient departs Costa Rica on a planned commercial flight 48 hours after extensive full-arch surgery because the original itinerary is nonrefundable, despite new swelling and inability to maintain oral intake. Consequence: dehydration, pain escalation during travel, inability to manage bleeding or nausea in transit, and poor compliance with early recovery instructions. Recovery action belongs partly to the next domain, but this chapter must capture any day-of-treatment extension recommendation in writing. If the clinical team indicates that return travel should be delayed, that instruction must appear in the discharge record and trigger immediate itinerary rework.

Decision point: if post-procedure bleeding saturates gauze repeatedly beyond the threshold given by the clinic, pain remains uncontrolled after the prescribed medication window, or swelling worsens rather than stabilizes in the first 24 hours, activate the urgent contact route immediately rather than waiting for the next routine visit. Output: documented time of symptom onset, medications taken, photo if requested, and clinic response. Downstream effect: recovery monitoring status changes from routine to active issue management.

Another frequent failure is assuming that warranty protection can substitute for documentation. It cannot. A lifetime warranty program may still require proof of maintenance, treatment records, and clarification of whether the issue involves the implant fixture, prosthetic component, occlusion, patient damage, or noncompliance. If the patient leaves without definitive records and later claims that a prosthesis fractured or fit changed, the administrative burden of proving what was delivered rises sharply.

Decision point: if the clinic references warranty coverage during discharge, request the treatment-specific warranty conditions tied to the actual work completed that day, especially if the delivered prosthesis is provisional rather than final. Downstream effect: U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control can only preserve warranty compliance when actual delivered status is known.

## Cross-Domain Handoffs
This chapter closes by exporting two classes of information. The first goes to Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance: medication schedule, symptom thresholds, next appointment timing, travel restrictions, escort duties, and the exact procedure performed. Without that transfer, recovery monitoring becomes guesswork. The second goes to U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control: implant system details, prosthetic identifiers, operative summary, and documented changes from the original plan.

The handoff to recovery should occur the same day, ideally before the patient reaches lodging. The patient or escort should photograph or duplicate the completed worksheets and discharge documents, then verify that the first-night recovery plan matches the actual procedure severity. If extractions, grafting, multiple implants, or full-arch temporaries were added, the recovery monitoring plan must be upgraded immediately. Trigger: any unplanned increase in invasiveness requires revised symptom surveillance and potentially longer local stay assumptions.

The handoff to U.S. follow-up is more than record storage. It establishes whether a stateside dentist can interpret what Cavallini did and whether future tightening, adjustment, relining, occlusal correction, or emergency triage will be possible without reverse engineering the case. For implant-supported cases, ws-05-c is the bridge document. For crown and bridge cases, the equivalent record is the exact restorative scope, material type if provided, cementation or screw-retention status if applicable, and whether the work is provisional or final.

Decision point: if the patient cannot leave the clinic area with a complete procedure summary and a clear urgent contact route, the chapter is not operationally complete. If the patient cannot identify who will receive U.S.-side records after return, the next domain starts in a degraded state and must be flagged as at-risk. Downstream effect: unresolved handoff items become priority tasks within 24 hours, not optional cleanup.

Closure criteria for this chapter are strict. The chapter is operationally complete only when all five outputs exist in usable form: ws-05-a completed with actual treatment performed and variances documented; ws-05-b completed for any sedation or major procedure day with escort and discharge status verified; ws-05-c completed for implant or prosthetic component capture to the extent provided by the clinic; ws-05-d completed with medication names, timing, and symptom thresholds; and ws-05-e completed showing receipt of the discharge packet or a documented missing-document resolution deadline. In addition, the patient or escort must possess a functioning urgent contact route, know the next in-country follow-up step, and have duplicated the key records for later U.S. transfer. Until those conditions are met, treatment may be clinically finished for the day, but operational control has not yet been achieved.

chapters.chapter_title: Post-Operative Symptom Surveillance, Medication Adherence, Emergency Escalation, and Return-Flight Clearance in Costa Rica

chapter_opener.what_this_is_for: This chapter controls the recovery period in San José after Cavallini treatment so you can detect complications early, escalate correctly, and leave Costa Rica only when both clinical stability and records completeness are confirmed.
chapter_opener.when_it_matters: It starts as soon as Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management gives you the discharge packet, medications, and post-op instructions, and it remains active until return-flight fitness and final packet completeness are both verified.
chapter_opener.failure_looks_like: The patient assumes worsening pain, swelling, bleeding, fever, nausea, or prosthetic problems are normal, does not log the trend, reaches flight day unstable, and either misses travel, boards without safe clearance, or returns to the U.S. without usable records for follow-up.
produces: Completed Costa Rica recovery symptom and medication log through departure decision point
Documented red-flag escalation record showing self-monitoring, clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency routing decisions
Return-flight fitness determination based on pain control, bleeding stability, swelling trend, hydration, mobility, and medication continuity
Duplicated final return packet for U.S. follow-up and warranty preservation
do_first: Before treating recovery as active, verify you have discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details from Cavallini

Within 2 hours of reaching the onsite hotel or other lodging, open ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c and record the baseline procedure, sedation end time, medications, first oral intake, and first pain score

minimum_viable_actions: Confirm the procedure performed, sedation or anesthesia end time, medication list, and urgent clinic contact route before the first night begins.
Start ws-06-a within 2 hours of arrival at recovery lodging and log pain, bleeding, swelling, temperature if relevant, oral intake, hydration, and every medication dose actually taken.
Use ws-06-b at each check-in to classify symptoms into self-monitoring, same-day clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency intervention.
For implant placement, extractions, sinus work, or All-on-X/full-mouth treatment, run symptom checks at minimum morning, mid-day, evening, and bedtime for the first 48 hours.
Before sleep on post-op day 0 and day 1, verify bleeding level, pain control, hydration, urination, medication completion, overnight contact ability, and safe mobility to the bathroom.
Do not begin airport or flight-prep execution until ws-06-d supports commercial travel and the final return packet has been audited and duplicated.
quick_reference_rules: Do not treat recovery as controlled until discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details are in hand — Rationale: symptom interpretation is invalid without knowing what was done and what was prescribed. Trigger: procedure-day discharge. Risk: missed complication signals, invalid travel clearance, and incomplete U.S. handoff.

Track symptoms on a fixed cadence rather than by memory — Rationale: trend reversal matters more than any single pain or swelling report. Trigger: first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus work, or full-mouth treatment. Risk: delayed recognition of infection, bleeding, prosthetic instability, or medication failure.

Record every dose actually taken, including missed, delayed, or vomited doses — Rationale: worsening symptoms cannot be interpreted correctly while medication adherence is unclear. Trigger: every antibiotic, analgesic, anti-inflammatory, or rinse event. Risk: reassessment delays, wrong clinical assumptions, and uncontrolled recovery drift.

If pain stays at 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive monitoring intervals despite taking medication as directed, contact Cavallini the same day — Rationale: rising pain despite adherence suggests a complication or inadequate control plan. Trigger: repeated high pain scores with documented adherence. Risk: lost in-country correction time before the booked return flight.

If active bleeding continues beyond the clinic's expected window, repeatedly soaks gauze, pools, or restarts after stopping, stop travel-prep tasks and escalate immediately — Rationale: ongoing bleed risk is incompatible with airport processing and flight stability. Trigger: persistent or recurrent active bleeding. Risk: emergency transport, flight cancellation, and unmanaged post-op instability.

Treat fever of 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, especially with increasing swelling, foul taste, or malaise, as an urgent reassessment path — Rationale: a red-flag cluster may indicate infection affecting healing or prosthetic stability. Trigger: fever plus worsening symptoms after early recovery. Risk: implant compromise, antibiotic changes under time pressure, and forced itinerary extension.

Do not self-clear for flight just because you feel better than yesterday — Rationale: commercial travel requires sustained function, hydration, medication continuity, mobility, and symptom predictability across a 6- to 10-hour travel block. Trigger: departure planning. Risk: instability during airport processing, missed flights, or post-arrival decompensation.

Do not leave San José without a duplicated return packet containing procedure summary, implant or prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, and written complication instructions — Rationale: U.S. dentists and warranty processes depend on exact documentation. Trigger: clinic checkout and airport departure preparation. Risk: refusal of follow-up care, warranty ambiguity, and duplicate imaging or remake costs.

decision_guide.if_condition: You have the discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details from Cavallini on procedure day.
decision_guide.then_action: Open ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c and establish the baseline within 2 hours of arriving at lodging.
decision_guide.else_action: Obtain the missing items from Cavallini before treating the recovery stay as operationally active.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Any required discharge item is still missing by the first monitoring interval or before the first night.

decision_guide.decision: Is the patient staying in the self-monitoring lane?
decision_guide.if_condition: Bleeding is light and decreasing, pain is controlled, fluids are tolerated, and the patient can ambulate safely.
decision_guide.then_action: Continue the scheduled monitoring cadence and maintain medication adherence logging in ws-06-a.
decision_guide.else_action: Reclassify the issue in ws-06-b to same-day clinic contact or higher.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Any monitored variable trends in the wrong direction for 2 consecutive check-ins.

decision_guide.decision: Does pain require same-day clinic contact?
decision_guide.if_condition: Pain remains at 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive monitoring intervals despite taking prescribed medication as directed.
decision_guide.then_action: Contact Cavallini the same day with the logged timeline, medication record, and current function status.
decision_guide.else_action: Continue self-monitoring if pain is improving or responds predictably to medication.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Pain is rising, waking the patient, or cannot be projected as manageable through the planned travel window.

decision_guide.if_condition: Active bleeding repeatedly soaks gauze, pools rather than diminishes, continues beyond the clinic's expected window, or restarts after having stopped.
decision_guide.then_action: Pause all travel-prep tasks and obtain immediate clinic guidance using ws-06-c contact routes.
decision_guide.else_action: Remain in self-monitoring if only light oozing or pink saliva is present and the trend is decreasing.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Pressure measures fail or bleeding is still active near departure day.

decision_guide.if_condition: Temperature reaches 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, or fever appears with increasing swelling, foul taste, or malaise.
decision_guide.then_action: Request urgent reassessment before any flight segment proceeds and document the symptom cluster in ws-06-a and ws-06-b.
decision_guide.else_action: Continue trend monitoring if no fever cluster exists and symptoms are stable or improving.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The fever cluster appears after temporary improvement or is accompanied by worsening facial swelling or increasing pain.

decision_guide.if_condition: Medications are being taken on schedule, kept down, and tolerated with adequate hydration or food as instructed.
decision_guide.then_action: Continue the prescribed schedule and log every dose, including response and tolerance.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark the event as missed, delayed by more than 2 hours, or vomited and contact Cavallini if adherence cannot be restored.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The patient cannot drink adequate fluids, keep medications down, or tolerate soft food for 12 hours or more after the expected sedation recovery period.

decision_guide.decision: Is oral function and prosthetic stability acceptable?
decision_guide.if_condition: The patient can swallow, sip water, take medications, tolerate the assigned diet stage, and close without a sudden high bite, clicking, or localized mechanical pain.
decision_guide.then_action: Maintain the soft-diet restrictions and continue monitoring without stress-testing the restoration.
decision_guide.else_action: Stop testing chewing function and request same-stay reassessment in San José.
decision_guide.escalate_when: A provisional feels unstable, a sudden high spot develops, repeated clicking occurs, or localized closure pain appears before departure.

decision_guide.decision: Is the patient fit for commercial return travel?
decision_guide.if_condition: Symptoms are stable or improving, bleeding is controlled, hydration and soft intake are adequate, mobility supports airport transit, medication continuity is intact, and ws-06-d is complete.
