decision_guide.then_action: Approve transition to departure execution and prepare the cross-domain handoff package for U.S. follow-up.
decision_guide.else_action: Remain in Costa Rica recovery control and obtain Cavallini reassessment before self-clearing for flight.
decision_guide.escalate_when: The patient still requires rescue pain medication at intervals shorter than prescribed, cannot predict symptom control through a 6- to 10-hour travel block, or cannot move safely through transport transitions.

decision_guide.if_condition: The return packet includes procedure summary, implant brand and components when applicable, prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, written post-op instructions, and complication guidance.
decision_guide.then_action: Duplicate the packet before leaving San José and attach it to the downstream U.S. follow-up handoff.
decision_guide.else_action: Stop departure prep and request the missing documents from Cavallini.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Any packet element needed for U.S. follow-up or warranty preservation is missing on airport day.

trigger_blocks: Trigger: Arrival at hotel or recovery lodging after discharge -> Response: establish baseline in ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c within 2 hours.
Trigger: IV sedation or major surgery with companion present -> Response: assign the companion as primary recorder for the first monitoring interval and first night checks.
Trigger: Two consecutive worsening symptom check-ins -> Response: move the issue out of observation and into same-day clinic contact or urgent reassessment.
Trigger: Pain 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive intervals despite adherence -> Response: contact Cavallini the same day with the logged trend and medication record.
Trigger: Repeated gauze saturation, pooled bleeding, or recurrent bleeding after prior control -> Response: suspend travel preparation and seek immediate clinic guidance.
Trigger: Fever 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window, especially with swelling, foul taste, or malaise -> Response: request urgent reassessment before any flight proceeds.

Trigger: Inability to keep fluids, soft food, or medications down for 12 hours or more after expected sedation recovery -> Response: contact Cavallini because symptom interpretation and healing support are no longer reliable.
Trigger: Sudden high bite, clicking, localized closure pain, or suspected provisional instability -> Response: stop chewing tests and request same-stay prosthetic reassessment.
Trigger: Planned departure day arrives -> Response: complete ws-06-d and audit the final return packet before any airport transfer.
risk_blocks: Risk: Misclassifying a complication as normal post-op discomfort -> Mitigation: use scheduled trend logging and red-flag classification instead of memory or reassurance alone.
Risk: Medication failure hidden by poor documentation -> Mitigation: record every actual dose, delay, skipped dose, vomiting event, and tolerance problem in ws-06-a.

Risk: Escalation delay caused by missing phone numbers or unclear routing -> Mitigation: preload Cavallini daytime, after-hours, lodging, transport, and local emergency routes in ws-06-c before a problem develops.

Risk: Flight-day instability after unchecked bleeding, swelling, pain, or dehydration -> Mitigation: block travel-prep tasks until ws-06-d supports commercial travel and no unresolved red-flag remains.

Risk: Early infection compromising implants or full-mouth stability -> Mitigation: treat fever plus worsening swelling, pain, foul taste, or malaise as an urgent reassessment cluster rather than waiting for scheduled follow-up.
Risk: Provisional fracture or loosening from early chewing tests -> Mitigation: measure function narrowly, follow diet restrictions, and escalate mechanical symptoms before leaving San José.

Risk: Sedated patient treated as independently reliable on the first night -> Mitigation: require escort verification of swallowing, orientation, dose completion, and safe bathroom mobility when IV sedation was used.
Risk: U.S. follow-up failure due to missing implant brand, component, or prosthetic data -> Mitigation: audit and duplicate the return packet before airport departure.
output_summaries: Recovery trend log completed through departure decision point — gives Cavallini and any U.S. dentist a time-sequenced record of pain, swelling, bleeding, oral intake, hydration, and medication events.
Red-flag escalation record — shows whether each issue stayed in self-monitoring or required same-day clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency routing.
Medication adherence history with actual use notes — allows symptoms to be interpreted in context and supports medication adjustment decisions.
Return-flight fitness assessment — determines whether commercial air travel is operationally safe given pain control, bleeding stability, swelling trend, mobility, and hydration.
Complete duplicated return packet — enables U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control to begin without documentation gaps.
Resolved-event summary for any in-country complication — prevents the next domain from inheriting an unexplained symptom spike or unmanaged prosthetic issue.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Costa Rica Dental Recovery Daily Symptom and Medication Tracking Log
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use immediately after arrival at recovery lodging and continue through the departure decision point, with at least morning, mid-day, evening, and bedtime entries for the first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus work, or full-mouth treatment.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables trend-based symptom interpretation, defensible clinic communication, and documented handoff to the U.S. follow-up domain.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Post-Implant and Full-Mouth Surgery Red-Flag Escalation Checklist for San José Stay
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use at every symptom check-in and whenever pain, swelling, bleeding, fever, nausea, oral function, or prosthetic stability changes.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables correct routing between self-monitoring, same-day clinic contact, urgent reassessment, and emergency intervention.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Clinic Contact, Local Emergency Option, and Escalation Routing Form for Post-Op Issues
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use within the first 2 hours of reaching lodging, before a problem occurs, to preload Cavallini daytime contacts, after-hours route if provided, onsite hotel assistance, transportation fallback, and local emergency options.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables rapid escalation without losing time searching for numbers, medication names, or transport support during a complication.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use on departure day and earlier if symptom instability raises doubt about airport processing or a 6- to 10-hour travel block.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables defensible go/no-go travel clearance and proper transition into U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

cascade_triggers: When Cavallini discharge instructions, medication list, contact routes, and procedure details are received, trigger recovery monitoring execution in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance.
When any symptom exceeds the clinic's post-op threshold during the San José stay, trigger immediate reassessment or emergency escalation before any flight segment proceeds.

When ws-06-d confirms commercial travel fitness and the final return packet is complete, trigger stateside handoff preparation in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When the patient leaves Costa Rica without unresolved red-flag issues and with duplicated records, trigger warranty-preserving follow-up scheduling and U.S. dentist transfer in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.
chapters.scenario_scene: On Tuesday at 8:00 p.m., Elena is 30 hours post-implant placement with extractions and has already logged four check-ins in ws-06-a. Her pain rises from 4/10 at noon to 8/10 by evening despite taking medication on schedule, and her temperature reaches 100.8 F with increasing one-sided swelling. Because ws-06-b classifies this as urgent reassessment rather than routine discomfort, she contacts Cavallini that night instead of waiting for her Thursday flight. The next morning she is reassessed in San José, her return timeline is adjusted before airport transfer is attempted, and the cost of unmanaged deterioration is contained before it becomes a missed-flight emergency.
success_metrics: 100% of required discharge items obtained before the first controlled recovery interval: discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details.
At least 4 symptom check-ins per 24 hours during the first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus work, or All-on-X/full-mouth treatment.
0 unresolved red-flag items above the self-monitoring threshold at the time ws-06-d is used for final travel clearance.
Pain below 7/10 and not rising across 2 consecutive monitoring intervals before commercial travel is approved.
No active bleeding requiring repeated gauze saturation at the departure decision point.
Final return packet duplicated before leaving San José with procedure summary, implant or prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, and written complication instructions all present.
chapters.detailed_explanation:
This domain converts post-op recovery from a vague waiting period into a controlled monitoring cycle. The operational objective is not to eliminate discomfort; it is to detect whether the recovery pattern is stabilizing, deteriorating, or becoming unsafe for travel while the patient is still inside Cavallini's correction window in San José. That distinction matters more in cross-border dental care than in local care because each lost day compresses the time available for reassessment before the booked return flight.

The chapter begins only after usable discharge inputs exist. Without the written instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, and procedure details, there is no reliable baseline for deciding whether pain, bleeding, swelling, nausea, oral function limits, or temperature changes are expected versus abnormal. That is why the first action is administrative verification, followed immediately by ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c setup. If IV sedation or major surgery occurred, the companion is not optional support; during the first interval and first night, the companion is a control function because the patient may not be a reliable recorder.

Symptom surveillance is built around trends. A single pain score has weak value by itself. A pain curve rising from 4/10 to 8/10 despite documented medication adherence is materially different from stable soreness that drops after dosing. The same applies to swelling, bleeding, fever clusters, oral intake, and function. This is why the chapter requires scheduled check-ins, especially during the first 48 hours after implants, extractions, sinus-related work, or full-mouth treatment. The point is to catch deterioration early enough that San José reassessment is still feasible.

Medication adherence is treated as a control variable because broken adherence contaminates the symptom picture. If an antibiotic was skipped, an analgesic was delayed, or medication was vomited after dosing, the clinical interpretation changes. A worsening symptom pattern with intact adherence points toward one decision path; the same pattern with failed adherence may point toward another. Logging actual use rather than intended use allows Cavallini to respond to a clean record instead of a confused verbal summary.

Escalation logic in this chapter is based on progression, function, and control, not anxiety. Light decreasing oozing, tolerable pain, successful hydration, and safe ambulation support self-monitoring. Two consecutive worsening check-ins, persistent high pain despite medication, recurrent active bleeding, a fever-plus-swelling cluster, inability to keep medications down, or mechanical instability of a provisional move the patient into clinic contact or urgent reassessment. Airway-threatening symptoms or severe functional decline convert the issue from clinic reassessment to emergency routing. ws-06-c exists to prevent dangerous lag during that transition.

Travel clearance is intentionally stricter than simple improvement. Commercial air travel requires sustained sitting, transport transfers, hydration, carry-on medication control, and enough symptom predictability to tolerate a 6- to 10-hour block including airport time. A patient can look better in a hotel room and still fail airport readiness because bleeding remains unstable, narcotic rescue dosing is too frequent, swelling is still rising, or mobility is too weak for transport transitions. ws-06-d formalizes this decision so that departure is based on function and trend stability rather than hope.

The final control point is administrative closure. Even if symptoms are acceptable, the chapter is not complete until the return packet is audited and duplicated. For implant-supported and full-mouth cases, brand and component data, prosthetic details, medications, instructions, and complication guidance are needed for U.S. triage, maintenance, and warranty protection. Leaving San José without those records turns a solvable documentation step into a cross-border failure. Domain closure occurs only when both conditions are true: the patient is stable enough for travel and the records are complete enough for the next domain to operate safely.
chapters.narrative:
## Orientation Snapshot
This domain begins the moment Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management hands you the discharge packet, medications, and immediate post-procedure instructions. Its job is not comfort optimization in the abstract. Its job is to determine whether your post-operative course in San José is trending toward stable recovery, clinic reassessment, or emergency escalation before commercial travel is attempted.

The central operational risk is misclassifying a complication as "normal post-op discomfort" until the scheduled return flight is already imminent. That failure is expensive and unstable in cross-border dentistry. If an implant site is bleeding beyond expectation, a provisional full-arch prosthesis becomes functionally unusable, or swelling rises instead of plateaus, the consequence is not just one bad recovery day; it can mean extra hotel nights, rebooking flights, replacement medication, emergency transport, or a return to the U.S. without a usable handoff packet.

This chapter therefore converts recovery into a monitored control period with four outputs: a completed daily symptom and medication log, a documented red-flag escalation decision, a return-flight fitness determination, and a complete final return packet. If any one of those outputs is missing, this domain is not complete. The chapter closes only when you are both clinically stable for travel and administratively equipped for U.S. follow-up.

Decision rule: if the discharge instructions, medication list, urgent contact route, or procedure details are missing on procedure day, then recovery monitoring cannot begin in a controlled way and you must obtain those items from Cavallini before treating the recovery stay as active. Downstream effect: Return-Flight Fitness Assessment After Cavallini Restorative Treatment cannot be validated, and U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control will start with incomplete records.

## Immediate Execution Path
Within the first 2 hours after arrival at the onsite hotel or other recovery lodging, open ws-06-a, ws-06-b, and ws-06-c and create the operational baseline. Record the exact procedure performed, date and time anesthesia or IV sedation ended, all medications dispensed or prescribed, your first oral intake, and the first post-op pain score. If a companion is present because IV sedation or major surgery occurred, the companion becomes the primary recorder for the first monitoring interval.

Your first-day cadence should be scheduled rather than improvised. Log symptoms at minimum morning, mid-day, evening, and bedtime for the first 48 hours after implant placement, multiple extractions, sinus-related work, or All-on-X/full-mouth treatment. For less extensive crown or bridge work without surgery, the cadence can be reduced after the first day if symptoms remain stable. The point is trend detection: a single pain number means little, but pain 4/10 at noon rising to 8/10 by 8 p.m. despite medication is an escalation signal.

Medication adherence is a control variable, not a suggestion. Record every dose actually taken, not just what was prescribed, including time, quantity, missed doses, nausea after dosing, and whether food or hydration was tolerated. If an antibiotic, anti-inflammatory, analgesic, or mouth rinse was skipped, delayed by more than 2 hours, or vomited within a short interval after use, then mark it as a medication event requiring review against the clinic instructions. Downstream effect: symptom interpretation changes because worsening pain, swelling, or fever cannot be cleanly classified while the medication schedule is broken.

Use ws-06-b to classify every issue into one of four lanes: routine self-monitoring, same-day clinic contact, urgent reassessment, or emergency intervention. This is not based on anxiety level; it is based on progression, function, and control. If bleeding is light and decreasing, pain is controlled, fluids are tolerated, and you can ambulate safely, remain in self-monitoring. If any variable trends in the wrong direction for two consecutive check-ins, move the issue out of observation and into a contact lane.

Decision rule: if pain remains uncontrolled at 7/10 or higher for more than 2 consecutive monitoring intervals despite taking prescribed medication as directed, then contact Cavallini the same day for reassessment instructions. Decision rule: if active bleeding soaks gauze repeatedly or continues beyond the clinic's expected window after pressure measures, then do not proceed with travel preparation tasks; escalate to immediate clinic guidance. Decision rule: if fever reaches 100.4 F or higher after the initial recovery window or is paired with increasing swelling, foul taste, or malaise, then treat this as a possible infection pathway and request urgent reassessment before any flight segment proceeds.

Before sleep on post-op day 0 and day 1, run the minimum viability check: bleeding level, pain control, hydration status, urination, medication completion, ability to contact the clinic overnight, and mobility to the bathroom without instability. If IV sedation was used, do not treat the patient as independently reliable during the first night. The escort or companion must verify that the patient can swallow medication safely, is not progressively confused, and has not missed scheduled analgesics or antibiotics.

## Deep Operational Guidance
Post-operative surveillance in San José must focus on pattern recognition rather than isolated sensations. After implants, extractions, bone-related procedures, or full-arch conversions, some pain, swelling, bruising, speech alteration, and chewing limitation are expected. What matters operationally is whether symptoms follow the expected sequence of early peak and gradual stabilization, or whether they show a delayed deterioration pattern that indicates bleeding, infection, prosthetic instability, medication intolerance, or inadequate discharge control.

Pain should be tracked as both intensity and responsiveness. A patient with 6/10 soreness that drops to 3/10 after medication and remains there is on a different trajectory from a patient whose pain rises from 4/10 to 8/10 despite taking the prescribed analgesic on schedule. The first pattern supports continued hotel recovery; the second requires same-day contact because it may indicate pressure from an ill-fitting provisional, dry socket in an extraction area, implant-site complication, or failure of the medication plan. In cross-border care, the cost of waiting is high because every lost day compresses the window available for clinic correction before the booked return flight.

Swelling should be monitored by location, symmetry, and effect on function. Expected swelling usually peaks within the early post-op period and then stabilizes or declines. If swelling extends rapidly, becomes distinctly one-sided, interferes with swallowing, or limits mouth opening more than expected, then the issue leaves the routine lane. Downstream effect: return-to-travel clearance is suspended until the clinic confirms the airway, infection risk, and functional status are acceptable.

Bleeding control is similarly practical. Oozing, blood-tinged saliva, and occasional pink saliva after oral surgery are not the same as persistent active bleeding. If you need repeated fresh gauze changes over several hours, blood is pooling rather than diminishing, or bleeding restarts after it had stopped, treat that as a threshold event. The financial implication is immediate: a same-day reassessment can preserve the planned itinerary, while an unchecked bleed on the morning of departure can force airport cancellation, replacement transport, and potentially emergency care outside the treatment chain.

Temperature, chills, foul odor, and malaise must be interpreted together. A temperature reading has limited value if hydration is poor and no baseline was taken, but fever plus worsening facial swelling plus increasing pain is a red-flag cluster. If that cluster appears after initial temporary improvement, infection moves from theoretical risk to active operating concern. This is especially important in implant and full-mouth cases because infection can jeopardize immediate prosthetic stability, complicate antibiotic selection, and create dispute over whether later failures arose from early unmanaged complications.

Decision rule: if you cannot drink adequate fluids, keep medications down, or tolerate soft food for 12 hours or more after the initial expected sedation recovery period, then contact Cavallini because medication adherence and healing support are no longer operationally intact. Downstream effect: your pain data become unreliable, dehydration risk rises, and flight readiness will likely fail. Decision rule: if mobility is impaired to the point that you cannot safely walk to the bathroom, descend stairs, or enter transport without assistance on the planned departure day, then return-flight clearance is not met regardless of whether oral symptoms appear acceptable.

Oral function must be measured in narrow terms. Can you swallow? Can you sip water without severe pain? Can you take medications? Can you tolerate the prescribed diet stage? Can you close without a sudden high bite or prosthetic interference? In full-mouth or provisional cases, even a small occlusal problem can escalate from irritation to fractured provisional or screw instability if the patient tests chewing aggressively before reassessment.

The discharge instructions from Cavallini should govern local specifics such as rinsing timing, brushing restrictions, ice or cold-pack intervals, sleeping position, and activity limits. This chapter does not replace those instructions. It operationalizes them by requiring evidence that they were followed and by tying deviations to symptom interpretation. If the patient began vigorous rinsing too early, used a straw against instructions, smoked, drank alcohol, skipped antibiotics, or attempted solid food before clearance, then symptom worsening may be behavior-linked but still requires documentation and often clinic contact.

Use ws-06-c to build the communication stack before a problem occurs. The form should contain Cavallini daytime contact, after-hours route if provided, onsite hotel front desk assistance if relevant, transportation fallback, and local emergency option details. When symptoms accelerate, the dangerous delay is usually not indecision about whether something feels wrong; it is wasting 45 to 90 minutes searching for numbers, translating medication names, or trying to reconstruct what procedure was done. Preloading the escalation path reduces that lag.

Travel readiness is not simply "I feel better than yesterday." Commercial air travel requires sustained sitting, transport transitions, hydration management, medication timing, carry-on control of prescriptions, and enough functional reserve to handle delay or turbulence without losing recovery stability. A patient who can rest comfortably in a hotel bed may still be unfit for airport processing if swelling is rising, narcotic pain medication is still required at short intervals, bleeding has not fully stabilized, or the escort burden remains high.

Decision rule: if you still require rescue pain medication at intervals shorter than prescribed, are waking due to escalating pain, or are unable to predict symptom control through a 6- to 10-hour travel block including airport time, then postpone self-clearance and request clinic input before departure. Decision rule: if the final return packet does not include procedure summary, implant brand and components when applicable, prosthetic details when applicable, medication record, and written complication instructions, then do not treat checkout as complete. Downstream effect: U.S. follow-up provider acceptance and warranty preservation can both fail.

## Failure Dynamics and Recovery
Failure scenario 1: the patient undergoes implant placement with extractions on Monday, improves slightly Tuesday morning, but by Tuesday evening reports pain 8/10, increasing unilateral swelling, and a temperature of 100.8 F. Because the patient assumes this is "normal after implants," no clinic contact is made. By the scheduled Thursday flight, swelling is worse, chewing is minimal, and the patient requires airport wheelchair support and misses the flight after arriving unstable. Consequence set: 2 to 4 additional hotel nights, airline change fees or fare difference, possible urgent clinic intervention, and elevated risk that an early infection compromises implant healing.

Recovery from this failure requires re-entering the controlled escalation path immediately. Update ws-06-a with the full symptom progression, note actual medication adherence, contact Cavallini with the timeline rather than a vague distress statement, and obtain explicit direction on reassessment timing. The operational lesson is that trend reversal matters more than day count; "day 2" or "day 3" after surgery does not by itself determine normality.

Failure scenario 2: the patient receives an All-on-X provisional, feels reasonably comfortable, but starts testing harder foods within 48 hours because the prosthesis seems fixed and functional. A bite interference develops, one side begins taking excess load, and by the night before departure the patient experiences sharp contact, localized soreness, and fear that the provisional is shifting. If the patient boards anyway and the provisional fractures or loosens in the U.S. within 72 hours, repair may not be available locally without the original prosthetic details and the treating clinic's protocol. Consequence set: emergency stateside visits, possible refusal by a U.S. dentist to alter another clinic's fresh provisional, and loss of stability during the exact period when the restoration needs protection.

Recovery requires immediate same-stay reassessment while still in San José, not remote troubleshooting after return. The threshold is mechanical instability or occlusal mismatch, not visible catastrophe. If chewing creates a sudden high spot, repeated clicking, or localized pain with closure, then stop testing the prosthesis and request evaluation before airport day.

Failure scenario 3: the patient is discharged after sedation with antibiotics, anti-inflammatory medication, and analgesics, but severe nausea prevents dose completion for most of the first night. By the next afternoon, pain control is erratic, hydration is poor, and the patient has taken only partial antibiotic coverage. Because there is no written medication log, the clinic receives a confused report and cannot quickly determine whether symptoms reflect expected inflammation, medication failure, or early complication. Consequence set: avoidable reassessment delays, possible need to change medication, dehydration risk, and a recovery timeline no longer aligned with the booked return segment.

This failure is prevented by recording each attempted dose and each tolerance failure. If medication is vomited, delayed by more than 2 hours, or skipped entirely, then it is not adherence and must be documented as a medication event. That record changes clinical decision-making; without it, the symptom picture is contaminated and expensive time is lost.

Failure scenario 4: the patient checks out and departs San José without a complete discharge packet because everything "looked fine." Back in the U.S., tissue irritation and bite discomfort develop within 5 days, and the local dentist asks for implant system, torque or component details, images, and procedure notes. The patient has only a generic receipt and cannot document whether Nobel Biocare or Zimmer components were used, what prosthetic work was delivered, or what medications were prescribed. Consequence set: delayed care, possible refusal of intervention, warranty ambiguity, and duplicate imaging or consultation costs.

The recovery action is administrative, not medical: before any departure to the airport, audit the packet against the domain output list. If any item is missing, halt departure prep and request completion from Cavallini. This is the last controlled moment to secure records without cross-border delay.

## Cross-Domain Handoffs
This chapter receives its starting conditions from Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management. That upstream domain must supply the procedure summary, discharge instructions, medication list, contact routes, and any implant or prosthetic identifiers available at that stage. If those inputs are incomplete, this chapter cannot generate a valid symptom interpretation because there is no agreed baseline for what was done, what was prescribed, or what warnings were already given.

Its primary downstream trigger is U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control. That trigger should fire only when two conditions are met: first, the patient meets return-to-travel criteria based on symptom stability, function, and medication control; second, the final return packet is complete enough for a U.S. dentist to triage, monitor, or intervene if needed. Sending a patient home with unresolved in-country instability or incomplete records pushes failure into the next domain and often converts a manageable San José correction into a more expensive U.S. access problem.

The handoff package should include, at minimum, the completed recovery log through departure day, any red-flag events and how they were resolved, the final medication list with actual use notes, written post-op instructions, emergency guidance, procedure summary, and implant/prosthetic details when relevant. For implant-supported and full-mouth cases, preserving brand and component data is not optional because later maintenance and warranty questions depend on them. For crown or bridge cases, occlusal adjustment issues, temporary restrictions, and cementation status should still be recorded because U.S. follow-up may need that context.

Closure criteria for this chapter are strict. The chapter is operationally complete only when all of the following are true: ws-06-a has a current recovery trend log through the departure decision point; ws-06-b shows no unresolved red-flag item above the self-monitoring threshold; ws-06-c contains active clinic and emergency routing details actually available during the stay; ws-06-d supports commercial travel based on pain control, bleeding stability, swelling trend, hydration, oral intake, mobility, and medication continuity; and the full return packet has been duplicated before leaving San José. If any one of those conditions is not met, the correct status is not "mostly ready" but "not cleared for domain closure."

Final decision rule: if symptoms are stable or improving, medications are being taken as directed, bleeding is controlled, hydration and soft intake are adequate, mobility supports airport transit, and the return packet is complete, then approve transition to U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control. If any of those variables fails, then remain in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance until Cavallini reassessment or emergency stabilization resolves the deficit.

chapters.chapter_title: Stateside Dentist Handoff, 30-Day Complication Capture, Cavallini Warranty Compliance, and Maintenance Scheduling

chapter_opener.what_this_is_for: This chapter converts completed Costa Rica treatment into a controlled U.S. follow-up system so recent restorative work remains clinically supported, documented, and warranty-defensible after you return home.
chapter_opener.when_it_matters: It begins within 72 hours before departure from Costa Rica, intensifies during the first 30 days after return, and remains active for all future hygiene, imaging, prosthetic review, and warranty-related events.
chapter_opener.failure_looks_like: You land in the United States with implants, crowns, provisionals, or All-on-X work but no confirmed U.S. provider, no readable implant or prosthetic records, no symptom log, and no proof of maintenance or clinic notification if problems emerge.
produces: Confirmed U.S. follow-up provider arrangement with defined role and first review window
Complete post-return dental handoff packet with duplicate storage and readability confirmation
First-30-days complication surveillance log with escalation history
Cavallini warranty compliance and claim-evidence register
Long-term cleaning, imaging, and prosthetic review schedule tied to delivered treatment
do_first: Within 72 hours before leaving Costa Rica, confirm one named U.S. office that has agreed to surveillance, emergency triage, or maintenance and record the exact scope accepted.
At final checkout, assemble and duplicate the complete handoff packet before leaving the clinic or onsite hotel area.

minimum_viable_actions: Confirm one named U.S. dentist, oral surgeon, prosthodontist, or restorative office before the return flight and document what they agreed to do.

Use the Post-Return Dental Handoff Packet Completeness Checklist before leaving Costa Rica to verify implant brand, procedure dates, prosthetic details, medications, instructions, images, and warranty terms are included.
Within 48 hours of arriving home, send the packet to the U.S. provider and obtain written acknowledgement that the files were received and can be opened.
Schedule the first U.S. review within 7 to 14 days after return for implant, implant-supported bridge, or All-on-X cases unless Cavallini gave a different timing instruction.
Use the First-30-Days Issue Capture Form daily for days 1 through 7, then every 3 days through day 30, and immediately whenever a new symptom appears.
Log every maintenance action, provider contact, symptom report, and evidence file in the Cavallini warranty register.
quick_reference_rules: Do not treat the Costa Rica phase as complete until a U.S. provider has affirmatively accepted a role in your follow-up — Rationale: post-return instability often appears after travel, not before it. Trigger: within 72 hours before departure and again before final return home. Risk: unmanaged complications, treatment delay, and refusal of local evaluation.

Do not leave Costa Rica without a duplicated handoff packet containing implant system details, prosthetic records, medications, instructions, dates, and provider names — Rationale: U.S. offices cannot safely triage undocumented foreign restorative work. Trigger: final checkout after surgery, provisional delivery, or final prosthetic delivery. Risk: cross-border records chase, delayed intervention, and warranty ambiguity.

Treat unreadable files or missing implant data as a control failure, not a minor admin problem — Rationale: platform and component details affect local intervention scope. Trigger: when the U.S. office reports imaging cannot be opened or records are incomplete. Risk: duplicate diagnostics, delayed mechanical care, and inability to verify compatibility.

For implant, implant-supported bridge, or All-on-X cases, anchor the first U.S. review to a scheduled date within 7 to 14 days after return unless Cavallini directs otherwise — Rationale: bite problems, loosening, and soft-tissue issues often emerge after swelling changes and normal function resumes. Trigger: once return date is known and records have been transmitted. Risk: preventable instability, emergency visits, and prosthetic damage.

Use trend-based symptom capture for the first 30 days, not memory-based reporting — Rationale: worsening over 24 hours is the operational threshold that separates routine recovery from escalation. Trigger: every day for the first 7 days, then every 3 days through day 30, and after any new symptom. Risk: late detection of bite interference, soft-tissue deterioration, or hardware instability.

If pain, swelling, looseness, bleeding, drainage, bad taste, or bite interference worsens over a 24-hour period, escalate the same day to the treating channel and log the contact — Rationale: deterioration after return can move from manageable to urgent quickly. Trigger: any worsening symptom trend. Risk: infection progression, provisional damage, and fragmented emergency care.

Treat the Cavallini lifetime warranty as a documentation-dependent system, not automatic protection — Rationale: claims require proof of treatment details, maintenance, timely notice, and evidence retention. Trigger: after every follow-up visit, symptom report, maintenance event, or clinic communication. Risk: denied coverage, uncovered repairs, and out-of-pocket return travel.

If any scheduled hygiene or surveillance review is missed by more than 30 days, open a maintenance deviation entry and reschedule immediately — Rationale: missed maintenance raises both clinical and warranty risk. Trigger: any overdue recurring appointment. Risk: tissue deterioration, prosthetic complications, and weakened warranty position.

decision_guide.decision: U.S. provider confirmed before final return
decision_guide.if_condition: A named U.S. office has agreed in writing or documented conversation to provide surveillance, emergency triage, routine maintenance, or another defined role, and a first review window is identified.
decision_guide.then_action: Record the office contact, accepted scope, and first review timing in the provider inventory and proceed to packet transfer.
decision_guide.else_action: Keep the case in active provider search, continue contacting offices from the provider inventory, and do not classify the Costa Rica phase as operationally closed.
decision_guide.escalate_when: No U.S. provider has affirmatively accepted any role before the final return home.

decision_guide.decision: Handoff packet complete enough for U.S. triage
decision_guide.if_condition: The packet includes procedure summary, treatment dates, provider names, implant brand if applicable, prosthetic details if provided, medications, post-op instructions, and supplied images or radiographs.
decision_guide.then_action: Duplicate the packet to cloud and local storage, transmit it within 48 hours of arrival home, and request readability confirmation.
decision_guide.else_action: Open a records recovery action with Cavallini the same business day and flag the missing items in the handoff checklist and warranty register.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Implant-related treatment lacks implant brand identification or procedure dates for each implant-related phase.

decision_guide.if_condition: The case involved implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X and no different written review timing was provided by Cavallini.
decision_guide.then_action: Schedule the first U.S. review for 7 to 14 days after return and use that date as the anchor for early surveillance and documentation.
decision_guide.else_action: For crown-only or adjustment-only cases, schedule review based on symptom presence and complexity, but do not leave follow-up open-ended.
decision_guide.escalate_when: No review date is scheduled after return for a surgical or multi-implant case.

decision_guide.if_condition: Pain, swelling, looseness, bleeding, bite interference, foul taste, drainage, or tissue irritation is worsening over a 24-hour period instead of improving or stabilizing.
decision_guide.then_action: Contact the treating channel specified in discharge instructions the same day, notify the U.S. provider or alternate local provider, and document symptoms, photos, and contact time in the issue capture form and warranty register.
decision_guide.else_action: Continue routine surveillance according to the 30-day schedule and compare each entry against the prior entry for trend classification.
decision_guide.escalate_when: Swelling increases after initial decline, foul taste or drainage appears, or bite prevents normal soft-food chewing for more than 48 hours after return.

decision_guide.if_condition: The office confirms the files were received, can be opened, and are sufficient to review the case.
decision_guide.then_action: Log the acknowledgement date and proceed with scheduling or maintaining the planned first review.
decision_guide.else_action: Resend files, follow up by phone within 2 business days, and request missing or reformatted files from Cavallini immediately.
decision_guide.escalate_when: No acknowledgement is received within 2 business days of packet transmission.

decision_guide.decision: Provider role sufficient for case complexity
decision_guide.if_condition: The chosen U.S. provider will handle the level of support the case requires, such as maintenance, occlusal checks, emergency triage, or implant-related assessment.
decision_guide.then_action: Classify the provider as active coverage and record the exact limits of authority in the provider inventory.
decision_guide.else_action: Classify the office as partial coverage only and continue building at least one backup pathway from the provider inventory.
decision_guide.escalate_when: An All-on-X or multi-implant case has only a provider willing to take emergency X-rays or offer minimal triage.

decision_guide.decision: Maintenance schedule adequate for warranty preservation
decision_guide.if_condition: Cleaning, surveillance imaging, restorative checks, and any written Cavallini maintenance expectations are assigned to actual dates or date ranges.
decision_guide.then_action: Build the recurring calendar, store completion evidence, and route every finished event into the warranty register.
decision_guide.else_action: Mark unknown intervals as REQUIRES CLARIFICATION and obtain written guidance rather than assuming the warranty remains valid without maintenance.
decision_guide.escalate_when: A hygiene or surveillance review is missed by more than 30 days.

trigger_blocks: Trigger: 72 hours before departure from Costa Rica with no named U.S. follow-up office confirmed. Immediate response: activate ws-07-a, contact providers from the inventory, document accepted scope, and keep the case in active stabilization status.

Trigger: Final checkout at Cavallini or departure from the onsite hotel area. Immediate response: run ws-07-b, verify packet completeness, and duplicate all files to cloud and local access storage before leaving.
Trigger: Arrival home plus 48 hours. Immediate response: transmit the packet to the U.S. office, request written readability confirmation, and log the transmission in ws-07-d.

Trigger: Implant, implant-supported bridge, or All-on-X case returned to the U.S. Immediate response: schedule the first U.S. review within 7 to 14 days unless Cavallini gave a different written interval.
Trigger: Any symptom trend worsens over a 24-hour period. Immediate response: complete a same-day entry in ws-07-c, notify the instructed treatment channel, and preserve photo evidence.

Trigger: Bite prevents normal soft-food chewing for more than 48 hours after return. Immediate response: request earliest possible occlusal review with the U.S. provider or Cavallini-directed local provider.

Trigger: U.S. office says imaging files cannot be opened or documentation is insufficient. Immediate response: resend files, request corrected or missing records from Cavallini the same business day, and keep the handoff task open.
Trigger: Any maintenance visit, imaging event, symptom report, or clinic communication occurs. Immediate response: create or update the evidence entry in ws-07-d and store supporting files.
risk_blocks: Risk: Returning home without a committed U.S. provider. Mitigation: confirm one named office before departure, record accepted scope, and maintain a backup provider path for complex implant cases.

Risk: Implant brand, component, or procedure-date data missing from the handoff packet. Mitigation: classify packet as incomplete, request records immediately from Cavallini, and do not close the handoff task until recovered.
Risk: Early bite interference or provisional instability goes undocumented. Mitigation: use ws-07-c on the required schedule and route worsening trends the same day.

Risk: U.S. provider refuses intervention because records are unreadable or incomplete. Mitigation: obtain readability acknowledgement within 2 business days and maintain duplicate packet storage in accessible formats.

Risk: Warranty claim weakened by undocumented phone calls or missing evidence. Mitigation: log every material communication, symptom event, photo set, and recommendation in ws-07-d with dates and storage location.

Risk: Missed maintenance creates biological, prosthetic, and warranty exposure. Mitigation: maintain ws-07-e as a controlled calendar and open a maintenance deviation entry for any visit missed by more than 30 days.

Risk: Patient assumes normal recovery when swelling, drainage, or bad taste indicate deterioration. Mitigation: use trend-based symptom review and escalate the same day when symptoms worsen over 24 hours.
Risk: Single-provider dependency for a complex All-on-X or multi-implant case. Mitigation: classify limited-scope offices as partial coverage only and maintain at least one backup specialist pathway.
output_summaries: Confirmed U.S. follow-up provider arrangement: establishes who owns surveillance, triage, or maintenance after return and prevents unmanaged post-travel gaps.
Post-return dental handoff packet: gives the U.S. provider enough implant, prosthetic, medication, and instruction data to assess the case without starting from zero.
Readability confirmation record: proves the packet was not only sent but actually received in usable form by the U.S. office.
First-30-days complication surveillance log: creates a dated symptom trend file that supports faster routing, better local care, and stronger warranty evidence.
Cavallini warranty compliance register: centralizes treatment dates, maintenance actions, communications, and evidence needed for later claims or disputes.
Long-term maintenance calendar: converts follow-up into due dates and completion tracking rather than vague intent to return for cleanings or imaging.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: U.S. Dentist Follow-Up Provider Inventory for Cavallini Restorative Cases
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use within 72 hours before departure from Costa Rica and again after return if the first office declines, delays, or offers only partial support.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables confirmed stateside surveillance, emergency triage routing, and backup provider coverage for implant or full-arch complications.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Post-Return Dental Handoff Packet Completeness Checklist for U.S. Providers
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use at final checkout in Costa Rica and before transmitting records to the U.S. provider.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables readable transfer of implant and prosthetic records and reduces refusal or delay by the U.S. office.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: First-30-Days Bite, Provisional, and Soft-Tissue Issue Capture Form After Return Home
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use daily for the first 7 days after return, then every 3 days through day 30, and immediately after any new symptom appears.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables trend-based escalation, targeted provider communication, and documented early complication management.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Cavallini Lifetime Warranty Compliance and Claim-Evidence Register
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use starting at final checkout and update after every provider visit, symptom event, maintenance action, and communication with Cavallini or U.S. offices.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables warranty preservation, claim support, dispute resolution, and reconstruction of the care timeline.

worksheet_linkage.worksheet_title: Long-Term Cleaning, Imaging, and Prosthetic Review Schedule for Costa Rica Restorative Work
worksheet_linkage.use_when: Use after the first post-return stabilization phase and whenever Cavallini or the U.S. provider defines maintenance intervals.
worksheet_linkage.unblocks: It enables routine maintenance control, overdue-visit escalation, and long-term treatment stability tracking.

cascade_triggers: When surgery or prosthetic delivery details are received from Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management, trigger handoff packet assembly and provider-transfer preparation in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When the return packet is complete and travel home is cleared in Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance, trigger U.S. provider transmission, readability confirmation, and first-review scheduling in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When the U.S. follow-up provider receives the full treatment packet, trigger the first post-return review and complication surveillance cycle in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When a worsening symptom trend is logged during the first 30 days after return, trigger same-day escalation to the discharge-instruction treatment channel and local provider triage in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When warranty-required maintenance or documentation deadlines are identified, trigger recurring maintenance scheduling and evidence-retention actions in U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.

When all 30-day issues have dispositions and the first review is complete, trigger transition from active stabilization to recurring maintenance control within U.S. Follow-Up Continuity, Warranty Preservation, and Long-Term Maintenance Control.
chapters.scenario_scene: Lisa returns to Texas 2 days after receiving an implant-supported provisional bridge at Cavallini. Before leaving San José, she confirmed a Dallas prosthodontist for surveillance, ran the handoff checklist, and stored her packet in both cloud and offline phone access. On day 5 at home, her issue log shows worsening right-side bite interference and reduced soft-food chewing tolerance compared with day 4, so she sends the symptom chronology and photos to both the U.S. office and Cavallini the same day. She is seen within 24 hours, receives an occlusal adjustment, and the event is logged in the warranty register with records, preventing a prolonged instability cycle and preserving claim evidence.
success_metrics: 1 named U.S. provider with defined role confirmed before final return home
100% of required handoff packet items transmitted within 48 hours of arrival home
U.S. provider readability acknowledgement received within 2 business days of packet transmission
First U.S. review scheduled within 7 to 14 days after return for implant, implant-supported bridge, or All-on-X cases unless Cavallini specifies otherwise
Issue capture entries completed daily on days 1 through 7 and at least every 3 days through day 30
0 unmanaged worsening symptoms left without same-day escalation during the first 30 days
0 scheduled maintenance visits missed by more than 30 days without a recorded deviation and reschedule date
chapters.detailed_explanation:
This chapter exists because major restorative treatment is not operationally finished when you leave Costa Rica. It has only changed custody. The clinical work may be complete for that phase, but continuity, warranty protection, and early complication control now depend on how well the case transfers into the U.S. care environment. That is why the chapter starts before the return flight rather than after you get home.

The first control objective is provider confirmation. A usable U.S. provider relationship is not a vague promise that an office might see you if needed. It is a documented agreement from one named office to perform surveillance, emergency triage, routine maintenance, or another clearly bounded role. This distinction matters because many offices will accept limited involvement with foreign restorative work, especially complex implant or full-arch cases. If the office will only take emergency X-rays, that is partial coverage, not full continuity.

The second control objective is packet integrity. The handoff packet is the translation layer between Cavallini's treatment environment and your U.S. provider's intervention environment. For crown-only work, a lighter packet may still be enough if it includes treatment dates, materials or prosthetic details when available, medications, instructions, and post-op notes. For implants, implant-supported bridges, and All-on-X, the packet must support component-level understanding. If implant identity data, timing, and prosthetic status are missing, the U.S. provider may delay even simple intervention because they cannot safely assume compatibility or reconstruct what was done abroad.

The third control objective is early trend capture. Many destabilizing issues appear only after swelling changes, diet expands, and medication support decreases. A bite that seemed acceptable on day 1 may be clearly dysfunctional on day 5. A provisional may begin to rock only after repeated function. Soft-tissue irritation can intensify gradually until eating and hygiene both deteriorate. The issue capture form therefore creates a trend file, not a diary. Its purpose is to classify each change as improving, unchanged, or worsening so routing decisions become operational rather than emotional.

The fourth control objective is same-day escalation discipline. This system does not require you to decide whether every symptom is an emergency in absolute clinical terms. It requires you to respond when the pattern worsens over a 24-hour period, when swelling increases after declining, when foul taste or drainage appears, or when bite interference blocks normal soft-food chewing beyond 48 hours. Those are operational escalation thresholds. Once crossed, the case moves from routine monitoring into same-day contact, documentation, and provider coordination.

The fifth control objective is warranty traceability. The Cavallini lifetime warranty cannot function as a passive reassurance mechanism. It only works if you can demonstrate what treatment was delivered, what maintenance was expected, what follow-up occurred, when a problem was reported, and what evidence supports your report. The warranty register is therefore not an archive for later. It is the live evidence ledger for every event that could affect care, cost, or coverage. If an issue was important enough to trigger a call, visit, photo, or recommendation, it belongs in the register.

The final control objective is long-term maintenance conversion. After the first 30 days, the case should leave active stabilization only if the first review is complete, known issues have disposition, and future cleaning, imaging, and prosthetic review dates exist. This matters most in implant-supported bridge and All-on-X cases, where mechanical stability, bite wear, soft tissue, hygiene access, and radiographic changes all require recurring review. If Cavallini has not given written maintenance intervals, the system does not allow assumption. The correct status is REQUIRES CLARIFICATION until a real interval is obtained and scheduled.

Operational closure is strict by design. You do not close this chapter because the travel ended. You close it only when a U.S. provider is confirmed, the packet has been transferred and validated, the first 30 days are documented, the warranty register is populated, and the long-term calendar exists. If any one of those pieces is missing, the case remains in active control because the value of the Costa Rica treatment is still exposed to preventable failure.
chapters.narrative:
## Orientation Snapshot

This domain begins before the return flight, not after it. Its job is to prevent a common post-tourism failure pattern: the patient lands back in the United States with recent implant surgery, crowns, provisional bridges, or an All-on-X prosthesis, but no committed U.S. provider, no organized implant documentation, and no structured process for detecting early problems. In that condition, minor occlusion issues, loose components, pressure spots, soft-tissue irritation, or hygiene breakdown can become emergency events with unclear responsibility and no clean warranty path.

The operational stake is high because Cavallini treatment is not complete when the patient leaves San José; it is only transferred. The transfer must include a U.S. follow-up provider, a complete clinical packet, a 30-day issue capture process, a warranty evidence trail, and a maintenance schedule tied to the actual restorative work delivered. If any one of those elements is missing, continuity of care becomes reactive and expensive, and the patient may face refusal of local treatment, uncovered repairs, or a repeat trip to Costa Rica.

This chapter therefore defines the control system for post-return stabilization. The primary outputs are a confirmed U.S. dentist follow-up arrangement, a complete handoff packet, a first-30-days complication surveillance log, a Cavallini warranty compliance register, and a recurring maintenance calendar. The chapter is operationally successful only when the patient can prove who will see them in the U.S., what records were transferred, what symptoms were monitored, what warranty conditions apply, and when each future surveillance event is due.

Decision threshold: if no U.S. dentist has affirmatively agreed to evaluate or triage the case before the final return home, the system is not in a stable state. Downstream effect: the patient must remain in active provider search and cannot treat the Costa Rica phase as operationally closed, even if surgery or final prosthetic delivery is complete.

## Immediate Execution Path

Start with provider confirmation, not symptom waiting. Within 72 hours before departure from Costa Rica, use the U.S. Dentist Follow-Up Provider Inventory for Cavallini Restorative Cases to confirm one specific stateside dentist, oral surgeon, prosthodontist, or restorative office willing to perform at least one of three roles: surveillance, emergency triage, or routine maintenance. "Willing to see me if something happens" is not enough; the output must be a named office contact, the scope they agreed to handle, and the first review window after return.

At final checkout in Costa Rica, assemble the Post-Return Dental Handoff Packet Completeness Checklist for U.S. Providers before leaving the clinic or onsite hotel area. The packet should include procedure summary, implant system details, prosthetic design details if provided, medications used and prescribed, post-op instructions, images or radiographs supplied by Cavallini, treatment dates, provider names, and warranty terms or warranty enrollment conditions. Duplicate the packet in at least two forms: one cloud-stored digital file set and one local copy accessible without internet during travel.

Within 48 hours of arriving home, transmit the packet to the U.S. provider and request written acknowledgement that the records were received and are readable. If the office cannot open the imaging files or says the documentation is insufficient, that is not an administrative nuisance; it is a control failure. The immediate corrective action is to request missing files from Cavallini the same business day and log the request in the warranty compliance register.

If the treatment involved implants, implant-supported bridges, or All-on-X, then the first U.S. review should be targeted for the first 7 to 14 days after return unless Cavallini provided a different timing instruction. If the treatment involved crowns or restorative adjustments without surgery, then the first review can be calibrated to symptom presence and treatment complexity, but it still must be scheduled, not left open-ended. Downstream effect: the confirmed review date becomes the anchor for the first complication surveillance cycle and for warranty-preserving documentation.

For the first 30 days after return, use the First-30-Days Bite, Provisional, and Soft-Tissue Issue Capture Form every day for the first 7 days, then every 3 days through day 30, and immediately after any new symptom appears. Track pain trend, swelling trend, bite comfort, chewing ability, bleeding, bad taste or odor, screw or prosthesis mobility, speech changes, tissue irritation, medication use, and food tolerance. The point is not subjective journaling; it is to create a usable symptom log that can be routed either to Cavallini or the U.S. provider with dates, severity, and progression.

Decision threshold: if pain, swelling, looseness, bleeding, or bite interference worsens over a 24-hour period instead of stabilizing or improving, escalate the same day to the treating channel specified in the discharge instructions and document the contact. If the U.S. provider is unavailable within the needed timeframe, route simultaneously to Cavallini and identify an alternate local provider from the provider inventory.

After initial stabilization, build the Long-Term Cleaning, Imaging, and Prosthetic Review Schedule for Costa Rica Restorative Work. This schedule must include hygiene visits, surveillance imaging, restorative checks, and any brand-specific or warranty-specific maintenance expectations actually stated by Cavallini. If no written maintenance interval is available, mark the field REQUIRES CLARIFICATION and obtain it rather than assuming that the lifetime warranty means indefinite passive coverage.

## Deep Operational Guidance

The key operational principle is that post-return continuity depends on document quality as much as clinical quality. A U.S. dentist may be entirely competent but still decline meaningful intervention if they do not know whether the case uses Nobel Biocare or Zimmer implants, what prosthetic configuration was delivered, whether the prosthesis is provisional or final, what screws or components may be involved, and what was done surgically in Costa Rica. The handoff packet therefore functions as a translation layer between the Cavallini treatment environment and the U.S. follow-up environment.

For implant and All-on-X cases, implant identity data is especially important. The U.S. provider needs enough information to understand platform, component compatibility, timing of placement, immediate-load status if applicable, and whether current symptoms may relate to soft tissue, occlusion, prosthetic fit, or hardware stability. If these details are absent, even simple interventions such as evaluating mobility or ordering component-specific parts can be delayed, and the patient may be forced into emergency diagnostics that duplicate work already performed abroad.

Decision threshold: if the handoff packet does not identify the implant brand and procedure date for each implant-related phase, classify the packet as incomplete and do not close the return-to-U.S. handoff task. Downstream effect: open a records recovery action with Cavallini, copy the U.S. provider if needed, and suspend any assumption that warranty support or local intervention can proceed smoothly.

The 30-day surveillance window exists because many destabilizing problems emerge after the patient is no longer under daily observation. A high spot in the bite may not be obvious on day 1 when soft foods and medication blunt function; it may become clear on day 5 when swelling decreases and chewing increases. A provisional bridge may feel acceptable initially but start rocking after repeated function. Tissue ulceration may begin as localized irritation and then progress into eating difficulty, sleep disruption, or noncompliance with hygiene instructions.

This is why symptom capture should focus on trend, not isolated discomfort. The patient should compare each entry against the prior entry and classify the condition as improving, unchanged, or worsening. That distinction determines routing: improving symptoms usually continue under routine monitoring; unchanged symptoms beyond the expected recovery window require provider review; worsening symptoms trigger same-day contact and possible urgent evaluation.

If the patient reports a bite that prevents even bilateral contact, creates repeated clicking on one side, or makes normal soft-food chewing impossible for more than 48 hours after return, then schedule an occlusal review with the U.S. provider or Cavallini-directed local provider at the earliest available appointment. Downstream effect: unresolved occlusal interference can overload provisionals, compromise comfort, and create avoidable fractures or screw loosening. In full-arch work, even a small misload can cascade into repeated instability.

Warranty preservation is not passive. A warranty only functions if the patient can show what was delivered, what maintenance was performed, what instructions were followed, and when the clinic was notified of a problem. The Cavallini Lifetime Warranty Compliance and Claim-Evidence Register should therefore log every relevant event: treatment date, return date, U.S. provider visit date, hygiene visit date, imaging date, reported issue, photo evidence captured, clinic notification date, response received, and any recommendation given.

A frequent failure is assuming that a verbal conversation with the clinic preserves rights. It does not create a reliable claim file. If the patient calls about a loose provisional on day 11 and receives informal advice but does not send photographs, does not log the conversation, and does not keep the date, there may be no usable evidence later if the issue worsens. Every material communication with Cavallini or a U.S. provider should therefore produce an entry in the register and, where possible, an email confirmation or saved message thread.

Decision threshold: if a complication affects function, appearance, comfort, hardware stability, or tissue health and is not documented with date, symptom description, and provider notification, treat the warranty file as noncompliant until corrected. Downstream effect: gather retrospective documentation immediately, including photos, visit summaries, and communication records, before memory and evidence degrade.

Long-term maintenance must be matched to treatment type. A single crown case may require straightforward surveillance and routine hygiene, while an implant-supported bridge or All-on-X prosthesis demands recurring evaluation of soft tissue, prosthetic integrity, screw stability, bite wear, and radiographic changes over time. The system does not assume that the U.S. provider will create this schedule for you; it requires the patient to hold the schedule as a controlled calendar with due dates, completion dates, and missed-visit escalation.

If a scheduled hygiene or surveillance review is missed by more than 30 days, then open a maintenance deviation entry in the warranty register and reschedule immediately. Downstream effect: missed maintenance may not only increase biological and prosthetic risk; it may also weaken any later argument that the patient complied with warranty conditions. The maintenance record should be clean enough that a third party can reconstruct the care history without relying on memory.

The U.S. provider relationship also needs role clarity. Some offices will agree to emergency triage only, not long-term maintenance of work placed elsewhere. Others will maintain implants but not adjust a complex full-arch prosthesis without consultation from the original clinic. The provider inventory must therefore record exactly what the office accepted: emergency assessment only, hygiene and routine surveillance, minor adjustment authority, or broad restorative follow-up. Ambiguity here creates treatment delay when symptoms appear.

Decision threshold: if the chosen U.S. provider will not perform the level of support required by the case complexity, keep that provider classified as partial coverage only and continue building the inventory. For an All-on-X or multi-implant case, a provider willing only to take X-rays in an emergency is not a full continuity solution. Downstream effect: maintain at least one backup office or specialist pathway for scenarios involving prosthetic loosening, component issues, or surgical concern.

## Failure Dynamics and Recovery

Failure scenario 1: the patient returns home after implant-supported provisional delivery and waits until discomfort becomes severe before contacting anyone. On day 6, they notice that the bite feels heavy on the right side. By day 10, chewing is limited and the provisional feels unstable, but no symptom log exists and no U.S. dentist was prearranged. The earliest available local consult is 12 days away, and the office wants implant records before touching the case.

Operational consequence: what might have been a simple early occlusal adjustment becomes a prolonged function problem, with possible provisional damage and avoidable suffering. Financial consequence: the patient may pay for emergency imaging, a private consultation, and possible temporary repairs entirely out of pocket because the case was not routed quickly and warranty evidence is weak. Recovery action: activate the provider inventory, send Cavallini the symptom chronology with photos the same day, request component and prosthetic details, and secure the first available local evaluation rather than waiting for a preferred office.

Failure scenario 2: the patient has All-on-X treatment using a named implant system but leaves Costa Rica without a complete discharge packet. Three weeks later, a local dentist identifies a possible loose component but asks for implant brand, prosthetic type, and original treatment notes. The patient only has a payment receipt and a few cellphone photos. The office cannot confirm compatibility or intervention scope, and the patient loses 3 to 5 business days waiting for records retrieval across borders.

Operational consequence: a mechanical issue remains unresolved while the patient continues to function on the prosthesis, increasing risk of additional wear or damage. Financial consequence: a simple tightening or adjustment may turn into a broader prosthetic repair if instability continues, and some U.S. offices will charge premium rates for undocumented foreign cases. Recovery action: classify the case as packet failure, request complete records from Cavallini immediately, pause nonessential chewing stress, document all symptoms daily, and ask the U.S. provider what minimum information is required to proceed with safe triage.

Failure scenario 3: the patient assumes the Cavallini lifetime warranty covers any future problem automatically and does not attend scheduled maintenance. They skip hygiene and review appointments for 11 months, then develop tissue inflammation and prosthetic complaints. When contacting the clinic, they cannot show maintenance records, dates of local follow-up, or timely notice of symptom onset.

Operational consequence: the distinction between a warrantable product issue and a maintenance-related deterioration becomes harder to prove. Financial consequence: the patient may need to fund diagnostics, cleaning, adjustments, or even travel back to Costa Rica without assurance of reimbursement or covered remake. Recovery action: reconstruct the maintenance history as far as possible, obtain current U.S. provider findings, send a complete chronology to Cavallini, and accept that some warranty leverage may already be reduced by documentation gaps.

Failure scenario 4: the patient experiences persistent swelling, bad taste, and increasing tenderness 4 days after returning, but assumes this is normal recovery. No same-day escalation occurs. By day 7, they seek urgent care through a general medical setting unfamiliar with recent dental implant work, and treatment is fragmented. Delay here can create higher clinical risk than the original symptom burden justified.

Decision threshold: if swelling increases after an initial decline, if foul taste or drainage appears, or if pain becomes progressively worse rather than better over a 24-hour period, treat it as an urgent escalation event. Downstream effect: contact Cavallini and a dental-capable U.S. provider the same day, not a week later, and preserve photographs, temperature readings if relevant, medication use, and functional limitations in the issue capture form.

Recovery discipline matters more than perfection. A failed handoff can often be repaired if the patient acts quickly, centralizes documentation, and re-establishes one lead coordinator. In most cases, that coordinator should be the patient using the worksheets in this domain, because the system spans an international clinic, local offices, and warranty evidence. The objective is not to create elegance after a disruption; it is to re-establish traceability, response ownership, and next-step deadlines.

## Cross-Domain Handoffs

This chapter receives its inputs from Onsite Treatment Execution, Sedation Day Control, and Clinical Handoff Management, and from Costa Rica Recovery Monitoring and Return-to-Travel Clearance. The quality of those upstream domains determines how much friction appears here. If the discharge packet is incomplete, if procedure details are vague, or if return-to-travel clearance occurred without documentation duplication, then this domain begins in deficit and must spend time recovering missing information before it can protect continuity or warranty status.

Its first formal handoff is from Costa Rica to the U.S. provider ecosystem. That handoff is complete only when the provider has received the packet, acknowledged readability, and either scheduled or declined the first review. Silence from a U.S. office is not successful transfer. Decision threshold: if no acknowledgement is received within 2 business days of transmission, follow up by phone and resend the packet; if still unresolved, move to the next office in the provider inventory.

Its second handoff is from acute return-home monitoring into long-term maintenance control. That transition occurs after the first 30 days are complete without unmanaged complication, all documented issues have a disposition, and the patient has a calendar for cleaning, imaging, and restorative review. If issues remain open at day 30, the chapter stays in active stabilization mode rather than moving to routine maintenance mode.

Its third handoff is into warranty preservation. Every completed maintenance visit, symptom report, local evaluation, or Cavallini communication should feed the warranty register. That register is not a passive archive; it is the operating evidence file that supports future claim submission, dispute resolution, or cross-border coordination. If an event matters enough to influence care, cost, or coverage, it belongs in the register.

Closure criteria are strict. This chapter is operationally complete only when all five conditions are true: one, a named U.S. follow-up provider has agreed to a defined role and has received the treatment packet; two, the packet includes implant and prosthetic records sufficient for local triage or maintenance, or missing items are flagged and actively being recovered; three, the first 30 days after return have been monitored through a documented issue capture cycle with all escalations logged; four, the Cavallini warranty compliance register contains treatment dates, maintenance obligations, communication records, and evidence storage locations; and five, the long-term cleaning, imaging, and prosthetic review schedule is built with actual dates or date ranges tied to the completed restorative work.

If any one of those five conditions is not met, the case is not operationally closed. It remains in active control because the treatment is still vulnerable to unmanaged complications, documentation loss, or avoidable warranty failure. This is the final domain in the Cavallini Costa Rica Restorative Dental Travel Control System, but it is also the domain that determines whether the value of the entire treatment pathway is preserved after the patient comes home.

decision_gates.condition: All capabilities that are critical to the probable treatment scope—such as implant brand availability, sedation pathway if likely needed, lab support, and lodging/recovery support—are marked Documented or have equivalent written confirmation from a named clinic representative.
decision_gates.pass_action: Allow ws-01-b and ws-01-c findings to be integrated into an initial Cavallini viability review.

Marking a capability 'verified' based on a phone call with no named staff member, no written follow-up, and no saved timestamped source.
